clean: id bigint GENERATED ALWAYS AS IDENTITY PRIMARY KEY

terms.title: Isolation level matches the anomaly the code must not permit

signals: correctness relies on a read staying stable or an invariant holding across concurrent writers
an explicit or defaulted isolation level for a multi-step transaction

terms.smell: A transaction's correctness assumes more isolation than its level gives — code that assumes "the row I read is the row I write" running under READ COMMITTED, or a no-write-skew invariant under REPEATABLE READ.
terms.why: Most apps run READ COMMITTED (Postgres default) or REPEATABLE READ (MySQL default) without realizing it. Under READ COMMITTED two reads in one txn can differ (non-repeatable read) and lost updates are possible; under REPEATABLE READ you still get write skew. The "at least one doctor on call" class of invariant silently breaks.
terms.fix: Match the level to the anomaly you must exclude: SERIALIZABLE for no-write-skew invariants (plus retry on serialization failure), or explicit locking. Don't rely on a guarantee the level doesn't provide.

trips: -- READ COMMITTED txn assumes the balance it read is still current at write
clean: SET TRANSACTION ISOLATION LEVEL SERIALIZABLE  -- with retry on 40001

terms.title: JSON columns are for sparse data, not queried/constrained fields

signals: a jsonb column the app repeatedly extracts and filters on (data->>'status')

terms.smell: A `jsonb`/`json` column holds fields the app constantly extracts and filters on — `data->>'status'` in `WHERE` clauses — instead of real typed columns.
terms.why: You lose type checking, `NOT NULL`/`CHECK`, and cheap indexing; every filter becomes a JSON extraction, and a typo'd key fails silently returning nothing. The schema stops describing the data.
terms.fix: Promote frequently-queried, schema-stable fields to real typed columns with constraints and indexes. Keep JSON only for genuinely sparse or variable data.

trips: WHERE data->>'status' = 'active'  -- status is always present, always filtered
clean: status text NOT NULL CHECK (status IN (...))  -- promoted out of jsonb

terms.title: Mutable business tables carry created_at / updated_at

signals: a new mutable business table with no created_at/updated_at

terms.smell: A new mutable business table has no `created_at` / `updated_at` audit timestamps.
terms.why: When something looks wrong you have no "when did this happen / when did it last change" — debugging, chronological ordering, and incremental syncs all depend on these columns.
terms.fix: Add `created_at timestamptz NOT NULL DEFAULT now()` and `updated_at timestamptz NOT NULL DEFAULT now()`, keeping `updated_at` current via a trigger or the ORM.

trips: CREATE TABLE orders (id bigint PRIMARY KEY, total numeric)  -- no timestamps
clean: created_at timestamptz NOT NULL DEFAULT now(), updated_at timestamptz NOT NULL DEFAULT now()

terms.title: CHECK constraints enforce domain rules in the DB

signals: a column with an obvious domain rule (age >= 0, quantity > 0, end >= start) and no CHECK

terms.smell: A column with a clear domain rule — `age`, `quantity`, `end_date` — has no `CHECK`, leaving negative quantities or end-before-start rows possible.
terms.why: A buggy write path inserts a negative quantity or an end date before the start date, and it corrupts every calculation built on that row. Nothing fails loudly; the bad data just sits there poisoning reports.
terms.fix: Add the guard in the DB: `CHECK (age >= 0)`, `CHECK (quantity > 0)`, `CHECK (end_date >= start_date)`. The bad write then fails at the source.

example.before: quantity int NOT NULL
example.after: quantity int NOT NULL CHECK (quantity > 0)

trips: end_date date, start_date date  -- no ordering check

terms.title: DB default on columns the app is expected to always set

signals: a NOT NULL column (created_at, status) with no DEFAULT, relying on every insert path

terms.smell: A `NOT NULL` column relies on every insert path to supply the value and has no DB `DEFAULT` — e.g. `created_at timestamptz NOT NULL` with nothing behind it.
terms.why: One insert path forgets it and the insert fails outright, or a different writer (a job, an admin script, a backfill) inserts a wrong value. A DB default makes the invariant hold regardless of which writer is involved.
terms.fix: Add a sensible `DEFAULT` at the column: `created_at timestamptz NOT NULL DEFAULT now()`.

clean: created_at timestamptz NOT NULL DEFAULT now()

terms.title: Foreign key declared for every reference (orphan prevention)

signals: a *_id reference column with no FOREIGN KEY constraint
parent existence enforced only in application code

terms.smell: A reference column (`order_items.order_id`) points at another table's key with no `FOREIGN KEY` constraint — parent existence is "guaranteed by the app".
terms.why: Orphaned rows accumulate — items pointing at deleted or nonexistent orders. Joins silently drop them, counts disagree, and "where did this row's parent go?" becomes an investigation. Concurrent delete/insert races produce orphans the app-only check can't prevent.
terms.fix: Declare the FK: `FOREIGN KEY (order_id) REFERENCES orders(id)`. On a large table add it `NOT VALID` then `VALIDATE`. Remember a declared FK is NOT auto-indexed in Postgres — index the FK column too.

example.before: order_id bigint NOT NULL
example.after: order_id bigint NOT NULL REFERENCES orders(id)

trips: order_id bigint NOT NULL  -- no FK
clean: order_id bigint NOT NULL REFERENCES orders(id)

terms.title: Index exists for each WHERE / JOIN / ORDER BY column

signals: a new query filtering/joining/sorting on a column with no supporting index

terms.smell: A query filters, joins, or sorts on a column with no index covering it — e.g. `SELECT * FROM events WHERE user_id = $1 ORDER BY created_at DESC` with nothing on `(user_id, created_at)`.
terms.why: A sequential scan reads every row and an explicit sort spills to disk. It's linear in table size — invisible at 10k rows, a multi-second query at 10M. The regression appears only in production.
terms.fix: Add the index the predicate needs, e.g. `CREATE INDEX ON events (user_id, created_at DESC);`. Confirm with `EXPLAIN` that the plan uses it.

example.before: SELECT * FROM events WHERE user_id = $1 ORDER BY created_at DESC  -- no index
example.after: CREATE INDEX ON events (user_id, created_at DESC);

trips: SELECT * FROM events WHERE user_id = $1  -- no index on user_id
clean: CREATE INDEX ON events (user_id, created_at DESC)

terms.title: NOT NULL on columns where null is meaningless

signals: a required column added without NOT NULL

terms.smell: A column that must always have a value (e.g. `email` on users) is declared nullable.
terms.why: `NULL` leaks in, and three-valued logic bites: both `WHERE email = $1` and `WHERE email <> $1` exclude the null rows, and `COUNT(email)` undercounts. Every consumer must now handle a state that should never exist.
terms.fix: Add `NOT NULL`. On a large live table, stage it (add nullable → backfill → `CHECK NOT VALID` → `VALIDATE` → `SET NOT NULL`) to avoid a locking full-table scan.

example.before: email text
example.after: email text NOT NULL

trips: email text  -- every user must have one

terms.title: UNIQUE constraint on every logically-unique column

signals: a column that should be unique (email, slug, external_id) with uniqueness checked only in app code

terms.smell: A logically-unique column (`email`, `slug`, `external_id`) has uniqueness "checked in the app" but no `UNIQUE` constraint or unique index in the DB.
terms.why: Two concurrent signups both pass the app-level check-then-insert and you get duplicate accounts — a classic race the application literally cannot close, because there is a gap between its `SELECT` and its `INSERT`. Only the DB constraint arbitrates atomically.
terms.fix: Add `UNIQUE (email)` (or a unique index, partial if soft-deleting: `WHERE deleted_at IS NULL`) and handle the conflict with `ON CONFLICT` / `ON DUPLICATE KEY`.

example.before: email text NOT NULL  -- uniqueness enforced in app
example.after: email text NOT NULL, UNIQUE (email)

trips: email text NOT NULL  -- app checks for dupes

terms.title: Timestamps are timestamptz (UTC), not naive timestamp

signals: a timestamp column declared `timestamp` without time zone
a created_at/updated_at/expires_at column type choice

terms.smell: A datetime column is declared `timestamp` (Postgres `timestamp without time zone`) rather than `timestamptz`.
terms.why: A naive timestamp carries no zone, so the same stored value means different instants depending on who reads it. Across a DST change or a multi-region deploy, event ordering and "is this token expired?" break — an off-by-an-hour class of bug that is invisible in a single-zone test.
terms.fix: Use `timestamptz` (stored as UTC) and set app/session time zone to UTC. In MySQL, `TIMESTAMP` is zone-aware but capped at 2038; use `DATETIME` only with an explicit UTC convention.

example.before: created_at timestamp NOT NULL DEFAULT now()
example.after: created_at timestamptz NOT NULL DEFAULT now()

clean: created_at timestamptz NOT NULL DEFAULT now()

terms.title: Primary key is a stable surrogate, not a mutable natural key

signals: PRIMARY KEY on email, ssn, or a composite of mutable business attributes

terms.smell: The primary key is a natural key that can change — `PK = email`, `ssn`, or `(country, tax_id)`.
terms.why: Natural keys change (people change email), and a PK change cascades to every FK referencing it — a massive, lock-heavy update across many tables. Identity should not be tied to a mutable attribute.
terms.fix: Use a stable surrogate PK (`bigint generated always as identity` or `uuid`) and put a `UNIQUE` constraint on the natural key.

example.before: PRIMARY KEY (email)
example.after: id bigint GENERATED ALWAYS AS IDENTITY PRIMARY KEY, email text NOT NULL UNIQUE

clean: id bigint GENERATED ALWAYS AS IDENTITY PRIMARY KEY, email text UNIQUE

terms.title: Irreversible/destructive migrations flagged with a recovery plan

signals: a destructive migration with an empty or NotImplementedError down()

terms.smell: A destructive migration (drop column, destructive transform) has an empty or `raise NotImplementedError` `down()` and no stated recovery plan.
terms.why: If the release goes wrong you can't roll the schema back, and a rollback of the *code* now hits a schema it doesn't expect. Data loss with no backup path is discovered during the incident, not before it.
terms.fix: Provide a real reverse where feasible. Where truly irreversible, say so explicitly, ensure a backup/snapshot exists, and gate the change behind review so the reviewer knows.

trips: def down(): raise NotImplementedError  -- after a DROP COLUMN
clean: -- irreversible: noted in PR, snapshot taken, reviewer sign-off

terms.title: Every foreign key column is indexed (the Postgres FK trap)

signals: a FOREIGN KEY declared with no index on the referencing column
a *_id column used in JOIN/WHERE with no supporting index

terms.smell: A foreign key column (`order_items.user_id REFERENCES users(id)`) has no index on the referencing side.
terms.why: Two-fold pain. (1) `WHERE user_id = $1` and `JOIN ... ON user_id` do a full scan. (2) In Postgres, deleting or updating a `users` row must scan the child table for referencing rows — a seq scan per parent delete that can take a lock and block writes. Postgres does NOT auto-create this index (MySQL/InnoDB does); it is one of the most common Postgres findings.
terms.fix: `CREATE INDEX ON order_items (user_id);` — on a live table use `CONCURRENTLY`.

example.before: FOREIGN KEY (user_id) REFERENCES users(id)  -- no index on user_id
example.after:
FOREIGN KEY (user_id) REFERENCES users(id);
CREATE INDEX ON order_items (user_id);

trips: FOREIGN KEY (user_id) REFERENCES users(id)  -- no index

terms.title: lock_timeout / statement_timeout guards on risky DDL

signals: a risky ALTER/DDL migration with no lock_timeout / statement_timeout set

terms.smell: A risky DDL migration sets no `lock_timeout` / `statement_timeout`, so it can block indefinitely waiting for its lock.
terms.why: Without `lock_timeout` the `ALTER` waits behind a long-running transaction for its lock, and every new query then waits behind the `ALTER` — a lock pile-up that takes the table (or the whole app) down.
terms.fix: `SET lock_timeout = '3s'; SET statement_timeout = '...';` before risky DDL so it fails fast instead of stalling traffic; retry off-peak.

example.before: ALTER TABLE big ADD COLUMN ...;  -- no timeout guard
example.after:
SET lock_timeout = '3s';
ALTER TABLE big ADD COLUMN ...;

trips: ALTER TABLE big ADD COLUMN x int  -- no lock_timeout
clean: SET lock_timeout = '3s'; ALTER TABLE big ADD COLUMN x int

terms.title: No function wrapping the indexed column in WHERE

signals: WHERE lower(col) = / date(col) = / EXTRACT(... FROM col) on an indexed column

terms.smell: A predicate wraps the indexed column in a function — `WHERE lower(email) = $1`, `WHERE date(created_at) = $1`, `WHERE EXTRACT(year FROM d) = 2024`.
terms.why: The index is on `email`, not `lower(email)`, so the planner can't use it — full scan. Fine at 10k rows, a sequential scan on 10M.
terms.fix: Add a matching functional index (`CREATE INDEX ON users (lower(email))`) or rewrite to a sargable range: `WHERE created_at >= $1 AND created_at < $1 + interval '1 day'`.

example.before: WHERE lower(email) = $1  -- index is on email
example.after: CREATE INDEX ON users (lower(email));  -- now WHERE lower(email) = $1 seeks

clean: WHERE created_at >= $1 AND created_at < $1 + interval '1 day'

terms.title: Predicate and join types match — no implicit cast on the column

signals: comparing a varchar column to a numeric literal, or joining int to bigint to text
an ORM passing a number for a string column

terms.smell: A predicate mixes types so the engine must cast the column — `WHERE phone = 123456` where `phone` is `varchar`, `WHERE id = '42'`, or joining `int` to `bigint` to `text`.
terms.why: The engine casts the column (not the literal) to compare, which is `func(col)` in disguise — the index is bypassed and you get a scan. Common right after an ORM passes a number for a string column.
terms.fix: Match types exactly: compare `varchar` to a string literal, align column types across a join.

example.before: WHERE phone = 123456  -- phone is varchar
example.after: WHERE phone = '123456'

trips: JOIN orders o ON o.user_id = u.id  -- int vs bigint

terms.title: No leading-wildcard LIKE on a plain B-tree index

signals: WHERE col LIKE '%x' or LIKE '%x%'

terms.smell: A `LIKE` predicate starts with a wildcard — `WHERE name LIKE '%smith'` or `'%smith%'`.
terms.why: A leading `%` means the engine can't know where in the B-tree to start, so it scans every row. Only an anchored prefix is index-usable on a plain B-tree.
terms.fix: Use an anchored prefix (`LIKE 'smith%'`) where possible; for contains/fuzzy search use a trigram index (`pg_trgm` GIN) or full-text search.

example.before: WHERE name LIKE '%smith%'
example.after: CREATE INDEX ON users USING gin (name gin_trgm_ops);  -- then LIKE '%smith%' can use it

clean: -- trigram GIN index for contains search

terms.title: OR across columns doesn't leave one side unindexed

signals: WHERE a = $1 OR b = $2 where only one of a, b is indexed

terms.smell: A predicate ORs across different columns where only one is indexed — `WHERE a = $1 OR b = $2` with no index on `b`.
terms.why: A single missing side forces the whole predicate to a full scan, because the engine must check every row for the unindexed branch — the indexed side buys nothing.
terms.fix: Index both columns and let the planner bitmap-OR them, or rewrite as a `UNION` of two indexed lookups.

example.before: WHERE email = $1 OR phone = $2  -- only email indexed
example.after: SELECT ... WHERE email = $1 UNION SELECT ... WHERE phone = $2  -- both indexed

trips: WHERE email = $1 OR phone = $2  -- phone not indexed
clean: WHERE email = $1 UNION ... WHERE phone = $2  -- both indexed

terms.title: NULL is not overloaded to mean zero or "none"

signals: a numeric column where NULL is documented/implied to mean 0 or default

terms.smell: A column uses `NULL` as a stand-in for a real value — `discount numeric` where `NULL` secretly means "0% discount".
terms.why: Aggregations (`SUM`, `AVG`) treat `NULL` as absent, not zero, so totals come out wrong, and readers split into two camps about what `NULL` means. The ambiguity corrupts every rollup.
terms.fix: Give the column a real default: `discount numeric NOT NULL DEFAULT 0`. Reserve `NULL` only for genuinely-unknown, and make that meaning explicit.

example.before: discount numeric  -- null means 0%
example.after: discount numeric NOT NULL DEFAULT 0

trips: discount numeric  -- null means no discount

terms.title: Concurrent user edits guarded by an optimistic version column

signals: a user-facing edit that loads a record, mutates fields, and saves the whole row back

terms.smell: A low-contention user-facing edit loads a record, mutates fields, and writes the whole row back with no concurrency guard — last write wins.
terms.why: Two people editing the same record silently clobber each other; the second save quietly discards the first person's changes with no conflict surfaced.
terms.fix: Add `version int NOT NULL DEFAULT 0`. Update with `... SET ..., version = version + 1 WHERE id = $1 AND version = $read_version;` and check the affected-row count — `0` means someone else won; reload and retry. Many ORMs support it (JPA `@Version`, SQLAlchemy `version_id_col`, ActiveRecord `lock_version`).

trips: UPDATE docs SET body = $1 WHERE id = $2  -- no version check, concurrent editors
clean: UPDATE docs SET body=$1, version=version+1 WHERE id=$2 AND version=$read  -- check rowcount

terms.title: ORDER BY can walk an index instead of a full sort

signals: ORDER BY on a column with no matching index, or index direction mismatched

terms.smell: A query's `ORDER BY` has no usable index — no index at all, or the index direction/column order doesn't match (`ORDER BY created_at DESC` with `WHERE a = $1` but only `(created_at)`).
terms.why: The planner does a full in-memory or on-disk sort of the matched rows instead of walking the index in order — slow and memory-hungry on large result sets, and it can spill to disk (`Sort Method: external merge Disk`).
terms.fix: Match the index to the sort: for `WHERE a = $1 ORDER BY b` build `(a, b)`. Postgres can read a single-column index backward, so direction alone rarely needs a separate index.

trips: WHERE tenant_id = $1 ORDER BY created_at DESC  -- index only on (created_at)
clean: CREATE INDEX ON t (tenant_id, created_at DESC)

terms.title: ORM-generated DDL is read; no synchronize/AutoMigrate against prod

signals: a Prisma/Alembic/Rails/Django generated migration merged without reading the SQL
synchronize: true (TypeORM) or AutoMigrate (GORM) against production

terms.smell: ORM-generated DDL is merged unread, or `synchronize: true` (TypeORM) / `AutoMigrate` (GORM) runs against production.
terms.why: The generated SQL is a draft: it may emit a plain (non-`CONCURRENTLY`) `CREATE INDEX`, a column add that rewrites, or an immediate FK/NOT NULL validation. `synchronize`/`AutoMigrate` diff and apply whatever they want — including drops — with no safety review, risking a lock or data loss.
terms.fix: Open the generated SQL and check for table rewrites, non-concurrent indexes, immediate FK/NOT NULL validation, and unbatched backfills before it touches a large table. Rails: `disable_ddl_transaction!` + `algorithm: :concurrently`. Django: mark long backfills `atomic = False`. Never `synchronize`/`AutoMigrate` in prod.

trips: synchronize: true  // TypeORM data source, production
clean: -- generated SQL reviewed: CREATE INDEX CONCURRENTLY, no rewrite, FK NOT VALID

terms.title: Not over-indexed — every index taxes every write

signals: an index added for every column just in case, or many indexes on a write-heavy table

terms.smell: Indexes added "just in case" for every column, or one per query on a write-heavy table.
terms.why: Every `INSERT`/`UPDATE`/`DELETE` must update every index — more indexes mean slower writes, more bloat, bigger storage, and more plans for the planner to get wrong. Indexes are a write tax paid for read speed, not free.
terms.fix: Index for real access patterns only; drop unused indexes (Postgres: `pg_stat_user_indexes.idx_scan = 0`).

trips: CREATE INDEX on every column of a write-heavy table just in case
clean: -- indexes only for observed WHERE/JOIN/ORDER BY patterns; unused ones dropped

terms.title: Partial index for status-queue and soft-delete access patterns

signals: an index over a full column where queries only ever hit a small subset (status='pending', deleted_at IS NULL)

terms.smell: A full-column index backs a query that only ever touches a small subset — the work queue (`status = 'pending'`) or live rows (`deleted_at IS NULL`).
terms.why: The full index is larger, slower to scan, and more expensive to maintain than one restricted to the rows actually queried.
terms.fix: Use a partial index: `CREATE INDEX ON jobs (created_at) WHERE status = 'pending';` — smaller, faster, cheaper, and ideal for soft-delete and status-queue patterns.

trips: CREATE INDEX ON jobs (created_at)  -- only pending rows ever queried
clean: CREATE INDEX ON jobs (created_at) WHERE status = 'pending'

terms.title: Flag obvious partitioning candidates early (don't force)

signals: a new time-series/event/log table expected to reach hundreds of millions of rows
queries almost always bounded by time or tenant

terms.smell: A time-series/event/log table that will reach hundreds of millions of rows, where queries are almost always bounded by time or tenant, is created as one flat table.
terms.why: Without partitioning, deleting old data is an expensive `DELETE` that bloats the table, `VACUUM` struggles, and every query scans the whole history. This is a raise-early flag, not a demand — partitioning is a real cost.
terms.fix: Consider range partitioning by time (or tenant) so old partitions drop cheaply and queries hit one partition. Only for genuinely large, bounded-access tables — not a 100k-row table.

trips: CREATE TABLE events (...)  -- billions of rows, always queried by day
clean: CREATE TABLE events (...) PARTITION BY RANGE (created_at)

terms.title: Hot-insert primary key is monotonic, not random UUIDv4

signals: id uuid DEFAULT gen_random_uuid() as PK on a high-insert table

terms.smell: A high-insert table uses a random UUID v4 (`id uuid DEFAULT gen_random_uuid()`) as its primary/clustering key.
terms.why: Random UUIDs scatter inserts across the B-tree, causing page splits, index fragmentation, and poor cache locality — measurably slower inserts and a bigger index than a monotonic key. In MySQL/InnoDB the PK is the clustering key, so a random PK is especially costly.
terms.fix: Prefer `bigint identity`; if you need a UUID, use a time-ordered UUID v7 / ULID so inserts append instead of scatter.

example.before: id uuid PRIMARY KEY DEFAULT gen_random_uuid()
example.after: id bigint GENERATED ALWAYS AS IDENTITY PRIMARY KEY  -- or uuidv7

trips: id uuid PRIMARY KEY DEFAULT gen_random_uuid()  -- hot insert path
clean: id bigint GENERATED ALWAYS AS IDENTITY PRIMARY KEY
id uuid PRIMARY KEY DEFAULT uuidv7()

terms.title: SET NOT NULL staged via CHECK NOT VALID → VALIDATE, not direct

signals: ALTER COLUMN ... SET NOT NULL directly on a large populated table

terms.smell: A migration runs `ALTER TABLE big ALTER COLUMN col SET NOT NULL;` directly on a large populated table.
terms.why: A direct `SET NOT NULL` scans the whole table to verify, holding `ACCESS EXCLUSIVE` the entire time → the table is blocked for reads and writes for the duration of the scan.
terms.fix: Postgres 12+: add `CHECK (col IS NOT NULL) NOT VALID`, then `VALIDATE CONSTRAINT` (weak `SHARE UPDATE EXCLUSIVE` lock, allows writes), then `SET NOT NULL` — cheap because the validated constraint proves it.

example.before: ALTER TABLE big ALTER COLUMN col SET NOT NULL;

trips: ALTER TABLE big ALTER COLUMN col SET NOT NULL  -- direct, large table
clean: ADD CONSTRAINT c CHECK (col IS NOT NULL) NOT VALID; VALIDATE CONSTRAINT c; SET NOT NULL

terms.title: Job-queue claims use FOR UPDATE SKIP LOCKED

signals: multiple workers selecting the next pending job then updating it

terms.smell: Many workers run `SELECT * FROM jobs WHERE status='pending' LIMIT 1` then update, with no `SKIP LOCKED`.
terms.why: Without locking they all grab the same job → duplicate processing. With plain `FOR UPDATE` they serialize behind one lock → a thundering herd queued single-file. Either way the queue misbehaves under concurrency.
terms.fix: `SELECT ... FOR UPDATE SKIP LOCKED LIMIT 1` — each worker claims a different unlocked row. The idiomatic contention-free job claim (Postgres 9.5+, MySQL 8.0+).

example.before: SELECT id FROM jobs WHERE status='pending' LIMIT 1;  -- then UPDATE
example.after: SELECT id FROM jobs WHERE status='pending' LIMIT 1 FOR UPDATE SKIP LOCKED;

trips: SELECT id FROM jobs WHERE status='pending' LIMIT 1  -- concurrent workers, no SKIP LOCKED
clean: SELECT id FROM jobs WHERE status='pending' LIMIT 1 FOR UPDATE SKIP LOCKED

terms.title: Soft-delete intent explicit; live-row queries and uniqueness scoped

signals: a deleted_at column added but queries/unique constraints don't scope to live rows

terms.smell: A `deleted_at` soft-delete column exists but some queries forget to filter on it, and unique constraints ignore it.
terms.why: A forgotten `WHERE deleted_at IS NULL` resurrects deleted data into the UI; a unique constraint that ignores `deleted_at` blocks re-creating a record with the same key after it was "deleted".
terms.fix: Decide deliberately. If soft-deleting, make live rows the easy path: a partial unique index `WHERE deleted_at IS NULL`, a `live_rows` view, and unique constraints scoped to non-deleted rows.

example.before: UNIQUE (email)  -- collides with soft-deleted rows
example.after: CREATE UNIQUE INDEX ON users (email) WHERE deleted_at IS NULL

trips: UNIQUE (email)  -- table has deleted_at, constraint ignores it
clean: CREATE UNIQUE INDEX ON users (email) WHERE deleted_at IS NULL

terms.title: No external I/O or waits inside a transaction

signals: an HTTP call / email send / user-input wait between BEGIN and COMMIT
a giant batch wrapped in a single transaction

terms.smell: A transaction stays open across external work — an HTTP call, an email send, a wait on user input — or wraps a giant batch in one txn.
terms.why: Held locks block other writers; in Postgres a long-open transaction also pins old row versions so `VACUUM` can't reclaim them → table bloat and degraded performance. "Idle in transaction" connections are a top cause of mysterious lock waits.
terms.fix: Keep transactions to the database work only; do external I/O before or after, not inside. Set `idle_in_transaction_session_timeout` (Postgres) as a backstop.

example.before: await tx.begin(); await http.post(url); await tx.query(...); await tx.commit();
example.after: await http.post(url); await tx.begin(); await tx.query(...); await tx.commit();

terms.title: Writes that must be atomic live in one transaction

signals: two related writes (debit/credit, order + line items) committed separately

terms.smell: Two writes that must succeed-or-fail together — debit one account, credit another — are committed in separate transactions.
terms.why: A crash between the two commits leaves the system half-applied: money debited but not credited. Atomicity is the whole point of the transaction; splitting it discards the guarantee.
terms.fix: Wrap operations that must be atomic in one transaction so they commit or roll back together.

example.before:
UPDATE a SET bal = bal - 100; COMMIT;
UPDATE b SET bal = bal + 100; COMMIT;

trips: await debit(a); await credit(b);  -- separate transactions
clean: await tx(async t => { await debit(t,a); await credit(t,b); })

terms.title: Dates, numbers, and booleans use real types, not varchar

signals: a date/number/boolean semantic value stored in a varchar/text column
a column named *_date, is_*, *_count typed as string

terms.smell: A value that is really a date, number, or boolean is stored as `varchar`/`text` — e.g. `signup_date varchar`, or `is_active varchar` holding `'true'`/`'yes'`/`'1'`.
terms.why: String dates sort lexically (`'2020-1-9' > '2020-12-1'`), can't do range math, and accept `'2020-13-40'`. String booleans accumulate `'t'`, `'true'`, `'TRUE'`, `'1'`, `'yes'` until every `WHERE` clause is a guessing game and half the rows are missed.
terms.fix: Use the real type: `date`/`timestamptz`, `integer`/`numeric`, `boolean`. Migrate existing string columns with a validated backfill.

example.before: signup_date varchar, is_active varchar
example.after: signup_date date, is_active boolean

clean: is_active boolean NOT NULL DEFAULT false

terms.title: Bounded-domain columns carry a length/format CHECK

signals: a column with a known fixed shape (country_code, phone, currency) typed as unbounded text

terms.smell: A column with a real bound — `country_code`, `phone`, `currency` — is unbounded `text` with no length or format constraint.
terms.why: Garbage like a 4 MB "country code" gets in, breaks downstream fixed-width assumptions, and bloats the row. The DB should reject the impossible value at the source.
terms.fix: Add a `CHECK` for the real rule: `country_code char(2) CHECK (country_code ~ '^[A-Z]{2}$')`. In Postgres `varchar(n)` vs `text` has no perf difference — use a `CHECK` for actual rules, not `varchar(255)` cargo-culting.

terms.title: Write-skew invariants protected by SERIALIZABLE or explicit locking

signals: each txn reads a set, checks an invariant, then writes a different row than it read
an at-least-one / at-most-N invariant across multiple rows

terms.smell: Two transactions each read a set, each check an invariant that holds, then each write a *different* row — e.g. two on-call doctors each see count=2 and each set their own row `on_call = false`.
terms.why: Together they break the invariant even though each individually saw a valid state — now zero doctors on call. Snapshot isolation (Postgres REPEATABLE READ) does NOT catch this; "we use transactions" is not automatically "we're safe."
terms.fix: SERIALIZABLE isolation (Postgres detects the conflict and aborts one — add retry), or materialize the conflict with explicit locking (`SELECT ... FOR UPDATE` over the rows the invariant depends on), or enforce it with a constraint where possible.

trips: -- SELECT count(*) WHERE on_call; if > 1: UPDATE me SET on_call=false  (REPEATABLE READ)
clean: SET TRANSACTION ISOLATION LEVEL SERIALIZABLE  -- with retry on serialization failure
SELECT ... WHERE on_call FOR UPDATE  -- lock the invariant's rows

terms.title: Every interactive control has an accessible name (placeholder isn't a label)

signals: icon-only <button> with no aria-label/text
<input> with no <label> / aria-label
placeholder used as the only label

terms.smell: An icon-only button or an input has no accessible name — no visible `<label>`, no `aria-label`/`aria-labelledby` — or a placeholder is used as the only label.
terms.why: A screen reader announces "button" or "edit text" with no purpose, so the control is unusable; placeholders vanish on input, leaving the field unlabeled once the user starts typing. (WCAG 1.3.1, 2.4.6, 3.3.2, 4.1.2)
terms.fix: Give every control a name: prefer a visible `<label for>`; use `aria-label`/`aria-labelledby` only when no visible text exists. Add `aria-label` to icon-only buttons and hide the icon with `aria-hidden`.

example.before:
<button><svg>…</svg></button>
<input type="email" placeholder="Email">
example.after:
<button aria-label="Close dialog"><svg aria-hidden="true">…</svg></button>
<label for="email">Email</label><input id="email" type="email" autocomplete="email">

terms.title: ARIA only where native won't do, with states kept in sync

signals: redundant role on a native element (role="button" on <button>)
div role="button" reinventing a native control
aria-expanded/selected/checked not updated when state changes
decorative icon without aria-hidden

terms.smell: ARIA is redundant or wrong: a `role` on an element that already has it, a fake role reinventing a native control, `aria-*` state that isn't updated when the actual state changes, or a decorative icon not hidden with `aria-hidden`.
terms.why: Wrong roles, fake roles, and stale `aria-*` states actively mislead screen-reader users — worse than no ARIA (announcing "expanded" on a collapsed menu). (WCAG 4.1.2)
terms.fix: Reach for the native element first; use ARIA only to express state native HTML can't. Keep `aria-expanded`/`aria-selected`/`aria-checked`/`aria-current` in sync with real state, and mark decorative icons `aria-hidden="true"`.

example.before: Menu
example.after: <button aria-expanded="true" aria-controls="menu">Menu</button>

terms.title: No barrel-file imports defeating tree-shaking; per-function lib imports

signals: import { X } from '@/components' (re-exporting index)
import _ from 'lodash' for one function

terms.smell: Imports come from a re-exporting barrel (`index.ts`) that pulls the whole module graph, or a whole utility library is imported for one function (`import _ from 'lodash'`).
terms.why: Barrel files and namespace imports defeat tree-shaking, so the bundle ships far more code than the app uses — extra parse/execute cost on every load.
terms.fix: Import directly from the module (`import { Button } from '@/components/Button'`) and per-function from libs (`import debounce from 'lodash/debounce'`). Ensure deps are ESM and `"sideEffects": false`; check the bundle with an analyzer for surprises.

example.before:
import { Button } from '@/components';
import _ from 'lodash';
example.after:
import { Button } from '@/components/Button';
import debounce from 'lodash/debounce';

terms.title: Every image/video/iframe/ad has dimensions or aspect-ratio

signals: /

terms.smell: An image, video, iframe, or ad slot has no `width`/`height` and no CSS `aspect-ratio`, so it reflows the page when it loads.
terms.why: The unsized element pushes content down when it arrives (layout shift) — users misclick or lose their place mid-read. Drives CLS above the 0.1 target.
terms.fix: Set `width`/`height` attributes or a CSS `aspect-ratio` on every image, video, iframe, and ad slot so the browser reserves the space before the asset loads.

terms.title: Contrast meets WCAG AA; meaning never conveyed by color alone

signals: light-gray text on white below 4.5:1
state signalled only by color (red border, green dot) with no text/icon

terms.smell: Text falls below 4.5:1 (or 3:1 for large text), UI components/icons/focus rings fall below 3:1, or meaning is conveyed by color alone (red border with no message, green dot with no label).
terms.why: Low-contrast text is unreadable for low-vision users; color-only signals are invisible to colorblind users — they can't tell an error from a normal field. (WCAG 1.4.3, 1.4.11, 1.4.1)
terms.fix: Meet contrast ratios: ≥ 4.5:1 body text, ≥ 3:1 large text and UI/graphics. Always pair color with text or an icon (error state = red border + icon + message). Re-check in both light and dark themes.

terms.title: Reusable components style off their container, not the viewport

signals: a reusable component using viewport @media queries to change its own layout

terms.smell: A reusable component sizes itself off the viewport width via `@media` queries, so it looks wrong when placed in a narrow sidebar on a wide screen (or vice versa).
terms.why: Viewport media queries know nothing about the component's actual available space, so the same component renders broken in different containers — the whole point of reusability fails.
terms.fix: Make the component respond to its own space with container queries: set `container-type: inline-size` on the wrapper and use `@container (min-width: …)`.

example.before: @media (min-width: 24rem) { .card { display: grid; } }
example.after:
.card-wrap { container-type: inline-size; }
@container (min-width: 24rem) { .card { display: grid; grid-template-columns: auto 1fr; } }

terms.title: Context split by update frequency; provider value memoized

signals: one context Provider holding both rarely-changing config and high-frequency values
Provider value={{ ... }} object literal not memoized

terms.smell: A single mega-context holds both rarely-changing config (theme, user) and fast-changing values (pointer/scroll position), or the provider `value` is a fresh object literal each render.
terms.why: Every consumer re-renders whenever any field changes — high-frequency values thrash the whole subtree, and an unmemoized `value` re-renders all consumers on every parent render. Jank on large trees.
terms.fix: Split context by update frequency (a stable ThemeContext separate from a fast PointerContext) and memoize the provider `value`.

example.before: <AppContext.Provider value={{ theme, user, cursorX, cursorY }}>
example.after:
<ThemeContext.Provider value={theme}>
  <PointerContext.Provider value={pointer}>

terms.title: Controlled inputs keep a defined value and an onChange

signals: <input value={x}> with no onChange
value that can be undefined then a string

terms.smell: An input has `value` but no `onChange` (read-only), or its `value` flips between `undefined` and a string across renders — switching the field between controlled and uncontrolled.
terms.why: The field appears frozen to the user, or React warns and loses cursor position when the control mode flips. Data entry silently breaks.
terms.fix: Pick one mode. For controlled inputs keep `value` a defined string (`value={name ?? ''}`) and always pair it with `onChange`.

example.before: <input value={name} />
example.after: <input value={name ?? ''} onChange={e => setName(e.target.value)} />

terms.title: Theming via tokens; prefers-color-scheme and color-scheme honored

signals: hard-coded colors instead of theme tokens
no prefers-color-scheme handling / missing color-scheme

terms.smell: Colors are hard-coded rather than driven through tokens, `prefers-color-scheme` is ignored, or `color-scheme` isn't set so form controls/scrollbars stay light in dark mode.
terms.why: Hard-coded colors can't theme, and forcing a bright UI on users who chose dark is jarring and can hurt readability; unstyled native controls clash. Contrast may also fail in the untested theme.
terms.fix: Drive colors through CSS custom-property tokens, provide a `@media (prefers-color-scheme: dark)` override, set `color-scheme: light dark` so native UI themes too, and re-check contrast in both themes.

terms.title: High-frequency input handlers are debounced/throttled (passive scroll)

signals: input/keyup handler firing a network request per keystroke
scroll/resize/mousemove handler doing layout work every event

terms.smell: A search/filter input fires work (often a network request) on every keystroke, or a scroll/resize/mousemove handler runs expensive work on every event, with no debounce/throttle and no `{ passive: true }` on scroll/touch.
terms.why: Un-throttled handlers run expensive work on every event, flooding the API and blocking the main thread so the UI lags behind the user's input — high INP, janky scroll, results trailing the typing.
terms.fix: Debounce input handlers (~300ms after the user stops typing), throttle scroll/resize (~100ms), and pass `{ passive: true }` to scroll/touch listeners.

example.before: input.addEventListener('input', e => fetchResults(e.target.value));
example.after: input.addEventListener('input', debounce(e => fetchResults(e.target.value), 300));

terms.title: Derived values computed in render, not synced via an effect

signals: useEffect that only calls setState to combine other state/props
a watch/effect that assigns a value derivable from its inputs

terms.smell: An effect (`useEffect`, Vue `watch`, Angular `effect`) exists only to compute a value from other props/state and store it in state — instead of computing it during render / in a `computed` / `useMemo`.
terms.why: The effect fires a second render on every change (flicker, wasted work) and can desync when inputs update but the effect hasn't run yet. Effects are for synchronizing with external systems, not for deriving values.
terms.fix: Compute the value inline during render (or `useMemo`/`computed`/signal `computed` if it's expensive). Delete the effect and the extra state.

example.before:
const [fullName, setFullName] = useState('');
useEffect(() => { setFullName(`${first} ${last}`); }, [first, last]);
example.after: const fullName = `${first} ${last}`;

clean: const total = useMemo(() => heavy(a, b), [a, b])

terms.title: Matches the design spec for spacing, type scale, and color

signals: magic spacing/color values that don't match design tokens
a design hand-off (Figma) to check the implementation against

terms.smell: The implementation drifts from the design spec on spacing, type scale, or color — ad-hoc pixel values and off-palette colors instead of the agreed tokens — and the deviation isn't noted.
terms.why: Small unmanaged drifts accumulate into an inconsistent, off-brand UI and make the design system meaningless. A silent deviation is a decision no one signed off on.
terms.fix: Match the spec's spacing scale, type scale, and color tokens; if a deviation is intentional, call it out so it's a decision, not an accident. Use design tokens rather than magic numbers.

terms.title: Empty, loading, error, and no-results states are all designed

signals: a view that renders data but has no branch for empty/loading/error
only the happy path handled in a data-driven component

terms.smell: A data-driven view handles only the happy path — no designed empty state, no loading skeleton/spinner, no error state, and no distinct "no results" state.
terms.why: Users hit a blank screen during load, a confusing void when there's no data, or an unhandled crash on error — the non-happy paths are exactly where real usage lands (slow network, new account, failed request).
terms.fix: Design and implement all four: empty (first-run/no data yet), loading (skeleton/spinner), error (with a retry), and no-results (distinct from empty, e.g. a filter that matched nothing).

terms.title: Subscriptions, listeners, and timers are cleaned up

signals: addEventListener / setInterval / subscribe inside an effect with no matching teardown

terms.smell: An effect registers an event listener, timer, or subscription but returns no cleanup (`removeEventListener`, `clearInterval`, `unsubscribe`, `onScopeDispose`, `takeUntilDestroyed`).
terms.why: Listeners, timers, and subscriptions accumulate across mounts/navigations — memory grows and duplicate handlers fire (a "ghost" callback updates a component that's already gone). Grows worse the longer the session lasts.
terms.fix: Return a cleanup function from the effect that tears down exactly what it set up. In Angular prefer the `async` pipe or `takeUntilDestroyed()`; in Vue register `onScopeDispose`/lifecycle cleanup in composables.

terms.title: Independently-failing regions are wrapped in an error boundary

signals: a new route/widget/data-loading subtree added with no ErrorBoundary/Suspense around it

terms.smell: A route or independently-loading widget has no error boundary (and no Suspense/loading fallback) around it, so a render error or a rejected data load has nothing to catch it.
terms.why: One component's render crash white-screens the entire app to a blank page instead of a localized error state; without a loading fallback users stare at empty space during fetches.
terms.fix: Wrap each independently-failing region (route, widget, data subtree) in an error boundary with a retry/fallback, and a Suspense/skeleton for loading. Note boundaries don't catch errors in event handlers or async code — handle those with try/catch + state.

example.before: <Dashboard />
example.after:
<ErrorBoundary fallback={<RetryCard />}>
  <Suspense fallback={<Skeleton />}>
    <Dashboard />
  </Suspense>
</ErrorBoundary>

terms.title: Fetch-on-change cancels or guards against out-of-order responses

signals: fetch inside an effect keyed on a query/id with no AbortController or active flag
mergeMap on a request-per-input stream

terms.smell: An effect/watcher fires a request whenever a query or id changes but doesn't cancel or guard the previous in-flight request (no `AbortController`, no `active` flag, RxJS `mergeMap` instead of `switchMap`).
terms.why: A slow earlier request can resolve after a faster later one and overwrite fresh results with stale data — the user searches "shoes", then "shirts", and sees shoe results. Silent and timing-dependent.
terms.fix: Cancel or ignore superseded responses: `AbortController` + an `active` guard in React, `onCleanup` in a Vue watcher, `switchMap` in Angular. Better, use a data library (TanStack Query, SWR, RTK Query) that handles this for you.

terms.title: Perf regressions confirmed against field/RUM, not one lab run

signals: a claimed Web Vitals regression/improvement backed only by a single Lighthouse run

terms.smell: A Web Vitals regression (or improvement) is asserted from a single Lighthouse/lab run, with no field/RUM confirmation.
terms.why: Lab numbers are a noisy proxy — they vary run to run and don't reflect real devices/networks. Field data is the source of truth; acting on one lab run can chase noise or miss a real regression users feel.
terms.fix: Confirm suspected regressions against field/RUM (the `web-vitals` library, Chrome UX Report, or your analytics) where available; use lab tools to diagnose, field data to decide.

terms.title: Prefer min-height over fixed height; don't clip content or disable zoom

signals: fixed height + overflow: hidden on a text container
user-scalable=no / maximum-scale=1 in the viewport meta

terms.smell: A container uses a fixed `height` with `overflow: hidden` on content that can grow, or the viewport meta disables zoom (`user-scalable=no`).
terms.why: Fixed height clips content when text is longer (other languages), the user zooms, or a larger font is set — the text is simply cut off. Disabling zoom blocks low-vision users from enlarging text.
terms.fix: Use `min-height` and let content dictate height; never disable pinch-zoom. Design for text that grows.

example.before: .card { height: 220px; overflow: hidden; }
example.after: .card { min-height: 220px; }

terms.title: Fluid sizing between breakpoints (clamp/min/max, auto-fit minmax)

signals: fixed widths/font sizes that only change at breakpoints
many media queries to size the same element

terms.smell: Fixed widths and font sizes that only change at breakpoints, producing jumpy resizing and awkward in-between states — instead of intrinsically fluid values.
terms.why: Stepwise sizing looks broken between breakpoints and needs a media query for every size; the layout doesn't adapt to the actual viewport.
terms.fix: Use fluid primitives — `clamp()`/`min()`/`max()` for sizes and `repeat(auto-fit, minmax(...))` for grids, `flex: 1 1 min(100%, 18rem)` for wrapping — so layout flexes smoothly and needs fewer media queries.

example.before:
h1 { font-size: 1.5rem; }
@media (min-width: 960px) { h1 { font-size: 3rem; } }
example.after:
h1 { font-size: clamp(1.5rem, 1rem + 2.5vw, 3rem); }
.auto-grid { grid-template-columns: repeat(auto-fit, minmax(16rem, 1fr)); }

terms.title: Client-side navigation moves focus and updates the document title

signals: SPA router navigation with no focus move or title update

terms.smell: On client-side (SPA) navigation the URL changes but focus stays on the old element and the document title isn't updated.
terms.why: Screen-reader users get no signal that the page changed — focus is stranded in stale content and the announced title is wrong, so they don't know where they are.
terms.fix: On route change, move focus to the new view's heading (or a skip target) and update `document.title` to match the new page.

terms.title: Visible focus ring; no blanket outline:none

signals: outline: none / outline: 0 with no :focus-visible replacement

terms.smell: Focus outlines are removed (`*:focus { outline: none }` or `outline: 0`) without a visible replacement.
terms.why: Keyboard users lose all indication of where they are on the page — navigation becomes guesswork. (WCAG 2.4.7 Focus Visible, 2.4.11 Focus Not Obscured)
terms.fix: Never blanket-remove the outline. Provide a clear `:focus-visible` ring (shows for keyboard, not mouse): `:focus-visible { outline: 2px solid; outline-offset: 2px; }`.

example.before: *:focus { outline: none; }
example.after: :focus-visible { outline: 2px solid; outline-offset: 2px; }

terms.title: Web fonts use font-display swap and a metric-matched fallback

signals: @font-face without font-display
a web font with no metric-matched fallback (size-adjust/ascent-override)

terms.smell: A `@font-face` has no `font-display: swap` (invisible text during load, FOIT), or swaps to a poorly-matched fallback that reflows the text when the web font arrives.
terms.why: Render-blocking fonts delay text paint; a metric-mismatched fallback visibly jumps the layout on swap (CLS) — the text shifts under the reader's eyes.
terms.fix: Set `font-display: swap` (or `optional`), `preload` the primary font, and pair with a metric-matched fallback (`size-adjust`/`ascent-override` or a well-chosen system font) to minimize the swap shift. Subset and ship `woff2`.

example.before: @font-face { font-family: Inter; src: url(inter.woff2) format('woff2'); }
example.after: @font-face { font-family: Inter; src: url(inter.woff2) format('woff2'); font-display: swap; }

terms.title: Form errors are programmatically associated and announced

signals: an error <span> not linked to its input via aria-describedby
validation error not in a live region / role=alert

terms.smell: A form error message isn't tied to its field (no `aria-describedby`, no `aria-invalid`) and isn't announced (`role="alert"`/live region) — just a loose `<span>` beside the input.
terms.why: An error not linked to its field and not announced is invisible to screen-reader users — they can't tell what failed or why, and can't fix the form. (WCAG 3.3.1, 3.3.3, 1.3.1)
terms.fix: Link the error to the field with `aria-describedby`, set `aria-invalid="true"`, and put the message in `role="alert"`/a live region so it's announced. Show errors in text, near the field.

example.before: Required
example.after: Name is required

terms.title: Reusable input/control components forward their ref

signals: a custom Input/Button wrapper that spreads props but drops ref

terms.smell: A reusable wrapper around a native control (`<Input>`, `<Button>`) renders the element but drops the ref, so a parent can't reach the underlying DOM node.
terms.why: Parents can't focus, scroll-to, or measure the control — breaking focus management (focus the first invalid field, focus a modal's input) which is core to keyboard accessibility.
terms.fix: Forward the ref to the underlying element (`forwardRef`, or in React 19 accept `ref` as a regular prop). Either way, thread it through.

example.before: function Input(props) { return <input {...props} />; }
example.after:
const Input = forwardRef<HTMLInputElement, Props>((props, ref) =>
  <input ref={ref} {...props} />);

terms.title: Grid for 2D layouts, Flexbox for 1D — no nested-flex grid hacks

signals: nested flex containers faking a 2D grid with aligned rows and columns

terms.smell: Nested flexbox hacks are used to fake a two-dimensional layout that needs aligned rows and columns, instead of CSS Grid.
terms.why: Nested-flex grids produce fragile alignment that breaks when content length varies — columns drift out of line as text wraps differently.
terms.fix: Use `display: grid` with explicit tracks for 2D layouts (rows and columns), and Flexbox for one-dimensional flow (a wrapping toolbar, a row/column of content-sized items).

terms.title: One h1 per view; no skipped heading levels

signals: multiple <h1> in one view, or a jump from h1 to h4
heading level chosen for font size rather than structure

terms.smell: A view has more than one `<h1>`, skips heading levels (h1 → h4), or picks heading levels for visual size rather than document structure.
terms.why: Screen-reader users navigate by heading level; skipped or misused levels break the document outline so they can't tell how content is organized. (WCAG 1.3.1, 2.4.6)
terms.fix: One `<h1>` per page/view, then descend without skipping (`h2`, `h3`…). Style headings with CSS, not by choosing a bigger/smaller tag.

terms.title: Hook/effect dependency arrays are complete and honest

signals: eslint-disable next-line react-hooks/exhaustive-deps
useEffect/useCallback/useMemo with a dependency array that omits a referenced value

terms.smell: A `useEffect`/`useCallback`/`useMemo` omits a value it reads from its dependency array, or suppresses the exhaustive-deps lint rule to silence the warning.
terms.why: A missing dependency captures a stale value forever; suppressing the lint hides a real correctness bug. The effect runs with outdated props/state and the UI silently desyncs.
terms.fix: List every referenced value, or restructure so it isn't needed (functional state updates, move the value inside the effect, useRef for mutable-but-non-reactive values). Trust the exhaustive-deps rule rather than disabling it.

trips: useEffect(() => { doThing(userId); }, []) // userId referenced but omitted

terms.title: Hover-only controls have a touch and keyboard equivalent

signals: actions revealed only on :hover (opacity 0 → 1 on hover)
hover-only affordance with no focus-within / touch fallback

terms.smell: A menu or action is revealed only on `:hover` (e.g. `.card:hover .actions { opacity: 1 }`) with no touch or keyboard path to it.
terms.why: Touch devices have no hover, so hover-only controls are undiscoverable or unreachable — the feature effectively doesn't exist on mobile, and keyboard users can't reach it either.
terms.fix: Make the action always visible/focusable, or gate the hover-only enhancement behind `@media (hover: hover) and (pointer: fine)` and ensure it's also reachable via tap and `:focus-within`.

example.before:
.actions { opacity: 0; }
.card:hover .actions { opacity: 1; }

terms.title: Injected banners/cookie bars reserve space or overlay — don't push content

signals: a banner/cookie bar/async section inserted above existing content

terms.smell: Banners, cookie bars, or async-loaded sections are inserted above content the user is already reading, shoving everything down.
terms.why: Injecting content above the fold mid-interaction shifts the page under the user — they tap the wrong thing or lose their place. A classic CLS source.
terms.fix: Reserve a fixed-height slot for the content ahead of time, insert it below the fold, or overlay it (position over, don't push).

terms.title: Interactive elements have hover, active, AND focus states

signals: a :hover style with no matching :focus/:focus-visible
new interactive control styled only in its resting state

terms.smell: An interactive control styles `:hover` (and maybe `:active`) but not `:focus`/`:focus-visible`, or lacks visible interaction feedback entirely; transitions are missing or abrupt.
terms.why: A hover state without a focus state leaves keyboard users with no feedback about where they are — the same styling that helps mouse users abandons keyboard users. Missing feedback makes controls feel dead.
terms.fix: Provide `:hover`, `:active`, and `:focus-visible` states together; use smooth transitions (gated by `prefers-reduced-motion`). Never ship a hover affordance without a focus equivalent.

terms.title: Everything is keyboard operable with logical tab order and a skip link

signals: onClick on a div/span with no tabindex/role/key handler
positive tabindex values
a composite widget with no arrow-key support

terms.smell: An actionable element isn't reachable/operable by keyboard (`onClick` on a `<div>` with no `tabindex`/`role`/key handler), tab order doesn't follow reading order, positive `tabindex` values are used, or there's no skip link past repeated nav.
terms.why: Mouse-only widgets exclude keyboard and switch users entirely — they literally cannot activate the control. Positive tabindex scrambles the tab order. (WCAG 2.1.1, 2.4.1, 2.4.3)
terms.fix: Use native `<button>`/`<a>` (keyboard support built in); for truly custom widgets add `role`+`tabindex="0"` and Enter/Space (and Arrow keys per ARIA Authoring Practices). Keep tab order = visual order, use only `tabindex="0"`/`-1`, and add a skip link.

example.before: Menu
example.after: Skip to content

terms.title: LCP element is eager and prioritized, never lazy-loaded

signals: loading="lazy" on an above-the-fold hero image
the largest above-the-fold image/text with no fetchpriority/preload

terms.smell: The above-the-fold hero (largest contentful element) is lazy-loaded or de-prioritized — `loading="lazy"` on the hero, no `fetchpriority="high"` or preload — or slow TTFB / a client-side fetch waterfall delays it.
terms.why: Lazy-loading or de-prioritizing the LCP element delays the main paint; users stare at a blank/partial page longer, blowing the LCP ≤ 2.5s target — a real user-facing regression.
terms.fix: Eagerly load and prioritize the hero (`fetchpriority="high"`, or `<link rel="preload" as="image">`), give it explicit dimensions, and never lazy-load above-the-fold imagery. For text/data LCP, stream HTML and fetch above-the-fold data on the server / in parallel.

terms.title: List keys / track use stable ids, never the array index for reorderable lists

signals: key={i} / :key="i" / *ngFor without trackBy over a list that can reorder or filter
map((item, i) => ... key={i})

terms.smell: A list uses the array index as its React `key` / Vue `:key` / omits Angular `track`/`trackBy`, on a list that can reorder, filter, insert, or delete.
terms.why: On reorder the framework reuses the wrong DOM node — checkbox state, focus, text-input contents, and transitions jump to the wrong row. Without Angular tracking, the list destroys and rebuilds every node (lost focus/scroll, flicker, slow updates).
terms.fix: Key on a stable unique id from the data (`key={item.id}`, `:key="item.id"`, `track item.id`). Only fall back to the index for a list that is static and never reorders.

example.before: {items.map((item, i) => <Item key={i} {...item} />)}
example.after: {items.map(item => <Item key={item.id} {...item} />)}

terms.title: Async status updates announced via a live region

signals: toast/save-confirmation/result-count updated with no aria-live region

terms.smell: Dynamic status changes (toasts, "3 results found", cart count, save confirmations) update the DOM with no `aria-live` region to announce them.
terms.why: Without a live region these changes happen silently — screen-reader users never learn the search returned results or the save succeeded, so they don't know the action worked.
terms.fix: Announce async updates from a polite live region (`aria-live="polite" aria-atomic="true"`); reserve `assertive` for urgent interruptions.

example.after: {{ statusMessage }}

terms.title: Logical properties for spacing/alignment (RTL-safe)

signals: margin-left/right, padding-left/right, text-align: left in a multi-locale app

terms.smell: Physical directional properties (`margin-left`, `padding-right`, `text-align: left`) are used in an app that supports (or may support) right-to-left locales.
terms.why: Physical left/right hard-code one direction, so RTL languages (Arabic, Hebrew) render mirrored/wrong — spacing lands on the wrong side and text aligns incorrectly.
terms.fix: Use logical properties that adapt to writing direction: `margin-inline-start`, `padding-inline-end`, `text-align: start`.

example.before: .card { margin-left: 1rem; padding-right: 2rem; text-align: left; }
example.after: .card { margin-inline-start: 1rem; padding-inline-end: 2rem; text-align: start; }

terms.title: No long tasks > 50ms on interaction; heavy work chunked or deferred

signals: a large synchronous loop processing thousands of items on click
reading offsetHeight/getBoundingClientRect then writing styles in a loop

terms.smell: An interaction runs a single synchronous long task — processing thousands of items on click, or forcing synchronous layout ("layout thrashing") by interleaving DOM reads (`offsetHeight`/`getBoundingClientRect`) and style writes in a loop.
terms.why: Any task over 50ms blocks input handling; the page feels frozen (high INP). Layout thrashing makes the browser recompute layout repeatedly within one frame.
terms.fix: Chunk the work and yield to the main thread (`await scheduler.yield()`, `setTimeout`, `requestIdleCallback`), move it to a Web Worker, or defer non-urgent updates (`useTransition`/`useDeferredValue`, Angular signals/`afterNextRender`). Batch all reads, then all writes.

terms.title: Informative images have meaningful alt; decorative images use alt=""

signals: with no alt attribute
decorative image given a descriptive alt (noise)

terms.smell: An informative image has no `alt` (or an unhelpful one), or a purely decorative image has a descriptive `alt` that adds noise instead of `alt=""`.
terms.why: A missing `alt` leaves screen-reader users with no idea what the image conveys; a decorative image with `alt` text clutters the reading experience with irrelevant announcements. (WCAG 1.1.1, 1.2.2)
terms.fix: Give informative images descriptive `alt` (convey the meaning, e.g. "Revenue rose 20% Q1→Q2"); give decorative images `alt=""` (empty, not missing) so they're skipped. Provide captions for video, transcripts for audio, and a long description for complex charts.

terms.title: TypeScript is meaningful — no any on props/state; variants as unions

signals: any on a prop, state, or public component API
a variant prop typed as string with a runtime switch instead of a union

terms.smell: Component props or state are typed `any` (an escape hatch that disables checking), or a variant prop is a bare `string`/`boolean` grab-bag instead of a discriminated union.
terms.why: `any` on the public surface silently propagates untyped values and hides real type errors at call sites; loose variant types allow illegal prop combinations the compiler should have caught. Bugs slip to runtime.
terms.fix: Type props/state precisely; model mutually-exclusive variants as discriminated unions so the compiler enforces which props go together. Replace `any` with `unknown` plus narrowing, or a real type.

terms.title: Modals trap focus, close on Esc, and return focus to the trigger

signals: a custom modal/dialog with no focus management
a dialog that doesn't close on Esc or restore focus on close

terms.smell: A dialog opens while focus stays on the page behind it, Tab leaks into the background content, Esc doesn't close it, and focus isn't returned to the trigger on close.
terms.why: Keyboard and screen-reader users get "lost behind" the modal — they Tab into hidden background content and can't operate the dialog. (WCAG 2.4.3, 2.1.2)
terms.fix: Use a native `<dialog>` or implement: move focus into the dialog on open, cycle Tab inside it, close on Esc, and restore focus to the trigger on close.

terms.title: Stable identity for props to memoized children; memoize only where it pays

signals: fresh object/array/function literal passed to a React.memo child every render
new closure per row in a list of memoized rows

terms.smell: A fresh object/array literal or inline function is passed to a memoized child on every render, defeating `React.memo`. Or, conversely, cheap values are wrapped in `useMemo`/`useCallback` where the bookkeeping costs more than it saves.
terms.why: A new reference each render makes the memoized child re-render anyway — the optimization is dead weight, and a per-row closure re-renders a whole long list on any state change (janky scroll). Over-memoizing cheap values just adds noise and overhead.
terms.fix: Stabilize the identity of values passed to memoized children with `useMemo`/`useCallback` (or hoist constants out of the component). Reserve memoization for expensive computations and for props crossing a `React.memo` boundary; leave cheap inline handlers alone.

example.before: <ExpensiveList style={{ marginTop: 8 }} items={data.filter(Boolean)} />

terms.title: No method calls or impure pipes doing real work in hot templates

signals: {{ formatTotal() }} / {{ heavyCompute(item) }} — a function called in a template binding
an impure pipe running non-trivial work

terms.smell: A template binding calls a method (`{{ formatTotal() }}`, `{{ heavyCompute(item) }}`) or uses an impure pipe that does real work — so it re-executes on every render / change-detection pass.
terms.why: Methods and impure pipes in templates run on every render regardless of whether inputs changed — a hidden, repeated cost that drags interaction latency (INP) on busy views.
terms.fix: Derive the value once with a `computed`/signal `computed` (cached, recomputes only when deps change), a pure pipe (memoized by input), or precompute in the component.

example.before: {{ formatTotal() }}
example.after: {{ total }}

terms.title: No horizontal scroll at common widths; wide content constrained

signals: width: 100vw
fixed pixel widths wider than the mobile viewport
long unbroken strings or wide tables with no overflow guard

terms.smell: Fixed widths or `100vw` (which includes the scrollbar) cause horizontal overflow, or long unbroken strings / wide tables / media aren't constrained.
terms.why: Unexpected horizontal scroll is a top mobile bug — content gets cut off and the page feels broken. Users can't reach the clipped content.
terms.fix: Use `width: 100%` not `100vw`; cap media/tables/`pre` with `max-width: 100%`; break long strings with `overflow-wrap: anywhere`; wrap wide tables in a horizontal scroll container.

example.before: .banner { width: 100vw; }
example.after:
.banner { width: 100%; }
img, video, pre, table { max-width: 100%; }

terms.title: Non-trivial Angular components use OnPush with immutable inputs

signals: a large/list component using default change detection
mutating @Input objects in place rather than replacing references

terms.smell: A large or frequently-rendered Angular component uses the default change-detection strategy, or feeds `OnPush` mutable inputs mutated in place.
terms.why: Default strategy re-checks the whole component on every async event anywhere in the app — wasted work and visible jank on large lists. Mutating `OnPush` inputs by reference means updates never show.
terms.fix: Set `changeDetection: ChangeDetectionStrategy.OnPush` on non-trivial components and feed them immutable inputs and signals. `OnPush` re-checks only on input-reference change, a component event, or an `async`-bound emission.

example.before: @Component({ /* default strategy */ })
example.after: @Component({ changeDetection: ChangeDetectionStrategy.OnPush })

terms.title: prefers-reduced-motion is honored for non-essential motion

signals: auto-playing animation/parallax with no prefers-reduced-motion guard
large transitions/animations not gated

terms.smell: Auto-playing or large animations/parallax/transitions run with no `prefers-reduced-motion` opt-out; content flashes more than 3×/sec.
terms.why: Large or vestibular-triggering motion can cause nausea and dizziness for affected users; flashing can trigger seizures. (WCAG 2.3.3, 2.3.1)
terms.fix: Gate non-essential motion behind `@media (prefers-reduced-motion: reduce)`, reducing animation/transition durations to near-zero and disabling parallax/auto-play. Avoid content that flashes more than 3×/sec.

example.before: .hero { animation: float 3s infinite; }

terms.title: Props are not mutated; changes flow up via events (one-way data flow)

signals: props.x = ... assignment inside a child component
$emit called with no defineEmits declaration

terms.smell: A child component mutates a prop directly (`props.modelValue = 'x'`) instead of emitting an event and letting the parent own the value.
terms.why: Mutating props breaks one-way data flow — Vue warns, and the parent and child disagree about the value, producing UI that flickers back or desyncs on the next parent render.
terms.fix: Emit an update (`emit('update:modelValue', v)`) and let the parent own state, or use `v-model` / a `computed` with get/set. For local editing, copy into a `ref`. Declare a typed `emits` contract so payloads are checked.

example.before:
const props = defineProps<{ modelValue: string }>();
props.modelValue = 'x';
example.after:
const emit = defineEmits<{ 'update:modelValue': [v: string] }>();
emit('update:modelValue', 'x');

terms.title: Props not copied into local state (remount via key to reset)

signals: useState(props.x) that then renders x without re-syncing

terms.smell: A component seeds local state from a prop (`useState(user.name)`) and then renders that state, so the value is frozen at mount and ignores later prop updates.
terms.why: When the prop changes, the UI keeps showing the stale value copied at mount — users see outdated data with no error. A common source of "the list updated but this card didn't."
terms.fix: Read the prop directly during render. If you genuinely need resettable local edit state when the entity changes, remount the subtree with `key={entity.id}` instead of copying the prop.

terms.title: No needless render-blocking CSS/JS; non-critical scripts deferred

signals: a synchronous <script> in <head> without defer/async
a blocking third-party script (analytics, chat, ads)
large synchronous CSS blocking first paint

terms.smell: Large synchronous CSS/JS in `<head>` blocks first paint, or a third-party script (analytics, chat, ads) loads render-blocking rather than `defer`/`async`.
terms.why: Every render-blocking byte pushes back the moment content appears (worse LCP); third-party scripts are frequent main-thread and INP offenders too.
terms.fix: Inline critical CSS and split the rest so only above-the-fold styles block; `defer`/`async` non-critical scripts; lazy-load third-party widgets and `preconnect` needed origins early.

example.before: <script src="analytics.js"></script>
example.after: <script src="analytics.js" defer></script>

terms.title: Responsive layout doesn't collapse to one column too early

signals: max-width media query that drops a multi-column grid to 1fr at a large width
breakpoint hard-coded to a device width (768px = iPad)

terms.smell: Desktop-first `max-width` queries collapse a multi-column layout to a single column at the first hint of narrowing (e.g. one column already at 1199px), or breakpoints are hard-coded to device widths rather than where content breaks.
terms.why: Collapsing early wastes tablet/laptop space and makes the design look broken on the most common screens; device-pinned breakpoints rot as device sizes change while content needs don't.
terms.fix: Go mobile-first with `min-width` queries, adding columns as space allows, and add breakpoints where the content actually starts to break (line length, wrapping, overflow) — not at device widths.

example.before:
.grid { grid-template-columns: repeat(4, 1fr); }
@media (max-width: 1200px) { .grid { grid-template-columns: 1fr; } }

terms.title: Images use modern format, responsive srcset/sizes, below-fold lazy

signals: a large fixed-src with no srcset/sizes
a 4000px original shipped into a small slot
below-the-fold image without loading="lazy"

terms.smell: Images ship in a legacy format, without responsive `srcset`/`sizes`, oversized for their slot (a 4000px original into a 400px box), or below-the-fold images aren't `loading="lazy"`.
terms.why: Oversized/uncompressed images are usually the single biggest LCP and bandwidth cost — slow loads on mobile data and a heavy page for no visual gain.
terms.fix: Serve AVIF/WebP with a fallback, compress to sane quality, provide `srcset` + `sizes` so each device gets an appropriately sized file, `loading="lazy"` below the fold (never the LCP image), and `decoding="async"`.

terms.title: Reusable components take props/slots and own no app-specific logic

signals: a shared/generic component imports a store, router, or API client directly
presentational component contains fetch calls or business rules

terms.smell: A component meant to be reusable hard-codes app-specific business logic, data fetching, or a concrete store/route — instead of taking data and callbacks via props/slots. Presentation and business logic are tangled in the same view file.
terms.why: The component can't be reused in another context or tested in isolation, and a change to a business rule forces edits scattered across view files. Coupling makes the UI brittle.
terms.fix: Keep reusable components dumb: they render props and emit events. Push business logic and data access into hooks/composables/services and pass results down. Separate the container (logic) from the presentational component.

terms.title: Routes are code-split; heavy optional features dynamically imported

signals: every route/feature statically imported into the entry bundle
a heavy dep (chart, editor, modal) imported eagerly but used conditionally

terms.smell: Every route and heavy optional feature (charts, rich editors, modals) is statically imported into the entry bundle instead of lazy-loaded on demand.
terms.why: Every shipped KB is parse/compile/execute time on the user's CPU; bundling all routes and heavy deps upfront bloats the initial download and hurts LCP and INP, worst on mid-range mobile.
terms.fix: Route-level split (`React.lazy`/dynamic `import()`, Angular `loadComponent`/`loadChildren`, Vue async components) so users download only the current view, and dynamically import heavy optional code when it's actually needed.

example.before: import Chart from './Chart';
example.after: const Chart = lazy(() => import('./Chart'));

terms.title: RxJS subscriptions torn down; prefer async pipe

signals: .subscribe( in a component with no takeUntilDestroyed / takeUntil / unsubscribe

terms.smell: A component subscribes to an observable (`interval(...).subscribe`, `svc.user$.subscribe`) without tearing it down — no `async` pipe, no `takeUntilDestroyed`/`takeUntil(destroy$)`.
terms.why: A leaked subscription keeps the destroyed component alive and its callback firing after navigation — memory growth and "ghost" updates that touch a component the user already left.
terms.fix: Prefer the `async` pipe (auto-subscribes/unsubscribes and plays well with `OnPush`). When you must subscribe manually, pipe `takeUntilDestroyed(this.destroyRef)` (Angular 16+) or `takeUntil(this.destroy$)`.

example.before: ngOnInit() { interval(1000).subscribe(() => this.tick()); }

terms.title: Native elements and landmarks over div soup

signals: <div onClick> / <div class="btn"> used as an interactive control
layout built from generic divs with no nav/main/header landmarks

terms.smell: Interactive controls are clickable `<div>`s (`<div class="btn" onClick>`) and page structure is generic `<div>`s instead of landmarks (`<nav>`, `<main>`, `<header>`, `<footer>`, `<aside>`) and native `<button>`/`<a>`.
terms.why: A clickable `<div>` is invisible to assistive tech and isn't keyboard-operable — screen-reader and keyboard users can't use it at all. Missing landmarks strip the navigation model they rely on. (WCAG 1.3.1, 4.1.2)
terms.fix: Use `<button>`/`<a>` for actions and links (built-in keyboard, focus, role), and landmark elements for structure. Reserve `<div>` for styling hooks, not behavior.

example.before: Save
example.after: <button type="button" onclick="save()">Save</button>

terms.title: Per-route title/meta description and structured data where relevant

signals: a new route/page with no <title>/meta description
product/article/breadcrumb pages with no JSON-LD structured data

terms.smell: A route/page ships without a meaningful `<title>` and meta description, or content that qualifies for rich results (products, articles, breadcrumbs) has no structured data.
terms.why: Missing/duplicate titles and descriptions hurt search ranking and click-through; absent structured data forgoes rich results. Semantic structure and `alt` double as SEO signals.
terms.fix: Set a meaningful `<title>` and meta description per route (one `<h1>`), and add JSON-LD structured data where it earns rich results.

terms.title: Props across the Server/Client boundary are serializable

signals: a function, class instance, or Date/Map passed from a Server Component to a Client Component

terms.smell: A Server Component passes a non-serializable value — a function (other than a Server Action), a class instance, or similar — as a prop to a Client Component.
terms.why: The framework can't serialize it across the boundary: a runtime serialization error, or silent loss of the behavior the prop was supposed to carry.
terms.fix: Pass only strings/numbers/plain objects/arrays, or a Server Action reference. Reconstruct rich objects on the client from plain data.

terms.title: Server-only code and secrets never cross into the client bundle

signals: 'use client' at the top of a module that imports a db client, server SDK, or secret env
process.env.SECRET / API private key referenced in a client component

terms.smell: A module marked `'use client'` (or otherwise bundled to the browser) imports server-only code — a database client, a server SDK, or a module that reads secret env vars.
terms.why: Marking a data/secret module `'use client'` ships server code and credentials into the browser bundle where anyone can read them, and bloats the bundle with heavy server deps. A leaked API key or DB URL is a security incident, not a perf nit.
terms.fix: Keep data access and secrets in Server Components / server modules; pass only plain serializable data down. Push `'use client'` to the interactive leaf components that actually need it, not over data/secret modules.

example.before:
'use client';
import { db } from '@/server/db'; // secrets + heavy deps bundled to the browser

terms.title: shareReplay is bounded with refCount to avoid a source leak

signals: shareReplay() with no refCount / bufferSize config

terms.smell: `shareReplay()` is used to dedupe a shared stream but without `{ bufferSize: 1, refCount: true }`.
terms.why: Without `refCount: true`, `shareReplay` keeps the upstream (e.g. an HTTP subscription) subscribed forever — a leak that never releases even after all consumers unsubscribe.
terms.fix: Configure it: `shareReplay({ bufferSize: 1, refCount: true })` so the source is released when the last subscriber leaves.

example.before: data$ = this.http.get('/api/config').pipe(shareReplay());
example.after: data$ = this.http.get('/api/config').pipe(shareReplay({ bufferSize: 1, refCount: true }));

terms.title: Handlers/effects don't capture stale state; use functional updates

signals: setInterval/setTimeout/subscription inside an effect reading state by closure
setState(state + x) where the new value depends on the old

terms.smell: A long-lived callback (interval, subscription, event handler set up once) reads state or props by closure, so it keeps seeing the value from when it was created. Or `setCount(count + 1)` is used where the update depends on the previous value.
terms.why: The counter freezes, the timer uses an old `step`, or rapid updates drop increments — a silent correctness bug that only shows under specific timing. Classic stale-closure trap.
terms.fix: Use the functional updater form `setX(prev => ...)` so the new value derives from the latest state, and re-create long-lived callbacks when their inputs change (correct deps).

terms.title: State lives in the lowest common owner, not over-lifted
