signals: state lifted to a top-level component when only one deep subtree uses it

terms.smell: State is lifted high (e.g. into a top-level `App`) when only one subtree actually uses it — over-lifting instead of colocating.
terms.why: Over-lifted state re-renders huge trees on every change and couples unrelated components, so a local toggle repaints the whole page.
terms.fix: Keep state in the lowest common component that needs it; lift only when two siblings must genuinely share it.

terms.title: State has one clear owner; no imperative DOM clobbering

signals: document.getElementById / querySelector then .innerHTML/.value/.style assignment inside a component
state duplicated in several components or in both the framework and the DOM

terms.smell: A component reaches past the framework to mutate the DOM directly (`document.getElementById(...).innerHTML = ...`, manual `.style`/`.value` writes) or the same state is owned in two places, so the DOM and the virtual tree disagree.
terms.why: Imperative DOM writes fight the framework's next render — the change is silently reverted, or the two sources of truth drift and the UI shows a stale/contradictory value. These bugs are intermittent and only appear after an unrelated re-render.
terms.fix: Give each piece of state a single owner and drive the DOM through render/bindings. Use a ref only for genuinely imperative APIs (focus, scroll, canvas) — never to overwrite content the framework also renders.

terms.title: Interactive targets are at least ~24x24 CSS px with spacing

signals: an icon button / tap target smaller than 24px
adjacent tap targets with no spacing

terms.smell: An interactive target is smaller than ~24×24 CSS px, or adjacent targets are packed with no spacing so they overlap.
terms.why: Tiny or cramped tap targets cause mis-taps, especially for motor-impaired and touch users who hit the wrong control. (WCAG 2.5.8 Target Size AA)
terms.fix: Size interactive targets at least 24×24 CSS px (44×44 is the comfortable mobile baseline) with enough spacing that adjacent targets don't overlap.

example.after: .icon-btn { min-width: 44px; min-height: 44px; }

terms.title: Never put v-if and v-for on the same element

signals: v-for and v-if on the same element

terms.smell: A single element carries both `v-for` and `v-if`, so the filter re-evaluates for every item on every render and the directive precedence is ambiguous.
terms.why: Precedence between the two is confusing and error-prone, and the condition runs for every item each render — needless work that scales with list size.
terms.fix: Filter in a `computed` list, or move `v-if` to an inner element wrapped in a keyed `<template v-for>`.

example.before: • {{ u.name }}

terms.title: Long lists are virtualized (windowed)

signals: rendering hundreds/thousands of rows directly to the DOM

terms.smell: A long list (hundreds to thousands of rows) is rendered entirely to the DOM instead of being windowed/virtualized.
terms.why: Huge DOM trees are slow to render, scroll, and update — memory use and interaction latency both suffer, and every state change re-diffs thousands of nodes.
terms.fix: Virtualize with a windowing library (`react-window`/`@tanstack/virtual`, Angular CDK `*cdkVirtualFor`, `vue-virtual-scroller`) so only visible rows are in the DOM.

terms.title: Vue lifecycle hooks registered synchronously in setup

signals: onMounted/onUnmounted registered after an await or inside a callback in <script setup>

terms.smell: A lifecycle hook (`onMounted`, `onUnmounted`) is registered after an `await` or inside a callback, rather than synchronously at the top level of `setup`.
terms.why: Hooks registered after the synchronous setup phase silently never fire — the mount logic (or its cleanup) just doesn't run, and there's no error to point at it.
terms.fix: Call all lifecycle hooks synchronously at the top level of `setup`; do async work inside the hook, not before registering it.

terms.title: Vue reactivity not lost by destructuring or reassigning a reactive

signals: const { x } = reactive(...) / defineProps() without toRefs
reassigning a reactive object: state = reactive({...})
destructuring a Pinia store without storeToRefs

terms.smell: Reactivity is broken by destructuring a `reactive`/props/store into plain values (without `toRefs`/`storeToRefs`), or by reassigning a `reactive` object wholesale instead of mutating it in place.
terms.why: The binding still points at the old proxy or a plain number, so values change in JS but the DOM never updates — the UI silently goes stale with no error.
terms.fix: Mutate `reactive` objects in place (or use a `ref` when you must swap the whole value). Keep reactivity when destructuring via `toRefs(state)`/`toRefs(props)`/`storeToRefs(store)`.

example.before: const { count } = reactive({ count: 0 }); // plain number now
example.after:
const state = reactive({ count: 0 });
const { count } = toRefs(state);

trips: let state = reactive({}); state = reactive({ count: 1 })

terms.title: z-index uses a named scale; stacking contexts understood

signals: escalating magic z-index numbers (z-index: 9999)
a high z-index that fails to escape a transformed/opacity parent

terms.smell: Escalating magic `z-index` numbers (`z-index: 9999`) scattered across files, or a high `z-index` that unexpectedly won't rise above a sibling.
terms.why: z-index wars cause modals to hide behind headers and tooltips to vanish; a high value can't escape a parent stacking context created by `transform`/`opacity < 1`/`filter`, so the fix doesn't work.
terms.fix: Define a small named z-index scale (`--z-dropdown`, `--z-modal`, `--z-toast`) and use it deliberately. Understand which properties create stacking contexts and lift the parent, not just the child.

example.after: :root { --z-dropdown: 10; --z-sticky: 20; --z-modal: 30; --z-toast: 40; }

terms.title: Autoscaling has min/max bounds tied to downstream capacity and budget

signals: an HPA with maxReplicas set absurdly high (e.g. 10000)
autoscaling with no upper bound, or a lower bound of 0/1 with no floor rationale

terms.smell: An autoscaler has no sensible bounds — `maxReplicas: 10000` (no ceiling) or an unconsidered `minReplicas` — untied to what downstream dependencies can absorb.
terms.why: A retry storm or bot flood drives autoscaling with `maxReplicas: 10000`; the service scales to thousands of pods, opens thousands of DB connections, exhausts the database's connection limit, and takes down every service sharing that database — plus a five-figure bill for the hour.
terms.fix: Bound `maxReplicas` to what downstream (DB connections, rate limits, budget) can absorb and set `minReplicas` for resilience. Same logic for cluster/node autoscaling and serverless concurrency limits.

example.before: spec: { minReplicas: 1, maxReplicas: 10000 }
example.after:
spec:
  minReplicas: 3
  maxReplicas: 40    # sized to DB connection limit / downstream / budget

terms.title: Migrations are backward-compatible during rollout (expand/contract); destructive step deferred

signals: a DROP COLUMN / RENAME COLUMN shipped in the same deploy as the code that needs it
a destructive schema change during a rolling/canary deploy

terms.smell: A migration makes a non-backward-compatible schema change (`DROP COLUMN`, `RENAME COLUMN`) and ships it with the code that needs it, so during the rollout old pods hit a schema they can't tolerate.
terms.why: During any non-atomic rollout old and new code run at once against one database. A `DROP COLUMN`/`RENAME` then rolls out new pods; for the 90 seconds the old pods still serve, every request touching that column throws — a self-inflicted partial outage inside a "safe" deploy.
terms.fix: Use expand/contract across separate ordered deploys: EXPAND (add nullable column/table, both work) → MIGRATE (backfill; new code writes both, reads new) → CONTRACT (drop the old shape in a later deploy once nothing reads it). Migration content/locking → database-review.

example.before:
ALTER TABLE users DROP COLUMN full_name;         -- old pods still SELECT full_name
ALTER TABLE users RENAME COLUMN email TO email_address;
example.after:
-- deploy A: ALTER TABLE users ADD COLUMN email_address text;   (additive)
-- deploy C (later): ALTER TABLE users DROP COLUMN email;       (contract)

trips: ALTER TABLE users DROP COLUMN full_name;   # shipped with the new code
ALTER TABLE users RENAME COLUMN email TO email_address;
clean: ALTER TABLE users ADD COLUMN email_address text;   # expand, additive

terms.title: Release branch has required checks and no direct/force push

signals: branch protection config changed or absent for the release branch
a deploy that trusts main without enforcing required status checks

terms.smell: The release branch allows direct pushes or force-pushes, or doesn't require status checks to pass before merge — so protected-branch invariants the deploy relies on aren't enforced.
terms.why: If anyone can force-push or push directly to main, the "only main deploys to prod" guarantee is hollow — a rewritten history or an unreviewed commit ships straight to production, bypassing the checks the deploy gate assumed had run.
terms.fix: Enable branch protection on the release branch: required status checks, required review, no direct push, no force-push. The deploy gate and branch protection are two halves of one control.

trips: allow_force_pushes = true   # on the release branch

terms.title: Breaking changes are additive/versioned or behind a feature flag rolled out gradually

signals: a removed endpoint / new required field shipped coupled to the deploy
a consumer-breaking change with no flag or version

terms.smell: A change that breaks a consumer — a removed endpoint, a new required request field, an incompatible message format — ships all-at-once, coupled into the deploy with no flag.
terms.why: A release removes an endpoint (or adds a required field) that mobile clients still call; the moment it deploys every old client breaks, and because it's coupled into the deploy you can't turn it off without another full release.
terms.fix: Keep the change backward-compatible (additive/versioned — cross-ref backend-review), or gate it behind a feature flag you can ramp 1% → 100% and instantly disable, keeping the old path live until the new one is proven.

trips: // ship removed endpoint / new required field, no flag → all clients break on deploy
clean:
if (flags.enabled("new-checkout-flow", { userId })) return newCheckout(req);
return legacyCheckout(req);

terms.title: A budget/cost alert (and anomaly detection) guards against runaway spend

signals: new infra provisioned with no budget alert / cost anomaly detection

terms.smell: New infra is provisioned with no budget alert and no cost-anomaly detection, so spend is silent until the invoice.
terms.why: A forgotten staging cluster (or a NAT-gateway-heavy data path) runs all month and the first anyone hears of it is a cloud bill thousands over expected — money already spent, unrecoverable.
terms.fix: Add a budget with threshold alerts (e.g. at 80% and 100%) and cost-anomaly detection so a spike is caught on day two instead of in next month's invoice; tag resources so the cost is attributable when it fires.

trips: # provision new infra, no budget alert, no anomaly detection

terms.title: Build caches aren't shared across trust levels; integrity re-verified

signals: actions/cache with a key derived from untrusted PR input in a fork-triggered job
the same cache key written by a pull_request job and read by a deploy job

terms.smell: A build cache is writable (or keyed) by untrusted input and later restored by a trusted, secret-bearing job — e.g. a fork PR that edits `package-lock.json` controls the cache key the deploy job reads.
terms.why: A forked PR seeds the dependency cache with a tampered `node_modules`; the next trusted job restores that cache and runs the planted code with deploy credentials. A cache writable by untrusted code is code writable by untrusted users.
terms.fix: Separate cache scopes per trust level; in untrusted PR builds use a read-only `cache/restore` or a distinct key namespace so a fork can't populate the trusted cache. Re-verify integrity (`npm ci` from a checked-in lockfile) rather than trusting restored binaries.

trips: # fork PR job writes: actions/cache@v4 key: deps-${{ hashFiles('package-lock.json') }}
clean: - uses: actions/cache/restore@v4   # restore only in untrusted PR builds

terms.title: CI job/build container images pinned to an exact version plus digest

signals: container: node:latest or an unpinned tool/service image in a CI job
a build image referenced by a floating tag (:3, :20, :latest)

terms.smell: A CI job runs in a container pinned to a moving tag — `container: node:latest`, `python:3` — so the image can change underneath the pipeline.
terms.why: A release built green on Monday fails Wednesday because `node:latest` rolled to a new major with a breaking change — and you can't rebuild last week's passing artifact to bisect, because the tag no longer points at the same image. The pipeline isn't reproducible.
terms.fix: Pin the job/build image to an exact version plus a digest, and bump it deliberately through review.

example.before: container: node:latest
example.after: container: node:20.11.1-bookworm@sha256:abc123...

terms.title: New component ships alerts on its golden signals, each with a `for:` and a runbook

signals: a new service/component deployed with no alert rules added
an alert with no `for:` or no runbook link

terms.smell: A new component ships with no alert rules on its golden signals (latency, traffic, errors, saturation), or alerts without a `for:` duration and a runbook link.
terms.why: A new webhook processor starts silently dropping events a week after launch; because no alert shipped with it, nobody notices until a partner asks why their callbacks stopped — days of lost events.
terms.fix: Ship alerts with the component on its golden signals, each with a `for:` (to avoid flapping) and a runbook link, so its failures page someone instead of surprising a customer.

example.after:
- alert: WebhookProcessorErrorRate
  expr: rate(webhook_processed_total{status="error"}[5m]) / rate(webhook_processed_total[5m]) > 0.05
  for: 5m
  annotations: { runbook: "https://.../runbooks/webhooks" }

trips: # new payment-webhook-processor deployed, no alert rules added

terms.title: A dashboard covers latency/traffic/errors/saturation for the new component

signals: a new component with no dashboard / no standard golden-signal panels

terms.smell: A new component ships with scattered logs and one ad-hoc metric but no dashboard covering the four golden signals.
terms.why: During an incident the on-call has no dashboard for the new service, so the first 15 minutes are spent writing Prometheus queries by hand instead of diagnosing — time the outage runs while they tool up.
terms.fix: Ship a golden-signals dashboard with the component: latency (p50/p95/p99), traffic (req/s by route/status), errors (rate by cause), saturation (queue depth, pool util, CPU/mem vs limits).

trips: # new service has scattered logs and one ad-hoc metric, no dashboard
clean: # dashboard: latency p50/p95/p99, req/s, error rate, saturation

terms.title: Component IAM/service-account role is least-privilege, bound via workload identity

signals: a component's task/pod role attached AdministratorAccess or a broad managed policy
a component authenticated with a static key rather than workload identity

terms.smell: A component's IAM/service-account role is over-broad (AdministratorAccess "to ship") or bound via a long-lived static key rather than workload identity.
terms.why: A service given admin, then hit by an SSRF or a leaked task-role credential, lets an attacker assume that role and — because it's admin — read every bucket and delete every database in the account. A full breach from one service bug.
terms.fix: Scope the role to the specific actions on the specific resources the component touches, and bind it via workload identity (IRSA on EKS, Workload Identity on GKE, managed identity on Azure) so there's no long-lived key to leak.

example.before: managed_policy_arns = ["arn:aws:iam::aws:policy/AdministratorAccess"]

terms.title: One immutable image; environment-specific config injected at runtime, not baked in

signals: ENV API_URL=... or a per-env value hardcoded in a Dockerfile
a different image built per environment

terms.smell: Environment-specific config is compiled into the image (`ENV API_URL=https://api.staging...`, `ENV LOG_LEVEL=debug`), so you need a different image per environment.
terms.why: The staging API URL is baked in, so the artifact validated in staging can't be promoted to prod as-is — a separate prod build is made, differs in some untested way, and that untested image is what breaks.
terms.fix: Build one immutable image and inject all environment-specific config at runtime (ConfigMap / Secret / env), so the exact artifact you tested is the one that ships (12-factor config).

example.before:
ENV API_URL=https://api.staging.example.com
ENV LOG_LEVEL=debug
example.after:
# image carries no per-env config; orchestrator injects it:
# envFrom: [{ configMapRef: { name: api-config } }, { secretRef: { name: api-secrets } }]

terms.title: Container declares a HEALTHCHECK (or relies on pod probes)

signals: a Dockerfile/compose service with no HEALTHCHECK and no orchestrator probe

terms.smell: A container image (used outside K8s, e.g. Compose/plain Docker) declares no `HEALTHCHECK`, so the orchestrator can't tell a hung process from a healthy one.
terms.why: With no `HEALTHCHECK` a wedged process keeps receiving traffic — the platform has no signal to restart or deregister it.
terms.fix: Declare a `HEALTHCHECK` that exercises a real endpoint. In Kubernetes, prefer pod liveness / readiness probes instead (see the probes term).

example.after:
HEALTHCHECK --interval=30s --timeout=3s \
  CMD curl -fsS http://localhost:8080/healthz || exit 1

trips:
FROM node:20-slim
CMD ["node","server.js"]   # no HEALTHCHECK, run under Compose

terms.title: COPY over ADD in Dockerfiles

signals: ADD used where COPY would do
ADD <url> fetching a remote resource, or ADD auto-extracting a tarball

terms.smell: A Dockerfile uses `ADD` where `COPY` would do — especially `ADD <url>` (fetches a remote resource) or `ADD file.tar` (auto-extracts).
terms.why: `ADD <url>` silently re-downloads a remote script whose contents can change between builds (non-reproducible, a supply-chain surface), and `ADD`'s implicit tar extraction is a surprise. `COPY` is explicit and local-only.
terms.fix: Use `COPY` for local files. If you genuinely need a remote fetch, do it with an explicit `curl`/`wget` + checksum verification, not `ADD <url>`.

example.before: ADD https://example.com/installer.sh /tmp/
example.after: COPY package*.json ./

terms.title: Dependencies expressed by direct resource references, not data lookups that race

signals: a data source that enumerates resources this same apply is creating
count/for_each driven by a data lookup rather than a resource reference

terms.smell: A `data` source reads something another resource in the same run is still creating (enumerating instances by tag, then driving `count` off the result), so on a fresh apply it races.
terms.why: A `data` source enumerating instances by tag runs before those instances exist, returns zero on a fresh apply, so the load-balancer attachments aren't made — the new environment comes up with nothing wired to the LB and serves 503s.
terms.fix: Reference the resource's attributes directly (which Terraform tracks as a dependency) instead of a `data` lookup of things you create in the same run; use `depends_on` only when a dependency is genuinely implicit.

trips: count = length(data.aws_instances.web.ids)   # data source races on fresh apply

terms.title: concurrency serializes deploys to one environment so they can't overlap

signals: a deploy job on push to main with no concurrency: group
cancel-in-progress: true on a deploy (rather than a CI build)

terms.smell: A deploy workflow has no `concurrency` group, so two runs racing to the same environment can interleave — or a `cancel-in-progress: true` that would kill a deploy mid-`apply`.
terms.why: Two PRs merge a minute apart; both deploy jobs run; the slower (older) one finishes last and overwrites prod with the previous artifact — a silent rollback nobody ordered, leaving prod half-old/half-new.
terms.fix: Add a `concurrency: { group: deploy-<env>, cancel-in-progress: false }` so one deploy per environment runs at a time and the next queues. Use `cancel-in-progress: false` for deploys (don't kill a half-finished rollout); `true` is only right for CI builds.

trips:
jobs:
  deploy: { steps: [ run: ./deploy.sh prod ] }   # no concurrency

terms.title: Dockerfile drops to a non-root USER

signals: a Dockerfile with no USER instruction (process runs as UID 0)
CMD/ENTRYPOINT that runs as root

terms.smell: A Dockerfile never sets a non-root `USER`, so the container process runs as root (UID 0).
terms.why: An RCE in the app gives the attacker root inside the container; from there a container-escape CVE (they appear regularly) makes them root on the node and then the cluster. A non-root user turns the same RCE into a much smaller foothold.
terms.fix: Create and switch to a fixed non-root UID (`USER 10001`), and enforce it again in the pod `securityContext` (runAsNonRoot).

example.before:
FROM node:20
COPY . /app
CMD ["node", "server.js"]
example.after:
FROM node:20.11.1-slim
RUN useradd -r -u 10001 app
COPY --chown=app:app . .
USER 10001
CMD ["node", "server.js"]

trips:
FROM node:20
COPY . /app
CMD ["node", "server.js"]   # no USER

terms.title: .dockerignore excludes .git/.env/secrets from the build context

signals: COPY . . with no .dockerignore in the build context
no .dockerignore excluding .git/.env/node_modules

terms.smell: A Dockerfile does `COPY . .` with no `.dockerignore`, so the build context sweeps in `.git`, `.env`, `node_modules`, and key files.
terms.why: `COPY . .` pulls `.git` (full history, possibly old secrets) and a local `.env` into the image; anyone with pull access can extract them. A leak that ships in the artifact.
terms.fix: Add a `.dockerignore` excluding `.git`, `.env*`, `node_modules`, `*.pem`, `dist`, `coverage`, and other junk/secrets, so an accidental `COPY . .` can't include them.

example.before:
# no .dockerignore
COPY . .
example.after:
# .dockerignore: .git, .env*, node_modules, *.pem, dist, coverage
COPY . .

trips: COPY . .   # and no .dockerignore in the repo

terms.title: Drift detected in CI (plan -detailed-exitcode); IaC is the single writer

signals: no scheduled drift check; infra changed out-of-band in the console
ignore_changes used to paper over a manual change rather than reconcile it

terms.smell: No scheduled `terraform plan -detailed-exitcode` drift check, or `ignore_changes` used to hide an out-of-band console change rather than reconcile it into code.
terms.why: An on-call engineer scales up a database in the console to survive a spike; days later a routine `terraform apply` of an unrelated change quietly reverts it to the small class, and the outage returns at the worst time.
terms.fix: Run scheduled `plan -detailed-exitcode` (exit 2 = drift) to surface drift and alert; treat IaC as the single source of truth and reconcile real changes back into code rather than hiding them with `ignore_changes`.

trips: lifecycle { ignore_changes = [instance_class] }   # papering over a console change
clean: - run: terraform plan -detailed-exitcode   # exit 2 = drift → fail/alert

terms.title: for_each keyed by identity instead of count where element add/remove matters

signals: count = length(var.list) with indexing by count.index over an identity-bearing list
resources keyed positionally where the list can have middle elements removed

terms.smell: A resource uses `count` indexed by position over an identity-bearing list (users, DNS records, queues), where removing or reordering a middle element shifts every later index.
terms.why: An ops change drops one name from the middle of a `count`-based list; because everything after it shifts index, Terraform destroys and recreates a dozen unrelated stateful resources, causing an outage.
terms.fix: Use `for_each` keyed by a stable identity (a set/map) so adding/removing one element only affects that element. Reserve `count` for "N identical, fungible" or on/off toggles.

terms.title: Force-replace of a stateful resource is caught; prevent_destroy guards it

signals: a change to a force-new attribute on a stateful resource (db, disk, volume, bucket)
plan shows `# forces replacement` or `-/+ destroy and then create`
renaming identifier/name, changing availability_zone/engine/kms_key on a database

terms.smell: A diff changes a force-new attribute on a resource that holds data (RDS, EBS volume, disk, bucket) — a renamed `identifier`, a changed `availability_zone`/`kms_key` — so the plan shows `-/+ destroy and then create`, and the resource has no `prevent_destroy`.
terms.why: Terraform destroys the old resource and creates a new empty one. For a database that is irreversible data loss: a "cleanup" PR that renames the RDS identifier deletes the production database and stands up an empty one — and unless a final snapshot existed, the data is gone.
terms.fix: Read every `# forces replacement` in the plan; modify in place instead of replacing where possible; put `lifecycle { prevent_destroy = true }` on every stateful resource so a mistaken replace fails closed; ensure deletion protection / final snapshots are on.

example.before: resource "aws_db_instance" "db" { identifier = "prod-db-v2" }   # rename forces REPLACE

trips: # plan: aws_db_instance.db must be replaced  -/+ destroy and then create

terms.title: No pull_request_target checks out and runs fork code with secrets in scope

signals: workflow triggered on pull_request_target (or an equivalent privileged trigger)
a CI job checks out github.event.pull_request.head and then runs build/test scripts
a fork-triggered job that can read secrets or has a write token

terms.smell: A workflow runs on pull_request_target (base-repo context, secrets + write token in scope) and checks out the PR head SHA/ref, then runs the fork's code — npm install, make, a test script — that can read ${{ secrets.* }}.
terms.why: A stranger opens a fork PR; your workflow checks out their branch and runs it with secrets reachable. A postinstall script POSTs every key — cloud creds, npm/signing tokens — to the attacker. You must rotate every secret and assume prod was breached (the tj-actions class of supply-chain incident).
terms.fix: Build untrusted PRs on `pull_request` (fork PRs get no secrets, a read-only token). If you must use pull_request_target (e.g. to label), do NOT check out the head and keep the job to trusted code only. Gate any privileged follow-up behind a separate workflow_run / approved environment.

example.before:
on: pull_request_target
jobs:
  test:
    steps:
      - uses: actions/checkout@v4
        with: { ref: ${{ github.event.pull_request.head.sha }} }
      - run: npm install && npm test
example.after:
on: pull_request            # fork PRs get NO secrets, read-only token
jobs:
  test:
    permissions: { contents: read }
    steps:
      - uses: actions/checkout@v4
      - run: npm ci && npm test

trips:
on: pull_request_target
    steps:
      - uses: actions/checkout@v4
        with: { ref: ${{ github.event.pull_request.head.sha }} }
on: [pull_request_target]  # then: git checkout $HEAD_REF && make test
clean:
on: pull_request
    permissions: { contents: read }
    steps:
      - uses: actions/checkout@v4
      - run: npm ci && npm test
on: pull_request_target  # labels only, no checkout of head

terms.title: App handles SIGTERM — fails readiness, drains in-flight work, then exits

signals: no SIGTERM handler; process exits immediately on termination
no terminationGracePeriodSeconds / preStop hook on the pod

terms.smell: The process has no `SIGTERM` handler (exits hard on termination) or the pod has no `terminationGracePeriodSeconds`/`preStop`, so in-flight requests and unfinished work are dropped on every rollout, scale-down, or node drain.
terms.why: Every deploy produces a spike of 502s because pods are killed mid-request; users see errors during what should be a zero-downtime rollout, and queue consumers lose the message they were processing.
terms.fix: On `SIGTERM` fail readiness (deregister from the LB), stop accepting new work, drain in-flight requests, then exit; set `terminationGracePeriodSeconds` and a `preStop` sleep long enough for the LB to deregister first.

trips: // no SIGTERM handler → process dies mid-request on every rollout
clean: process.on("SIGTERM", async () => { readiness.fail(); await server.close(); process.exit(0); })

terms.title: Promotion gated on golden-signal metrics; a failed gate triggers automatic rollback

signals: a rollout that promotes on a timer or on 'pods Running' rather than metrics
no analysis/metric check between canary steps; no auto-rollback

terms.smell: A rollout promotes to the next stage on a timer (or on "pods are Running") rather than on error-rate/latency metrics, and has no automatic rollback on a failed gate.
terms.why: A canary at 5% is clearly erroring, but the rollout promotes on a fixed timer, advancing to 50% then 100% — turning a contained 5% problem into a full outage because nothing checked the metrics between steps.
terms.fix: Gate every promotion on golden-signal metrics (error rate < 1%, p99 < target) and configure automatic rollback on a failed gate, so a bad canary reverts itself before a human is paged.

example.after:
analysis:
  metrics:
    - name: error-rate
      successCondition: result < 0.01
    - name: p99-latency
      successCondition: result < 0.5
# failure → automatic rollback

trips: # deploy → wait 60s → promote to 100%   (no metric check)
clean: analysis: { metrics: [{ name: error-rate, successCondition: result < 0.01 }] }

terms.title: No privileged/hostPath/hostNetwork/hostPID on app workloads

signals: securityContext.privileged: true
hostPath volume, hostNetwork: true, or hostPID: true on an app workload

terms.smell: An app workload requests host-level access — `privileged: true`, a `hostPath` mount, `hostNetwork`/`hostPID: true` — breaking the container boundary.
terms.why: A `privileged: true` app pod "to make a sysctl work" is effectively root on the node, so a later compromise of that pod is a compromise of the node and every pod on it — full cluster takeover from one app bug. `hostPath: /` mounted read-write lets a pod rewrite kubelet config or read other tenants' data.
terms.fix: Remove host access from app workloads. Reserve it for genuine node agents (CSI/CNI/monitoring), scope `hostPath` to a single specific path (read-only where possible) with explicit justification, and block it for app workloads via policy.

example.after:
# app workloads: none of the above.
# node agent only: hostPath scoped to /var/run/agent.sock, type: Socket

clean: hostPath: { path: /var/run/agent.sock, type: Socket }   # scoped node agent

terms.title: IAM policies scope Action and Resource — no "*"/"*" admin grants

signals: an IAM policy with Action = "*" and/or Resource = "*"
AdministratorAccess attached to a workload/app role

terms.smell: An IAM policy grants `"Action": "*"` on `"Resource": "*"` (or attaches AdministratorAccess) — effectively admin — to a workload or app role.
terms.why: An app role is granted `*:*` "to ship faster"; later its credentials leak (a logged token, an SSRF to the metadata endpoint), and the attacker can read every bucket, delete every database, and create IAM users. Least-privilege at the IaC layer caps the blast radius of any future leak.
terms.fix: Scope both `Action` and `Resource` to exactly what the workload needs — specific verbs on specific ARNs. Avoid `*` in either.

example.before: policy = jsonencode({ Statement = [{ Effect = "Allow", Action = "*", Resource = "*" }]})

terms.title: Deploy scripts are idempotent and set -euo pipefail — a partial failure can be re-run

signals: kubectl create / aws s3 mb style commands that fail 'already exists' on re-run
a deploy script without set -euo pipefail

terms.smell: A deploy script uses create-style commands that fail on re-run (`kubectl create configmap`, `aws s3 mb`) or lacks `set -euo pipefail`, so a partial failure leaves a broken in-between state.
terms.why: A deploy script dies at step 7 of 12 on a transient timeout; re-running aborts at step 2 with "configmap already exists," so the environment is stuck half-deployed and someone has to hand-unwind it under pressure.
terms.fix: Make steps idempotent (`kubectl apply`, `||`-guards, upserts) and add `set -euo pipefail` so a partial failure can simply be re-run to completion.

example.before:
kubectl create configmap app-config --from-file=config/
aws s3 mb s3://app-assets
example.after:
set -euo pipefail
kubectl apply -f config/
aws s3api head-bucket --bucket app-assets || aws s3 mb s3://app-assets

terms.title: Image scanning runs in CI and fails the build on high-severity CVEs

signals: an image build pipeline with no vulnerability scan step
a scan that reports but never fails the build

terms.smell: The image build pipeline has no vulnerability scan (Trivy/Grype/Snyk), or a scan that reports without failing on high-severity CVEs.
terms.why: A base or dependency with a known critical CVE ships to prod because nothing gated on it — the vulnerability is now running in production and nobody was told.
terms.fix: Add an image scan in CI that fails the build on high/critical CVEs (with a documented allowlist for accepted findings), so vulnerable images can't be promoted.

trips: - run: docker build -t app .   # no scan step anywhere in the pipeline
clean: - run: trivy image --exit-code 1 --severity HIGH,CRITICAL app:${{ github.sha }}

terms.title: No single deploy fuses code + schema + infra into one irreversible step

signals: a deploy that drops a table AND ships new code AND deletes old infra together
a destructive step fused with the code change that would need it rolled back

terms.smell: One deploy couples a destructive schema/infra change with the code change — `DROP old table` + new app version + delete old infra in a single irreversible step.
terms.why: A deploy drops the old table and ships new code in one shot; the new code has a bug but you can't roll back to the old version because it needs the table you just dropped — you're trapped, forced to roll forward under maximum pressure.
terms.fix: Keep each deploy individually reversible and push the one irreversible action (drop, delete, destroy) to a separate, later step that runs only once the previous version is provably retired.

example.before: deploy: DROP old table + new app version + delete old infra   (rollback impossible)
example.after:
1. additive schema (expand) — reversible
2. new code working with both old+new schema — reversible
3. ...soak, verify...
4. destructive cleanup (contract / delete) — after rollback no longer needed

trips: # deploy: DROP old table + new app version + delete old infra
clean: # deploy A: expand (reversible); deploy C later: drop old column (contract)

terms.title: K8s manifest references the image by digest, not :latest

signals: image: repo/app:latest in a manifest
an image tag with no @sha256 digest

terms.smell: A K8s manifest references its container image by a moving tag (`image: app:latest`) instead of an immutable digest.
terms.why: `:latest` means a pod restart or reschedule can silently pull a different image than the one you deployed and tested, so what's running in prod isn't what you shipped — and a rollback can't reliably re-pull the previous bytes.
terms.fix: Reference the image by digest (`app@sha256:...`) so the running image is exactly the one that was built, tested, and promoted.

example.before: image: registry/app:latest
example.after: image: registry/app@sha256:abc123...

terms.title: K8s secrets referenced via secretKeyRef from a real store, not plaintext in the manifest

signals: an env var with a literal secret value in a manifest or ConfigMap
DB_PASSWORD / API_KEY hardcoded in a Deployment/values.yaml

terms.smell: A secret value is placed directly in a manifest (or ConfigMap) as `value: "Pa55w0rd!"` instead of referenced via `secretKeyRef`.
terms.why: A `DB_PASSWORD: "..."` literal is committed in a manifest; now it's in git history forever and printed by `kubectl describe pod`, so anyone with repo or namespace read has the prod DB password.
terms.fix: Reference a `Secret` via `valueFrom.secretKeyRef`; populate that Secret from a real manager (External Secrets Operator, Sealed Secrets, Secrets Store CSI from Vault/SSM) and enable etcd encryption-at-rest.

example.before:
env:
  - name: DB_PASSWORD
    value: "Pa55w0rd!"

clean:
- name: DB_PASSWORD
    valueFrom: { secretKeyRef: { name: db-creds, key: password } }

terms.title: Risky new behavior has a kill switch that defaults safe and flips without a deploy

signals: a new risky code path always on, with no flag to disable it
disabling the path would require a rollback or hotfix

terms.smell: A new risky code path is always on with no kill switch, so the only way to disable it is a rollback or a hotfix.
terms.why: A new pricing path computes wrong totals under an edge case discovered in prod; with no flag, disabling it means rolling back a deploy that also contained five unrelated good changes — so you either revert all of it or ship a hotfix under pressure.
terms.fix: Guard the risky path behind a flag that defaults safe (off) and can be flipped in seconds without a deploy, read consistently across instances.

example.before: return runExperimentalPricing(cart);

terms.title: Multi-stage build; runtime stage is minimal/distroless, no build toolchain

signals: a single-stage Dockerfile that builds and runs in the same image
the runtime image ships compilers/dev deps/source

terms.smell: A single-stage Dockerfile ships the full build toolchain (compilers, dev deps, source) in the runtime image instead of a thin runtime stage.
terms.why: A 900 MB image carrying gcc, git, package managers, and source ships to prod; when one dependency has an RCE, the attacker has a full toolchain to pivot with — and pull times and registry bills are larger for it.
terms.fix: Use a multi-stage build: compile in a fat build stage, copy only the artifact into a minimal / distroless final stage (no shell, non-root), cutting the image to the binary plus its runtime.

example.before:
FROM golang:1.22
COPY . .
RUN go build -o app .
CMD ["./app"]
example.after:
FROM golang:1.22 AS build
RUN CGO_ENABLED=0 go build -o /app .
FROM gcr.io/distroless/static:nonroot
COPY --from=build /app /app
USER nonroot
ENTRYPOINT ["/app"]

trips:
FROM golang:1.22
RUN go build -o app .
CMD ["./app"]
clean:
FROM golang:1.22 AS build
...
FROM gcr.io/distroless/static:nonroot
COPY --from=build /app /app

terms.title: A default-deny NetworkPolicy exists; ingress/egress opened only as needed

signals: a namespace/workload with no NetworkPolicy (flat default network)
a new service with no ingress/egress restriction

terms.smell: A workload/namespace has no `NetworkPolicy`, so the default flat network model lets any pod reach any pod and all egress.
terms.why: With no NetworkPolicy, a breached front-end pod can reach the database and every internal service directly — lateral movement is unrestricted, so one compromised pod exposes the whole namespace.
terms.fix: Apply a default-deny `NetworkPolicy` (Ingress + Egress) and open only the specific ingress and egress the workload needs.

example.after:
kind: NetworkPolicy
spec:
  podSelector: { matchLabels: { app: api } }
  policyTypes: [Ingress, Egress]
  ingress: [{ from: [{ podSelector: { matchLabels: { app: gateway } } }] }]

trips: # Deployment with no NetworkPolicy in the namespace
clean:
kind: NetworkPolicy
spec: { podSelector: {...}, policyTypes: [Ingress, Egress] }

terms.title: CI authenticates to cloud via short-lived OIDC, not long-lived static keys

signals: configure-aws-credentials (or gcloud/az) fed static access keys from secrets
AWS_ACCESS_KEY_ID / AWS_SECRET_ACCESS_KEY stored as CI secrets

terms.smell: CI authenticates to a cloud provider with long-lived static keys stored in secrets (`aws-access-key-id: ${{ secrets.AWS_ACCESS_KEY_ID }}`) instead of federated OIDC.
terms.why: A static key never expires and is copyable; one leak — from a log, a fork, a compromised laptop — gives an attacker the account until a human notices and rotates, which can be weeks. OIDC issues credentials that live minutes and are bound to a specific repo/branch/environment, so a leak is near-worthless.
terms.fix: Federate CI to the cloud with OIDC (`permissions: id-token: write`, `role-to-assume`). GitLab `id_tokens`, Azure, and GCP support the same workload-identity-federation pattern. Reserve static keys for systems that genuinely can't do OIDC.

terms.title: An owner, SLO, and runbook exist; alerts route to the owning on-call rotation

signals: a new service with no documented owner / SLO / runbook
alerts that route to nobody or a rotation that's never seen the service

terms.smell: A new component ships with no documented owner, no SLO, and no runbook, and its alerts route to nobody (or a rotation unfamiliar with it).
terms.why: An alert fires for the new service at 3am, pages a rotation that's never seen it, and there's no runbook — so the responder spends the outage reverse-engineering the system instead of mitigating.
terms.fix: Define the owner, an SLO (so "is this bad?" has an answer), and a runbook (symptoms → diagnosis → mitigation → rollback), and route every alert to the owning on-call rotation with its runbook attached.

trips: # new service deployed; no SLO, no runbook, alerts route to nobody

terms.title: Config and credentials are per-environment; non-prod never holds prod creds or data

signals: one DATABASE_URL / credential shared across staging and prod
a non-prod environment pointing at prod data or holding prod creds

terms.smell: One config or credential is shared across environments — a `DATABASE_URL` that points staging and prod at the same database, or prod creds present in a non-prod environment.
terms.why: Staging and prod share a `DATABASE_URL`, so a load test or a destructive integration test run against "staging" wipes production data — because they were never actually separate.
terms.fix: Keep config and credentials strictly per-environment; a non-prod environment must not hold prod credentials or point at prod data.

example.before: DATABASE_URL points staging AND prod at the same database
example.after:
config/staging.yaml → staging DB, staging keys
config/prod.yaml    → prod DB, prod keys   (never shared)

clean: # staging.yaml → staging DB;  prod.yaml → prod DB;  never shared

terms.title: Deploy credentials are separate per environment

signals: one secret / one role used to deploy to both staging and prod
a shared deploy credential across environments

terms.smell: A single deploy credential (secret, role, service account) is used across environments, so a staging pipeline holds the keys to prod.
terms.why: A leak or misuse in the lower-trust staging path grants access to production, and a compromised staging job can deploy to prod. Environments that share a credential aren't actually isolated.
terms.fix: Give each environment its own scoped deploy credential/role (ideally per-environment OIDC subjects), so staging can only touch staging and a lower-env compromise can't reach prod.

trips: role-to-assume: arn:aws:iam::123:role/deploy   # same role for staging AND prod

terms.title: Dockerfile base image pinned to an exact version plus digest, no :latest

signals: FROM image:latest or a floating major tag
a base image with no @sha256 digest

terms.smell: A Dockerfile pins its base to a moving tag — `FROM python:latest`, `FROM python:3` — with no digest, so the build isn't reproducible and you can't tell which base you shipped.
terms.why: `:latest` rolls to a new base with a regression or a new CVE and your next build ships it without anyone choosing to; and when a scanner flags "the python base," you can't tell which image is affected because the tag has moved many times.
terms.fix: Pin to an exact version plus an immutable digest (`FROM python:3.12.4-slim@sha256:...`) and bump it deliberately through review.

example.before: FROM python:latest
example.after: FROM python:3.12.4-slim@sha256:abc123...

terms.title: Providers, modules, and required_version pinned; lockfile committed

signals: required_providers with no version constraint
a module source with no version pin (floating latest)
.terraform.lock.hcl absent / not committed

terms.smell: Providers or modules have no version constraint, or `.terraform.lock.hcl` isn't committed — so `init` can pull a new major with breaking or destructive behavior changes.
terms.why: `terraform init` on a teammate's machine pulls AWS provider v6 whose changed defaults rewrite a security group or rename an attribute, and the next apply proposes destructive churn nobody intended.
terms.fix: Pin `required_version`, provider `version` constraints, and module `version`s; commit `.terraform.lock.hcl` so every machine and CI run resolves the same, verified versions.

example.before:
terraform { required_providers { aws = { source = "hashicorp/aws" } } }
module "vpc" { source = "terraform-aws-modules/vpc/aws" }

terms.title: CI runs plan on the PR; apply is gated and applies the saved plan, not auto-approve

signals: terraform apply -auto-approve on push/merge
no plan step posted on the PR before apply

terms.smell: The pipeline runs `terraform apply -auto-approve` on push/merge, or applies a freshly computed plan rather than the exact plan that was reviewed on the PR.
terms.why: The plan is the review artifact — it shows `# forces replacement` before it happens. A refactor that looks innocuous in HCL can force-replace a stateful resource; with `-auto-approve` it executes before anyone sees the diff, and prod is gone.
terms.fix: Run `terraform plan -out=tfplan` on the PR and post it for review; on merge, in a gated/approved environment, run `terraform apply tfplan` — the exact saved plan, so what was reviewed is what runs.

example.before: - run: terraform apply -auto-approve
example.after:
# PR: terraform plan -out=tfplan → post to PR
# merge (gated env): terraform apply tfplan

terms.title: Liveness + readiness (+ startup) probes set; liveness has no downstream dependency

signals: a container spec with no readinessProbe / livenessProbe
a liveness probe that checks a downstream dependency
a slow-booting app with no startupProbe

terms.smell: A K8s container has no readiness/liveness probes, or a liveness probe that depends on a downstream service, or a slow-booting app with no startup probe.
terms.why: With no readiness probe, the Service sends traffic before the app is ready and users get 500s; with no liveness probe a deadlocked pod serves errors forever because nothing restarts it. A liveness probe that fails because a downstream is down crash-loops the whole fleet.
terms.fix: Add a readiness probe (traffic gate, may check dependencies), a cheap dependency-free liveness probe (restart), and a startup probe for slow boots so liveness doesn't kill them mid-start.

trips:
containers:
  - name: api
    image: api@sha256:...   # no probes
livenessProbe: { httpGet: { path: /db-check } }   # liveness checks a downstream
clean:
readinessProbe: { httpGet: { path: /readyz } }
livenessProbe: { httpGet: { path: /livez } }

terms.title: A post-deploy smoke test exercises critical paths and can trigger rollback

signals: a deploy declared successful the moment pods are Running
no post-deploy check that hits a real request path

terms.smell: A deploy is declared "done" the moment pods are Running, with no smoke test exercising a real request path in prod.
terms.why: A deploy succeeds at the orchestration level — all pods Running — but a misconfigured env var means the app can't reach its database, and nobody notices until customer reports roll in, because nothing actually exercised a request after the deploy.
terms.fix: Run a handful of post-deploy smoke checks against the critical paths; a non-zero exit triggers the same automatic rollback as the health gate.

example.after:
curl -fsS https://api.prod/healthz
curl -fsS -X POST https://api.prod/v1/echo -d '{"ping":1}' | grep -q '"pong"'
# non-zero exit → trigger rollback

trips: # rollout complete → mark deploy successful   (no smoke test)

terms.title: Production deploys sit behind a required approval/environment gate, fail-closed

signals: a deploy-prod job triggered on push to main with no environment: gate
a required check marked continue-on-error, or a deploy with no `needs:`

terms.smell: A push/merge to main runs a prod deploy with no human/automated gate — no `environment: production`, no required `needs: [test, scan]` — or a security gate that is `continue-on-error` (fails open).
terms.why: A contributor merges a PR that wasn't meant for release and it deploys to prod in seconds; the only "rollback" is another panicked deploy. A compromised account or a fat-fingered branch ships instantly and irreversibly.
terms.fix: Gate prod behind a protected `environment: production` (reviewers / wait timer / branch rule) and `needs:` the required checks so they must pass first. Make gates fail-closed — never `continue-on-error` a security/test gate. Pair with branch protection.

example.before:
on: { push: { branches: [main] } }
jobs:
  deploy-prod:
    steps: [ run: ./deploy.sh prod ]
example.after:
jobs:
  deploy-prod:
    environment: production
    needs: [test, security-scan]
    steps: [ run: ./deploy.sh prod ]

trips:
on: { push: { branches: [main] } }
jobs:
  deploy-prod:
    steps: [ run: ./deploy.sh prod ]
clean:
jobs:
  deploy-prod:
    environment: production
    needs: [test, security-scan]

terms.title: Risky/user-facing changes roll out progressively (canary/blue-green), not big-bang

signals: a deploy that shifts 100% of traffic to the new version at once
a risky user-facing change with no canary/blue-green step

terms.smell: A risky or user-facing change ships to 100% of traffic at once, with no canary/blue-green staging.
terms.why: A release with a memory leak (or a bad query) goes to 100% instantly; within minutes every pod is degraded and all users are affected before the on-call even acknowledges the page. The blast radius is the entire user base before the first alert.
terms.fix: Roll out progressively (canary 5% → 25% → 50% → 100% with metric checks between steps, or blue-green) using Argo Rollouts / Flagger, matching the strategy to the risk.

example.after:
strategy:
  canary:
    steps:
      - setWeight: 5
      - analysis: { templates: [{ templateName: error-rate-and-latency }] }
      - setWeight: 50

trips: # deploy new version → 100% of traffic immediately

terms.title: ServiceAccount RBAC is a namespaced least-privilege Role, not cluster-admin

signals: a ClusterRoleBinding granting cluster-admin to a workload ServiceAccount
a broad ClusterRole where a namespaced Role would do

terms.smell: A workload's ServiceAccount is bound to `cluster-admin` (or a broad ClusterRole) when it needs only a few verbs in its own namespace.
terms.why: An app pod bound to `cluster-admin` to read one ConfigMap is, once compromised, a token to create pods, read every Secret, and pivot across namespaces — full cluster compromise from one app bug.
terms.fix: Grant a namespaced `Role` with exactly the verbs/resources the workload needs, bound with a RoleBinding to its ServiceAccount.

example.after:
kind: Role
rules: [{ apiGroups: [""], resources: ["configmaps"], verbs: ["get","list"] }]

trips:
roleRef: { kind: ClusterRole, name: cluster-admin }
subjects: [{ kind: ServiceAccount, name: api }]

terms.title: Terraform state uses a remote backend with locking and encryption at rest

signals: no backend block (local state), or an s3 backend without lock table / encrypt
terraform.tfstate committed to the repo

terms.smell: Terraform state is local (no backend block) or a remote backend without locking or encryption — an S3 backend with no lock table and no `encrypt = true`.
terms.why: Two engineers `apply` at once with no lock; the writes interleave and corrupt the state file, so Terraform's view of reality is wrong and the next plan proposes to destroy resources that actually exist. Unencrypted state (which holds RDS passwords and generated keys) in a stolen laptop or public bucket is a secret breach.
terms.fix: Use a remote backend with locking (DynamoDB table, or native S3 lockfile on newer Terraform) and `encrypt = true` with a KMS key. Never commit `terraform.tfstate`.

terms.title: K8s container sets both resources.requests and resources.limits for cpu and memory

signals: a container spec with no resources block
requests set but limits missing (or vice versa)

terms.smell: A K8s container has no `resources.requests` and `resources.limits` (or only one) for cpu and memory, so it's scheduled blindly and uncapped.
terms.why: A memory leak or big request makes one pod balloon; with no `limits` it grows until the node runs out of memory and the kernel OOM-killer reaps other tenants' pods — one bad deploy takes down unrelated services (the noisy-neighbor outage).
terms.fix: Set `requests` (scheduling floor) and `limits` (hard ceiling) for cpu and memory; keep the limit realistic so healthy traffic isn't throttled/OOM'd.

example.before:
containers:
  - name: api
    image: api@sha256:...

trips:
containers:
  - name: api
    image: api@sha256:...   # no resources block

terms.title: Resources tagged for owner/env/cost-center, ideally via provider default_tags

signals: a resource created with no tags
no provider default_tags baseline

terms.smell: Resources are created with no (or inconsistent) tags — no owner, env, or cost-center — and no provider `default_tags` baseline.
terms.why: A cost spike appears and nobody can tell which team or service owns the runaway fleet because the instances are untagged; or an orphaned, untagged resource runs for months unnoticed on the bill. Tags are the guardrail for cost attribution, ownership, and incident response.
terms.fix: Set `default_tags` on the provider (team/env/managed_by/cost_center) so every resource inherits the baseline; add resource-specific tags where needed.

example.before: resource "aws_instance" "worker" { ami = "..." instance_type = "m5.large" }

terms.title: Reusable/3rd-party workflows are pinned and passed only the secrets they need

signals: uses: org/repo/.github/workflows/x.yml@main — a floating reusable-workflow ref
secrets: inherit passed to a reusable workflow

terms.smell: A reusable workflow is called by a moving ref (`@main`) and/or handed `secrets: inherit`, which runs their code in your context with all of your secrets.
terms.why: `secrets: inherit` against a workflow you don't control hands every secret to code that can change under you. When that upstream repo is compromised (or its `@main` re-pointed), the attacker gets your full secret set on your next run.
terms.fix: Pin the reusable-workflow ref to a SHA and pass secrets explicitly and minimally, so the blast radius is one secret, not all of them.

example.before:
uses: some-org/ci/.github/workflows/deploy.yml@main
secrets: inherit

terms.title: A tested rollback path exists — one action, not "roll forward"

signals: a deploy that replaces prod with no kept previous version
the recovery plan is 'roll forward with a fix'
a one-way migration/deploy with no documented revert

terms.smell: A change to prod has no tested way back: the previous version isn't kept deployable, or the only documented recovery is "roll forward with a fix."
terms.why: A deploy introduces a subtle bug that only shows at prod scale; the plan is to roll forward, so the outage lasts as long as it takes to diagnose, code, review, and re-deploy — an hour of downtime a one-command rollback would have ended in 30 seconds.
terms.fix: Keep the last-known-good artifact deployable and make rollback a single, tested runbook action (`kubectl rollout undo`, re-point to the previous image tag). A change whose rollback path is "roll forward" is a blocker.

example.before: # deploy = build new image → replace prod → done   (no kept previous version)
example.after: kubectl rollout undo deployment/api    # tested revert to last-known-good

trips: # rollback plan: we'll roll forward with a fix
clean: kubectl rollout undo deployment/api   # documented, tested

terms.title: Bounded rolling-update strategy plus a PodDisruptionBudget

signals: a Deployment with no rolling-update bounds and no PodDisruptionBudget
a service that must stay available with no PDB

terms.smell: A Deployment has no bounded rolling-update strategy and no `PodDisruptionBudget`, so a deploy or a node drain can take down all replicas at once.
terms.why: A cluster autoscaler scales down (or an admin drains a node for patching) and, with no PDB, evicts all three replicas of a service that happened to land on that node — instant outage during a routine, planned operation.
terms.fix: Set a rolling-update strategy that keeps capacity (`maxUnavailable: 0, maxSurge: 1`) and add a `PodDisruptionBudget` (`minAvailable`) so voluntary disruptions (drains, autoscaler) keep enough pods up.

trips: kind: Deployment   # default surge, and no PodDisruptionBudget anywhere

terms.title: No secrets committed or in plaintext config; all secrets sourced from a manager

signals: a password/api key literal in a committed values.yaml / ConfigMap / .env
a secret value in version control rather than a reference

terms.smell: A secret is committed in the repo — in a `values.yaml`, a `ConfigMap`, or a plaintext `.env` — rather than referenced from a manager.
terms.why: An API key in a committed `values.yaml` is readable by everyone with repo access and permanently in git history; even after removal it must be rotated because the old commit still exposes it.
terms.fix: Source every secret from a dedicated manager (Vault, AWS Secrets Manager/SSM, GCP Secret Manager, Sealed Secrets, External Secrets Operator) and keep only references in the repo; CI/runtime fetches the value at deploy time. Detection/rotation depth → security-review.

example.before:
database:
  password: "Pa55w0rd!"
apiKey: "sk_live_abc123..."
example.after:
database:
  passwordSecretRef: prod/db-password   # resolved by External Secrets / Vault / SSM

terms.title: No secrets baked into image layers via ARG/COPY; build secrets via BuildKit mount

signals: ARG for a token/key followed by a RUN that uses it
COPY .env (or other secret files) into the image

terms.smell: A build-time secret enters an image layer — `ARG NPM_TOKEN` used in a `RUN`, or `COPY .env /app/.env` — where it persists in image history even after a later step "removes" it.
terms.why: A build does `ARG GITHUB_TOKEN` / `COPY .env` to install private deps; the token is now in a layer of every image pushed to your registry, extractable via `docker history` by anyone with pull access — a leak that survives deleting the line because it lives in an earlier layer.
terms.fix: Use a BuildKit secret mount (`RUN --mount=type=secret,id=...`) for build-time secrets so they exist only during that RUN; inject runtime secrets from the orchestrator; never COPY secret files (add a `.dockerignore`).

example.before:
ARG NPM_TOKEN
RUN npm config set //registry.npmjs.org/:_authToken=$NPM_TOKEN
COPY .env /app/.env
example.after:
RUN --mount=type=secret,id=npm_token \
    NPM_TOKEN=$(cat /run/secrets/npm_token) npm ci

trips:
ARG NPM_TOKEN
RUN npm config set //registry.npmjs.org/:_authToken=$NPM_TOKEN

terms.title: No secrets in committed tfvars or hardcoded into resources; state is access-controlled

signals: a password/key/token literal in a committed *.tfvars or *.tf
a secret hardcoded in a resource argument (db password, api key)

terms.smell: A secret is committed in a `*.tfvars` file or hardcoded in a resource — `db_password = "Pa55w0rd!"` — landing in git and, for many resources, in state in cleartext.
terms.why: A `prod.tfvars` with the DB password is readable by everyone with repo access and lives in history forever; even after removal it must be rotated. The same value also lands in state, so unencrypted state in a public bucket is a second exposure.
terms.fix: Gitignore `*.tfvars`; source real secrets from a manager (Secrets Manager/SSM/Vault); and because anything in state is sensitive, encrypt state and restrict who can read it.

example.before: resource "aws_db_instance" "db" { password = "Pa55w0rd!" }

terms.title: Pod securityContext hardened — runAsNonRoot, no privilege escalation, read-only rootfs, drop ALL

signals: a workload with no securityContext
missing runAsNonRoot / allowPrivilegeEscalation:false / readOnlyRootFilesystem / drop ALL

terms.smell: A K8s workload has no (or a permissive) `securityContext`: it may run as root, can escalate privileges, has the default capability set, and a writable root filesystem.
terms.why: An exploited pod running as root with default caps and a writable filesystem lets the attacker install tools, write a cron persistence hook, and probe the node — all things `runAsNonRoot`, `drop: ["ALL"]`, and `readOnlyRootFilesystem` would have blocked or hugely slowed.
terms.fix: Set a restrictive `securityContext` on every workload (`runAsNonRoot`, `allowPrivilegeEscalation: false`, `readOnlyRootFilesystem: true` with emptyDir for writable paths, `capabilities.drop: ["ALL"]`, `seccompProfile: RuntimeDefault`) and enforce cluster-wide via Pod Security Admission.

trips:
containers:
  - name: api
    image: api@sha256:...   # no securityContext
clean:
securityContext:
  runAsNonRoot: true
  allowPrivilegeEscalation: false
  readOnlyRootFilesystem: true
  capabilities: { drop: ["ALL"] }

terms.title: Workflow permissions set explicitly with a read-only floor, write granted per-job

signals: a workflow with no top-level permissions: block
GITHUB_TOKEN used with default write-all scope

terms.smell: A workflow has no `permissions:` block, so the default token is read/write to everything in the repo, and every step (and dependency) inherits write-all.
terms.why: A build step pulls a compromised npm dependency; because the token is write-all, the malware pushes a backdoor commit to main and publishes a poisoned package on your authority. Scoping to `contents: read` would have contained the blast to a failed build.
terms.fix: Set `permissions: { contents: read }` as a repo-wide floor and widen only per-job where a step genuinely needs write (e.g. `contents: write` to push a tag, `packages: write` to publish).

trips:
jobs:
  build:
    steps: [ ... ]   # no permissions block anywhere

terms.title: No Activity/View/Context held in a static, singleton, or delayed callback

signals: an Activity/View/Context stored in an object/companion/singleton
a non-static inner Handler/Runnable/listener posting delayed work not removed in onDestroy

terms.smell: An Activity, View, or Context is held in a static/singleton/long-lived object, or a non-static inner Handler/Runnable/listener posting delayed work is never removed in onDestroy.
terms.why: A static reference pins the Activity (and its whole view tree, ~MBs) after it's destroyed — every rotation leaks another copy until OutOfMemoryError. A delayed Runnable keeps the destroyed Activity alive for the length of its delay.
terms.fix: Use applicationContext for anything long-lived; never store an Activity statically; removeCallbacksAndMessages(null) in onDestroy, or use a WeakReference / lifecycle-scoped coroutine.

example.before: object Cache { var ctx: Context? = null }; Cache.ctx = this // pins the Activity
example.after: Cache.ctx = applicationContext // outlives any single Activity safely

terms.title: Coroutines scoped to the lifecycle, not GlobalScope

signals: GlobalScope.launch { ... }
a coroutine that outlives the screen and is never cancelled

terms.smell: Work is launched in GlobalScope (or an unscoped custom scope) instead of viewModelScope/lifecycleScope.
terms.why: GlobalScope work keeps running after the user leaves — it may touch a dead view, leak the scope, and waste battery/network. Lifecycle scopes cancel automatically when the ViewModel clears or the lifecycle ends.
terms.fix: Launch in viewModelScope / lifecycleScope (or a scope tied to the component's lifetime) so it cancels with the screen.

example.before: GlobalScope.launch { repo.sync() } // outlives the screen; never cancelled
example.after: viewModelScope.launch { repo.sync() } // cancelled when the ViewModel clears

terms.title: R8/ProGuard keep rules present for reflection-based libraries

signals: adding a Gson/Moshi model or reflection-based lib without keep rules
minifyEnabled true with models that get obfuscated/stripped

terms.smell: A reflection-based library (Gson/Moshi models, anything using reflection) is added under minifyEnabled without a keep rule.
terms.why: R8 strips/renames the classes the library reflects on, so parsing crashes only in the minified release build — it works in debug and blows up in production for real users.
terms.fix: Add keep rules for reflection-based classes/models; verify a release build; and keep minifyEnabled true for the size win.

terms.title: App download size isn't regressed by the change

signals: bundling uncompressed assets or multiple image scales you don't need
a new heavy dependency; no resource shrinking / app thinning

terms.smell: Uncompressed assets, redundant image scales, an unused/heavy dependency, or no resource shrinking added by the change.
terms.why: A large download deters installs and is dropped over cellular; oversized binaries slow update adoption, and some markets cap or warn on size.
terms.fix: Ship App Bundles / app thinning (per-device assets), compress/convert images (WebP/HEIC), enable resource shrinking (R8 shrinkResources), and audit the dependency tree for dead weight.

terms.title: Back navigation does the expected thing and doesn't pile up screens

signals: push/navigate called in a loop or from a notification/handler each time
hardware back / predictive back / swipe-back not handled

terms.smell: Repeated push of the same route, or a back handler (hardware back, predictive back, iOS swipe-back) that doesn't do the expected thing, leaving duplicate screens stacking on the back stack.
terms.why: Dozens of identical screens accumulate — memory grows and the back button takes forever to escape the flow. Ignoring Android hardware/predictive back or fighting iOS swipe-back produces a screen that feels broken on that platform.
terms.fix: Use navigate (which dedupes by route+params) or popToTop/reset when re-entering a flow; handle the platform back gesture explicitly on each OS.

terms.title: Backgrounding pauses work and persists drafts; foreground refreshes stale data

signals: camera/socket/timer kept alive with no AppState / scenePhase / onStop handling
no draft persistence or stale-data refresh on foreground

terms.smell: No handling of the background/foreground transition (RN AppState, iOS scenePhase, Android onStop/onStart) — expensive work keeps running when backgrounded and stale data is shown on return.
terms.why: A backgrounded screen holding a camera/socket/timer drains battery and can crash; iOS may suspend JS so long-running work won't finish; and the user sees stale data or loses an unsaved draft when the app is killed while backgrounded.
terms.fix: On background, pause expensive work and persist drafts; on foreground, refresh data if stale. Wire AppState / scenePhase / onStop+onStart.

example.before: // socket + polling stay live in the background

terms.title: No tight polling, wakelocks, or always-on GPS/sensors

signals: setInterval(fetch, 3000), a short-interval location poll kept on permanently
PARTIAL_WAKE_LOCK, continuous high-accuracy GPS, always-listening sensor

terms.smell: Tight polling (short setInterval), a held wakelock, or continuous high-accuracy GPS / always-listening sensor kept on to "stay fresh".
terms.why: Tight polling keeps the radio awake (its tail energy is significant per wakeup); continuous GPS is one of the heaviest costs on the device. The OS battery screen names your app as a top drainer and users uninstall.
terms.fix: Prefer push (FCM/APNs) over polling; coalesce and widen intervals and stop when backgrounded; use geofencing / significant-location-change over continuous GPS; unregister sensors when off-screen.

terms.title: Threading across the native boundary is explicit; callbacks marshal to the right thread

signals: a native callback touching UI without marshaling to the main thread
heavy native work run on the UI thread; code assuming a thread it isn't on

terms.smell: A native callback touches the UI without hopping to the main thread, or heavy native work runs on the UI thread, or code assumes a thread it isn't actually on.
terms.why: Touching UI off the main thread is undefined (crash/garble); heavy native work on the UI thread janks or ANRs. The failure reproduces only under certain timing.
terms.fix: Marshal native callbacks explicitly back to the right thread (main for UI), and run heavy native work off the UI thread.

terms.title: Caches have a TTL/staleness story; sensitive cached values are encrypted

signals: a cache with no expiry / fetchedAt stamp
tokens or PII cached in plaintext

terms.smell: A cache with no TTL/fetchedAt that serves stale data forever, or tokens/PII cached in plaintext.
terms.why: An unbounded cache shows wrong prices, balances, or content indefinitely; plaintext- cached secrets are exfiltratable from a backup or a rooted device.
terms.fix: Stamp entries with a TTL/fetchedAt, show staleness or revalidate (stale-while-revalidate), and encrypt sensitive cached values with a Keychain/Keystore-backed key. (Crypto depth → security-review.)

terms.title: CancellationException is re-thrown, never swallowed

signals: catch (e: Exception) that also swallows CancellationException
a long loop with no isActive/ensureActive check

terms.smell: A broad catch (e: Exception) swallows CancellationException, or a long loop never checks isActive/ensureActive.
terms.why: Swallowing CancellationException breaks cooperative cancellation — the coroutine keeps running after its scope is cancelled, leaking work, wasting battery, and corrupting state that assumed the coroutine stopped.
terms.fix: Re-throw CancellationException before handling other exceptions; make long loops cooperatively cancellable with isActive/ensureActive().

example.before: try { work() } catch (e: Exception) { handle(e) } // swallows cancellation
example.after: try { work() } catch (e: CancellationException) { throw e } catch (e: Exception) { handle(e) }

terms.title: Cold-start TTI isn't regressed; non-critical init is deferred

signals: eager SDK init in Application.onCreate / AppDelegate / RN root
large synchronous reads before first paint

terms.smell: SDKs are initialized eagerly (analytics/crash/ads) or heavy synchronous work runs in Application.onCreate / AppDelegate / the RN root before first paint.
terms.why: A long white/splash screen on launch — the metric users and the app stores notice. Every eagerly-initialized SDK adds to it.
terms.fix: Defer non-critical init (lazy / App Startup library / post-first-frame), parallelize what you can, and measure cold-start TTI on a low-end device — not a flagship.

terms.title: Combine cancellables stored; Tasks cancelled on view disappear

signals: a .sink whose AnyCancellable is discarded
a long Task {} started in onAppear and never cancelled

terms.smell: A Combine sink's AnyCancellable is discarded (not stored), or a long-running Task {} started in onAppear is never cancelled.
terms.why: An unstored cancellable is torn down immediately (the pipeline never runs) or leaks and keeps firing; an orphaned Task keeps fetching after the user left — wasted battery/network and a @MainActor write to a gone view.
terms.fix: Store cancellables in a set (its deinit cancels); use .task {} (auto-cancels on disappear) or hold the Task and cancel() in onDisappear; check Task.isCancelled in loops.

example.before: publisher.sink { self.update($0) } // cancelled at once or leaked
example.after:
publisher.receive(on: DispatchQueue.main)
  .sink { [weak self] in self?.update($0) }.store(in: &cancellables)

terms.title: Compose params are stable; remember/derivedStateOf used; state read low

signals: a composable taking a plain List/lambda Compose can't prove stable
re-sorting/re-allocating in the composable body every recomposition
a fast-changing state (scroll offset) read high in the tree

terms.smell: A composable takes an unstable param (plain List, unstable class), recomputes/ re-allocates in its body every recomposition, or reads a fast-changing state too high in the tree.
terms.why: An unstable param makes Compose conservatively recompose the composable and its children on every parent recomposition; re-sorting every frame burns the main thread; a high read of scroll offset or text input invalidates the whole subtree — jank on a busy screen.
terms.fix: Use stable/immutable types (ImmutableList, @Immutable/@Stable) and hoist lambdas; wrap derived work in remember(key)/derivedStateOf; push the state read down to the smallest composable that needs it; put side effects in LaunchedEffect/DisposableEffect, not the body.

example.before: val sorted = items.sortedBy { it.name } // re-sorts every recomposition
example.after: val sorted = remember(items) { items.sortedBy { it.name } }

terms.title: Animations run on the compositor, not per-frame on the app/JS thread

signals: Animated with useNativeDriver: false, or JS setting layout props every frame
animation driven by main-thread state on each tick

terms.smell: An animation is driven by setting layout/state properties from the JS or main thread every frame (e.g. useNativeDriver: false) instead of on the compositor.
terms.why: The animation stutters the instant the driving thread gets busy — a fetch resolving, a list re-rendering. Compositor-driven transform/opacity animations keep running independently of app-thread work.
terms.fix: Run it on the compositor/render thread — useNativeDriver: true / Reanimated (RN), Core Animation / withAnimation (iOS), animate*AsState/Animatable (Compose) — animating transform/opacity. Layout animation needs Reanimated.

example.before: Animated.timing(opacity, { toValue: 1, duration: 300 }).start(); // JS per frame
example.after: Animated.timing(opacity, { toValue: 1, duration: 300, useNativeDriver: true }).start();

terms.title: Config changes don't crash or lose in-progress input

signals: form/editor state lost on rotation, dark mode, font scale, locale, or split-screen change
fixed point sizes or hardcoded layout that assumes one configuration

terms.smell: In-progress input or view state is lost, or the layout crashes, when a configuration changes — rotation, dark mode, font scale, locale, or split-screen.
terms.why: Android recreates the Activity on a config change; if state isn't retained the user's half-typed form vanishes, and layouts built for one configuration clip, crash, or overlap under another.
terms.fix: Retain state across recreation (ViewModel / rememberSaveable / state restoration), use scalable units, and test rotation, large font scale, dark mode, and split-screen.

terms.title: Sync conflict resolution is explicit, not accidental last-write-wins

signals: an update sends the whole record with no base version / updatedAt
no conflict detection between two devices editing the same record

terms.smell: An update PUTs the record with no base version or updatedAt, so whoever syncs last silently overwrites — last-write-wins by accident, not by choice.
terms.why: Two devices (or two sessions) touching the same record clobber each other with no error — the user's edits vanish silently, the worst kind of data loss because nothing signals it.
terms.fix: Pick a strategy on purpose: server-authoritative (send base version/updatedAt, server rejects a stale write with 409, client reconciles), documented LWW only for low-stakes single fields, or merge/CRDT for collaborative data. Detect conflicts via version/updatedAt.

example.before: api.update(doc); // clobbers any change since you read
example.after: api.update(doc, { ifMatchVersion: doc.version }); // 409 → reload, merge, retry

terms.title: Connectivity events are drain hints; success is decided by the response

signals: sync gated purely on a NetInfo/NWPathMonitor/ConnectivityManager boolean
a write marked sent because 'connected' was true, not because the response arrived

terms.smell: Sync is gated purely on a connectivity boolean, and a write is marked sent because the device reported "connected" rather than because the response actually arrived.
terms.why: "Connected" doesn't mean the internet works — captive portal, no DNS, server down. Fire-on-event-and-forget marks writes as sent when they weren't, losing data.
terms.fix: Treat connectivity events as hints to attempt a drain; decide success from the actual response. The durable-queue + idempotency-key model tolerates false "connected" signals.

terms.title: Core Data accessed via perform; object IDs crossed between threads, not objects

signals: an NSManagedObject used on a different queue than its context
the view context used on a background queue; heavy fetch/save on the view context

terms.smell: An NSManagedObject is passed between threads, or the view context is used on a background queue, instead of perform/performBackgroundTask with NSManagedObjectIDs crossing threads.
terms.why: Core Data is not thread-safe — cross-thread access corrupts the store or crashes intermittently (the worst kind: reproduces only under load). Heavy fetches/saves on the view context also jank the UI.
terms.fix: Use perform/performBackgroundTask and pass NSManagedObjectIDs across threads, then re-fetch with existingObject(with:). Enable -com.apple.CoreData.ConcurrencyDebug 1 to catch violations.

example.before: DispatchQueue.global().async { obj.title = "x" } // obj belongs to another context

terms.title: Deep links reset the stack to a valid, restorable screen

signals: deep link / universal link / push handler appends onto whatever stack exists
navigate/push from a link instead of reset

terms.smell: A deep link, universal link, or push-notification handler navigates by appending onto whatever stack happens to exist rather than resetting to a known stack.
terms.why: The user lands with an unpredictable or broken back stack, or on a screen that can't restore its params after process death — back goes somewhere nonsensical or the screen is blank on relaunch from the link.
terms.fix: Reset the navigation stack to a known, valid, restorable state for the linked screen; pass an id (not a heavy object) and read the record from the store.

terms.title: Background work uses deferrable scheduling that respects OS limits

signals: a foreground Service, plain Timer, or long Task expected to run/sync after backgrounding
background work that ignores Doze/App Standby or iOS suspension

terms.smell: Guaranteed/deferrable background work (upload, sync) rides on a foreground Service, a plain Timer, or a long Task, expecting it to keep running after the app backgrounds.
terms.why: iOS suspends a backgrounded app within seconds and Android defers background jobs under Doze/App Standby — a plain timer won't fire and the work silently doesn't run, so the feature appears broken.
