Holding a wakelock or an unjustified foreground service to fight the limits drains battery and risks store rejection.
terms.fix: Use WorkManager (Android) with constraints + backoff, or BGTaskScheduler / BGAppRefreshTask (iOS) with an expiration handler; design for "runs eventually," not "runs now."

example.before: GlobalScope.launch { repo.upload() } // killed on process death, ignores Doze
example.after:
val req = OneTimeWorkRequestBuilder<SyncWorker>()
  .setConstraints(Constraints.Builder().setRequiredNetworkType(CONNECTED).build())
  .setBackoffCriteria(BackoffPolicy.EXPONENTIAL, 10, SECONDS).build()
WorkManager.getInstance(ctx).enqueue(req)

terms.title: Sync is delta-based with a persisted cursor and resumable

signals: re-pulls the whole dataset on every launch/sync
no since/cursor token, no paginated resumable sync

terms.smell: Sync pulls "everything" on launch or re-pulls the whole dataset each time, with no cursor/since token and no persisted progress.
terms.why: A full re-pull is slow, data-hungry, and likely to be interrupted mid-way — then it restarts from zero. On cellular it burns the user's data and battery.
terms.fix: Sync deltas with a cursor/since token, paginate, and persist the cursor so an interrupted sync resumes. (Server cursor semantics → backend-review.)

terms.title: In-flight mutations persist to a durable queue before the network call

signals: a mutating network call (placeOrder, send, upload, create) is awaited with nothing persisted first
retry/queue state held only in memory

terms.smell: A user-initiated mutation calls the network directly (or retries in memory) without first persisting the intent to durable on-disk storage.
terms.why: iOS/Android kill backgrounded apps freely and the user can swipe-kill at any moment. If the app dies while the request is in flight, the write is silently lost — the order the user thinks they placed never happened, the message never sent. An in-memory retry vanishes with the process.
terms.fix: Persist the mutation to a durable queue BEFORE the network call, then drain the queue (delete on success). On next launch / connectivity, resume draining so it survives a kill.

example.before: await api.placeOrder(cart); // lost if the app dies mid-request
example.after:
await queue.enqueue({ type: 'placeOrder', cart, idempotencyKey }); // durable first
drainQueue(); // pops, calls api, deletes on success

trips: for (let i=0;i<3;i++){ try { await api.save(x); return } catch {} }
clean: await queue.enqueue({ type:'placeOrder', cart, idempotencyKey }); drainQueue();

terms.title: Dynamic Type / font scaling respected — no clipping at large sizes

signals: fixed point/px font sizes instead of text styles / scalable units (sp)
fixed-height text containers that clip at large accessibility font sizes

terms.smell: Text uses fixed point/px sizes (not text styles / sp) or sits in fixed-height containers, so it clips or truncates at large accessibility font sizes.
terms.why: Low-vision users who raise the system font size get clipped, truncated, or overlapping text — the content is literally unreadable for the people who most need the larger size.
terms.fix: Use text styles / scalable units (sp, Dynamic Type), let containers grow, and test at the largest accessibility font setting.

terms.title: Feature flags default safe; a forced-update / kill-switch path exists

signals: a new flag that defaults ON, or fails open when config can't load
no remote kill-switch or forced-update path for a bad release

terms.smell: A new feature flag defaults on (or fails open when config can't load), or there's no remote kill-switch / forced-update path for a bad release.
terms.why: A flag that fails on ships an unfinished feature to everyone the moment config fails; with no kill-switch a broken native release can only be fixed by a multi-day store review cycle while users crash.
terms.fix: Default flags to the safe/off state and fail closed; ensure a remote kill-switch and a forced-update path exist to disable or gate a bad release.

terms.title: Frame budget met; jank measured with tools, not eyeballed on a flagship

signals: overdraw / deep view hierarchies, layout thrash, un-virtualized lists
performance claims verified only by eye on a high-end device

terms.smell: Work per frame risks exceeding the budget (~16ms at 60Hz, ~8ms at 120Hz) — overdraw, deep hierarchies, layout thrash, main-thread IO, un-virtualized lists, JS-thread animation — and smoothness is judged by eye on a flagship.
terms.why: Scrolling and transitions don't feel smooth even though nothing crashes; the hitch only shows on the mid/low-end devices the reviewer didn't test.
terms.fix: Measure dropped frames with platform tools — Android Profile GPU Rendering / Perfetto / JankStats, Xcode Core Animation FPS / hitch ratio, RN perf monitor — on a representative device, and cut overdraw / main-thread work.

terms.title: Replayable mutations carry a stable idempotency key

signals: a POST that charges, orders, or messages is retried or drained from a queue
no idempotency key generated once per logical operation

terms.smell: A retried or queue-replayed mutation (charge, order, message) is sent with no stable dedupe key, or a fresh key is generated per attempt.
terms.why: Networks make "did it succeed?" ambiguous — a request can succeed while the response is lost, so the client retries and the action happens twice: double charge, duplicate order, double message. A stable key lets the server collapse duplicates.
terms.fix: Generate one idempotency key when the operation is created, persist it with the queued op, and reuse it across every retry. (Server-side enforcement → backend-review.)

example.before: await api.charge(amount); // retry after a lost response → double charge
example.after:
const key = op.idempotencyKey ?? uuid(); // once per op, persisted
await api.charge(amount, { idempotencyKey: key });

terms.title: Images are downsampled to display size and cached

signals: a full-resolution remote/asset image loaded into a thumbnail-sized view
no image cache lib on a list of images

terms.smell: A full-resolution image is decoded into a thumbnail-sized view, or list images have no caching strategy.
terms.why: Each full-res bitmap is tens of MB; a scrolling photo grid spikes memory to OOM-termination. With no cache the same image re-downloads and re-decodes on every scroll — draining data and battery.
terms.fix: Request/decode at display size and cache decoded bitmaps — Glide/Coil (Android), Nuke/UIImage thumbnailing (iOS), expo-image/FastImage (RN); set explicit dimensions.

example.before: imageView.load("https://.../photo-4032x3024.jpg") // full res into a 64dp view
example.after: imageView.load(url) { size(Size(128,128)); memoryCachePolicy(ENABLED) }

terms.title: iOS privacy manifest present with required-reason API declarations

signals: using a required-reason API (file timestamps, UserDefaults, disk space, etc.) with no PrivacyInfo.xcprivacy
a new SDK that requires a privacy manifest entry

terms.smell: A required-reason API or tracking SDK is used without the corresponding PrivacyInfo.xcprivacy privacy-manifest declaration.
terms.why: Apple rejects (or warns and later rejects) builds that use required-reason APIs without a declared reason in the privacy manifest — the release is blocked from the store.
terms.fix: Add PrivacyInfo.xcprivacy declaring the required-reason API categories and data collection; include manifests for third-party SDKs that require them.

terms.title: Escaping closures capture [weak self]; delegates are weak

signals: an escaping closure (network completion, Combine sink, timer, stored handler) using self. without [weak self]
var delegate declared strong; [unowned self] on a closure that can outlive self

terms.smell: An escaping closure (network completion, Combine sink, timer, stored handler) captures self strongly, a delegate is declared strong, or [unowned self] is used on a closure that can outlive self.
terms.why: ARC never frees the cycle — the view controller / view model and its image caches and observers leak; revisit the screen a few times and the app is jettisoned for memory. [unowned self] after dealloc is a use-after-free crash, not a leak.
terms.fix: Capture [weak self] and guard let self { return }; declare delegates weak var; use unowned only when self's lifetime strictly bounds the closure.

example.before: service.fetch { result in self.items = result } // service retains closure retains self
example.after: service.fetch { [weak self] result in guard let self else { return }; self.items = result }

terms.title: Permissions requested just-in-time with rationale; denial degrades gracefully

signals: calling a guarded API (camera.open, location) assuming the permission is granted
a permission requested up-front at launch with no rationale; no denial handling

terms.smell: A guarded API is called assuming the permission is granted, or a permission is requested up front with no rationale, and denial isn't handled.
terms.why: An ungranted permission throws (SecurityException / crash) or leaves a dead screen; on Android 11+ repeated denial becomes permanent, so re-prompting does nothing. Speculative up-front prompts also get denied and risk store rejection.
terms.fix: Request at the moment the feature is used, with a rationale; on denial degrade, explain, and route to Settings — never crash or dead-end.

example.before: camera.open() // SecurityException if CAMERA not granted

terms.title: Each declared background mode is genuinely used and justified

signals: a UIBackgroundModes entry (location, audio, fetch) with no matching feature
a background mode added to keep a timer/service alive

terms.smell: A background mode is declared (UIBackgroundModes: location/audio/fetch, a foreground service) that no genuine feature uses, or is added just to keep work alive.
terms.why: Declaring a background mode you don't genuinely use is an App Store rejection; an unjustified always-on background mode also drains the battery the OS then blames on your app.
terms.fix: Declare only background modes a real, justified feature needs; remove any added to circumvent suspension limits.

terms.title: Work started on appear/resume is stopped in the matching teardown

signals: starts a camera, sensor, location, socket, timer, or observer in onResume/viewWillAppear/onAppear/useFocusEffect
no matching stop in onPause/viewWillDisappear/onDisappear/cleanup

terms.smell: A resource (camera, sensor, location session, socket, timer, observer) is started in a lifecycle-enter callback with no symmetric stop in the matching exit callback.
terms.why: The resource keeps running while the screen is off-screen — the camera/GPS drains battery, the observer leaks the view, and a callback may touch a gone view and crash. On iOS onAppear/onDisappear can fire more than once and aren't symmetric with allocation, so "init once in onAppear" is wrong too.
terms.fix: Pair every start with a stop in the matching teardown (onDisappear / viewWillDisappear / onPause+onStop / useFocusEffect cleanup). On Android prefer a lifecycle-aware observer or repeatOnLifecycle so it auto-stops.

example.before: override fun onResume() { locationManager.requestUpdates(listener) }
example.after:
override fun onResume() { locationManager.requestUpdates(listener) }
override fun onPause() { locationManager.removeUpdates(listener) }

terms.title: Lists use stable item keys, never the array index

signals: keyExtractor returning String(index), or no keyExtractor on non-id data
LazyColumn items() with no key; RecyclerView without stable IDs

terms.smell: A list uses the array index as the item key (or no key at all) — keyExtractor returning the index, LazyColumn items() without a key, RecyclerView without stable IDs.
terms.why: On reorder/filter/insert/delete the framework reuses the wrong row component: input focus, selection, swipe, and expanded state jump to the wrong item — a correctness bug, not just a perf one. Scroll position also drifts.
terms.fix: Key by a stable id — keyExtractor={i => i.id}, items(rows, key = { it.id }), setHasStableIds(true)+getItemId with DiffUtil.

example.before: keyExtractor={(_, i) => String(i)}
example.after: keyExtractor={item => item.id}

terms.title: Long lists are virtualized/recycled, not fully mounted

signals: ScrollView + items.map, a manual for-loop adding views, a non-recycling list
a large array rendered without FlatList/FlashList/LazyColumn/RecyclerView

terms.smell: A long list mounts every row — RN ScrollView + .map, a manual view loop, or a non-recycling list — instead of a virtualizing/recycling list.
terms.why: Every item, including the thousands off-screen, is realized: seconds of jank on mount, stutter on scroll, and ballooning memory that can OOM-terminate the app.
terms.fix: Use the platform's virtualizing list — FlatList/FlashList (RN), LazyColumn/ RecyclerView (Android), List/UICollectionView with cell reuse (iOS) — with layout hints (getItemLayout) for fixed-height rows.

example.before: <ScrollView>{items.map(i => <Row key={i.id} {...i} />)}</ScrollView>
example.after:
<FlatList data={items} keyExtractor={i => i.id}
  renderItem={({item}) => <Row {...item} />} />

terms.title: Every listener, timer, and subscription is cleaned up

signals: useEffect with addListener/setInterval/subscribe and no cleanup return
an AppState/DeviceEventEmitter/socket subscription never removed
work started on focus that only blurs (stays mounted) without useFocusEffect

terms.smell: A listener, timer, interval, or subscription is registered (useEffect, DeviceEventEmitter, AppState, socket, setInterval) without a matching cleanup, or a stacked-but-blurred screen keeps its subscriptions running.
terms.why: Handlers and intervals accumulate across mounts — duplicate handlers fire, a setInterval keeps polling and waking the radio after the screen is gone, setState on an unmounted component leaks, and N stacked-but-unfocused screens all poll, draining battery and bandwidth.
terms.fix: Return a cleanup from useEffect (sub.remove(); clearInterval(id)); use useFocusEffect so work stops on blur for screens that stay mounted in the stack.

example.before: useEffect(() => { const id = setInterval(poll, 1000); }, []); // never cleared
example.after: useEffect(() => { const id = setInterval(poll, 1000); return () => clearInterval(id); }, []);

terms.title: No network/disk/parse/decode/crypto on the main/UI/JS thread

signals: synchronous http/file/JSON/image-decode/crypto on the main thread or JS thread
prefs.commit(), getSync, blocking read in a UI callback
a 50ms+ synchronous loop on the UI thread

terms.smell: Network, disk, JSON parse, image decode, or crypto runs synchronously on the main/UI thread (the JS thread in RN), or a long (~50ms+) loop runs there without chunking.
terms.why: The main thread's only job is to render and handle input. Blocking it drops frames (jank) and, on Android, > ~5s blocked throws an ANR ("close the app?") dialog; on iOS the watchdog kills a long-frozen app.
terms.fix: Do the work off-thread (Dispatchers.IO / Task.detached / background queue / off the JS thread) and hop back to main only for the UI update; chunk or yield long loops.

example.before: viewModelScope.launch { val d = api.fetch(); state.value = d } // blocks main if fetch blocks

terms.title: No retained Activities/VCs, unbounded caches, or unreleased bitmaps

signals: a cache that only grows with no eviction/TTL
bitmaps/large buffers never released; a Fragment view held past onDestroyView

terms.smell: Leak signatures: an Activity/ViewController retained after navigation, a cache that only grows (no eviction/TTL), bitmaps/large buffers never released, or a Fragment's View held past onDestroyView.
terms.why: Memory climbs over a session until the OS jettisons the app — a "random" crash users can't reproduce on demand. Each leaked Activity is megabytes.
terms.fix: Profile with Android Studio Memory Profiler / Xcode Instruments (Allocations, Leaks) / Flipper — don't guess. Fix the GC root (static, listener, closure, coroutine, cache) LeakCanary/Instruments names; null Fragment view bindings in onDestroyView.

terms.title: Requests are batched, compressed, cached, and paginated — no per-screen fan-out

signals: a screen firing N small round-trips where one batched call would do
uncompressed, uncached, unpaginated requests

terms.smell: A screen fans out many small, uncompressed, uncached requests where one batched call would do.
terms.why: The screen is slow on cellular and burns the user's data and battery via chatty round-trips; the radio wakes for each one.
terms.fix: Batch and prefetch; compress (gzip/brotli); cache with sensible TTLs; paginate. (Server API shape and pagination semantics → backend-review.)

terms.title: No blanket ATS exception shipping plaintext HTTP

signals: NSAllowsArbitraryLoads = true in App Transport Security settings
plaintext http:// endpoints in a shipping build

terms.smell: App Transport Security is disabled wholesale (NSAllowsArbitraryLoads = true) to allow plaintext HTTP.
terms.why: A blanket ATS exception ships traffic in the clear — sniffable and tamperable on any network — and is an automatic App Store rejection.
terms.fix: Use HTTPS; if one legacy host truly needs an exception, scope it narrowly with NSExceptionDomains, never a blanket NSAllowsArbitraryLoads. (Transport security depth → security-review.)

terms.title: No private/undocumented platform APIs

signals: calling a private/undocumented API, KVC into private ivars, reflection into system internals

terms.smell: The change calls a private/undocumented platform API (or reflects/KVCs into system internals).
terms.why: Private-API use is an automatic App Store rejection and can break with no notice on the next OS update, crashing the app in the field.
terms.fix: Use only public, documented APIs; find a supported alternative for the capability.

terms.title: Every requested permission maps to a visible feature

signals: a permission declared/requested that no shipped feature uses
requesting broad permissions speculatively 'for later'

terms.smell: A permission is declared or requested that no shipped feature actually uses — asked for speculatively.
terms.why: App Review rejects permissions with no user-facing justification, and each unnecessary permission erodes user trust (and shows up in store privacy labels).
terms.fix: Request only permissions a visible feature needs, at the point it's used; remove speculative declarations.

terms.title: Reads come from a local source of truth; writes go local + queued

signals: a screen calls the network directly on load and shows a blank/error screen offline
a save that throws or is lost when there is no connection

terms.smell: A screen reads directly from the network and dead-ends (blank/error) on failure, or a save fails outright when offline, instead of reading from a local store and queuing the write.
terms.why: A network-coupled UI is unusable on the subway, in an elevator, on a plane. The user waits on connectivity or loses their action to it — for an offline-first product that's a broken app.
terms.fix: Read from the local store as the single source of truth (the network is a background reconciler); write locally and enqueue sync so the user never waits on or loses to connectivity.

example.before:
onLoad { items = api.list() }   // blank screen offline
onSave { api.create(item) }     // lost offline
example.after:
onLoad { items = db.list() }                          // always works
onSave { db.insert(item); queue.enqueue(SyncCreate(item)) }

terms.title: Optimistic updates roll back or reconcile on failure

signals: UI/local store updated optimistically before the network call
no catch that restores the previous state on failure

terms.smell: The UI or local store is updated optimistically and the network failure path doesn't undo it.
terms.why: An un-rolled-back optimistic update leaves the UI lying — it shows a state the server never accepted, so the next refresh "loses" the action and the user is confused or acts on false data.
terms.fix: Snapshot before the optimistic change; on failure restore the snapshot (or reconcile with the server) and tell the user it didn't save.

example.before: db.like(postId); api.like(postId); // fails → UI still shows liked
example.after:
const prev = db.snapshot(postId); db.like(postId);
try { await api.like(postId) } catch { db.restore(prev); toast("Couldn't save") }

terms.title: OTA updates are staged, rollback-able, and don't push native changes

signals: a CodePush/EAS OTA bundle that assumes a native module the installed binary lacks
an unstaged OTA push with no rollback path

terms.smell: An OTA update (CodePush/EAS) pushes JS that depends on a native change the installed binary can't satisfy, or is shipped unstaged with no rollback.
terms.why: A JS-only OTA that calls a missing native module crashes on launch for everyone who gets it — and without staging/rollback the whole install base is bricked at once. It can also violate store rules on what may be updated over the air.
terms.fix: Keep OTA JS-only and compatible with the shipped native binary; stage the rollout, keep a rollback, and respect store OTA rules.

terms.title: Dark mode supported; reads as HIG on iOS and Material on Android

signals: hardcoded light colors that break or wash out in dark mode
a single cross-platform UI that ignores per-OS conventions and haptics

terms.smell: Hardcoded colors that break in dark mode, or a single cross-platform compromise that ignores per-OS conventions (navigation, haptics, share sheet) and feels alien on both.
terms.why: A screen that's unreadable in dark mode, or that fights the platform idiom, feels low-quality and off-brand for the OS — users notice and reviewers only test one platform.
terms.fix: Support dark mode with semantic colors, use haptics appropriately, and keep each platform's conventions on its side (HIG on iOS, Material on Android).

terms.title: Platform navigation and gestures respected, not fought

signals: disabling or reimplementing iOS back-swipe / Android system or predictive back
a custom gesture that conflicts with the platform's own edge gestures

terms.smell: The change disables or reimplements a native navigation gesture — iOS back-swipe, Android system/predictive back — or a custom gesture fights the platform's edge gestures.
terms.why: Users rely on muscle memory; a screen that ignores hardware/predictive back or swallows the back-swipe feels broken and can trap the user in a flow.
terms.fix: Honor the platform back gesture and standard navigation; branch with Platform.OS and handle Android hardware/predictive back explicitly rather than assuming iOS behavior.

terms.title: ATT before tracking, scoped media storage, no sensitive data logged

signals: reading the IDFA / tracking a user before the ATT prompt (iOS)
broad external-storage access instead of scoped storage / READ_MEDIA_* (Android)
tokens / PII / emails written to logs

terms.smell: Tracking (IDFA) begins before the ATT prompt on iOS, broad storage access is used instead of scoped storage / READ_MEDIA_* on Android, or sensitive data (tokens, PII) is written to logs.
terms.why: Tracking without ATT and requesting more than scoped access are App Store / Play rejections and privacy violations; logged secrets are exfiltratable from logs and a privacy leak.
terms.fix: Show the ATT prompt before any tracking; use scoped storage / READ_MEDIA_* for the narrow media you need; never log tokens/PII. (Crypto/credential depth → security-review.)

terms.title: Sync retries use exponential backoff + jitter and resume on next launch

signals: a tight for-loop retry with no delay
retry count/state that does not resume after an app restart

terms.smell: The queue drain retries in a tight loop with no backoff, or retry state doesn't resume on next launch / connectivity restore.
terms.why: Tight no-backoff retries DDoS a recovering backend right when it's fragile, and drain the device battery/radio. If the retry state doesn't survive a restart the queued write never finishes after a kill.
terms.fix: Drain with exponential backoff + jitter, and persist enough state that the queue resumes draining on next launch and on connectivity restore.

terms.title: Derive values during render, don't sync them via useEffect

signals: useState + useEffect that just setState from other state/props
a derived string/list mirrored into state

terms.smell: A value computable from props/state is stored in state and kept in sync with a useEffect that just calls setState.
terms.why: The effect causes a second render on every change (flicker, wasted work) and can desync — the mirrored state briefly disagrees with its source.
terms.fix: Compute it during render; reach for useMemo only when the computation is expensive.

example.before:
const [label, setLabel] = useState('');
useEffect(() => setLabel(`${first} ${last}`), [first, last]);
example.after: const label = `${first} ${last}`;

terms.title: Navigation params are small ids, not large serialized objects

signals: navigation.navigate('Screen', { bigObject })
a full record passed as a route param

terms.smell: A large object is passed as a React Navigation route param instead of an id.
terms.why: Nav params are serialized into persisted navigation state — big params bloat state restoration and can break it, so the app fails to restore after process death.
terms.fix: Pass an id and read the record from a store/cache on the destination screen.

terms.title: Stable identities passed to memoized children; renderItem is cheap

signals: inline object/array/function literals passed to a memoized child or renderItem
data.filter(...)/() => go() created fresh each render

terms.smell: Fresh object/array/function literals are passed to memoized children (or allocated per row in renderItem) every render — {{padding:8}}, data.filter(Boolean), () => go().
terms.why: New identities defeat React.memo/PureComponent, so children re-render every time; in a list this re-renders the whole visible window on any state change — the difference between 60fps and a stutter.
terms.fix: Memoize with useMemo/useCallback and hoist a memoized row component; pass item.id, not a fresh closure.

example.before: <Header style={{ padding: 8 }} actions={data.filter(Boolean)} onTap={() => go()} />
example.after:
const style = useMemo(() => ({ padding: 8 }), []);
const onTap = useCallback(() => go(), []);

terms.title: Safe areas, notches, Dynamic Island, and camera cutouts handled

signals: content laid out under the status bar, home indicator, notch, or Dynamic Island
hardcoded top/bottom padding instead of safe-area insets

terms.smell: Content is positioned without respecting safe-area insets, so it sits under the status bar, home indicator, notch, Dynamic Island, or camera cutout.
terms.why: Controls fall under system UI and can't be tapped, or text is hidden behind the notch — the screen is visibly broken on devices with cutouts.
terms.fix: Use SafeAreaView / safeAreaInsets / WindowInsets rather than hardcoded padding, and test on a notch/Dynamic Island device.

terms.title: Every local schema change ships a tested, forward-only migration

signals: adds/renames/retypes a column, table, or entity in a local DB (Room/Core Data/SQLite/WatermelonDB)
bumps DB version without an added Migration, or uses fallbackToDestructiveMigration

terms.smell: A local schema change (new/renamed/retyped column or table) ships without an explicit migration, or falls back to a destructive migration in production.
terms.why: A missing migration crashes on launch for everyone who updates (the on-device DB version mismatches), and fallbackToDestructiveMigration silently WIPES the user's data. Users skip versions, so the migration must be forward-only and tested from the oldest supported installed version.
terms.fix: Add an explicit migration for every schema change; never destructive in prod; test upgrading across version jumps from the oldest supported version.

example.before:
Room.databaseBuilder(ctx, Db::class.java, "db")
  .fallbackToDestructiveMigration() // wipes user data on any schema bump
  .build()
example.after:
Room.databaseBuilder(ctx, Db::class.java, "db")
  .addMigrations(MIGRATION_3_4) // ALTER TABLE ...; forward-only, tested
  .build()

trips: // bumped version = 4 but no addMigrations() call

terms.title: Screen-reader labels present; decorative elements hidden; custom controls expose role/state

signals: icon-only buttons / images with no accessibilityLabel / contentDescription
custom controls that don't expose role or state to the accessibility tree

terms.smell: Interactive elements lack accessibilityLabel/contentDescription, decorative images aren't hidden from the reader, or custom controls don't expose role/state.
terms.why: A VoiceOver/TalkBack user hears "button" with no name, or the reader announces decorative noise, or a custom toggle gives no on/off state — the feature is unusable without sight.
terms.fix: Add accessibilityLabel/contentDescription to meaningful elements; mark decorative ones as hidden from accessibility; expose role and state on custom controls.

terms.title: Secrets/tokens live in Keychain/Keystore, never UserDefaults/AsyncStorage

signals: auth token / secret written to UserDefaults, plain SharedPreferences, or AsyncStorage
credentials in a plist / plaintext key-value store

terms.smell: An auth token, refresh token, or secret is written to UserDefaults, plain SharedPreferences, or AsyncStorage instead of secure storage.
terms.why: UserDefaults/SharedPreferences/AsyncStorage are unencrypted — tokens there are trivially readable from a device backup or a rooted/jailbroken device, handing an attacker the user's session.
terms.fix: Store credentials in the Keychain (iOS, with an appropriate accessibility class) / Keystore-backed EncryptedSharedPreferences (Android); on RN use a secure store (react-native-keychain). (Crypto depth → security-review.)

example.before: UserDefaults.standard.set(authToken, forKey: "token") // plaintext plist
example.after:
SecItemAdd([kSecClass: kSecClassGenericPassword, kSecAttrAccount: "authToken",
  kSecValueData: Data(token.utf8),
  kSecAttrAccessible: kSecAttrAccessibleAfterFirstUnlockThisDeviceOnly] as CFDictionary, nil)

terms.title: RN bridge/TurboModule use is sparing — no per-frame crossings or huge payloads

signals: reading a native value or calling a native module on each scroll/gesture tick
passing a multi-megabyte object / base64 image through a native module call

terms.smell: A native module is called (or a native value read) every scroll/gesture frame over the bridge, or a multi-megabyte payload is serialized across the boundary.
terms.why: The old bridge is async and serialized — each crossing is a JSON round-trip; per-frame crossings flood it, lag the gesture, and stall JS. Serializing a big payload freezes the JS thread for its duration — a frozen UI right when the data arrives.
terms.fix: Keep high-frequency work native (Reanimated worklets, Animated.event with useNativeDriver) or batch; pass a handle/URI for big data and let native read the bytes (JSI/HostObject on the New Architecture).

terms.title: Screen state is restored after OS process death

signals: in-progress input, scroll position, or a multi-step flow held only in memory
no savedInstanceState / SceneStorage / state restoration / persisted nav state

terms.smell: Screen or navigation state that a user cares about lives only in memory, with no savedInstanceState, SwiftUI SceneStorage/state restoration, or persisted navigation state.
terms.why: Android (and iOS) can kill and recreate the process at any time under memory pressure. On return the user lands on a blank or wrong screen and loses the form they were filling in, the step they reached, or the item they had selected.
terms.fix: Persist restorable state — savedInstanceState / rememberSaveable, SceneStorage or UIStateRestoration, RN's persisted navigation state — and rehydrate on recreate.

example.before: class WizardActivity : Activity() { var step = 0 } // reset to 0 after process death
example.after:
override fun onSaveInstanceState(b: Bundle) { b.putInt("step", step) }
override fun onCreate(b: Bundle?) { step = b?.getInt("step") ?: 0 }

terms.title: Local store fits the access pattern; sensitive data encrypted at rest

signals: large/queried data stuffed into a key-value blob (MMKV/DataStore/UserDefaults)
personal/financial data stored unencrypted at rest

terms.smell: Large or queried/relational data is stored in a key-value blob, or personal/ financial data is stored unencrypted at rest.
terms.why: A KV blob for queried data means loading and rewriting the whole blob per change — slow and racy. Unencrypted sensitive data at rest is readable from a device backup or a rooted/jailbroken device.
terms.fix: Use KV (MMKV/DataStore/UserDefaults) for small prefs and SQLite/Room/Core Data/ WatermelonDB for relational/large/queried data; encrypt the DB or sensitive columns (SQLCipher / Core Data encryption / Keystore-backed keys).

terms.title: @StateObject owns models; @ObservedObject only observes

signals: @ObservedObject var model = SomeModel() created inside a view
@State holding a reference type expected to trigger updates

terms.smell: A view creates its observable model with @ObservedObject var model = Model() (or holds a reference type in @State) rather than owning it with @StateObject.
terms.why: @ObservedObject = Model() builds a NEW model every time the view is reconstructed — in-progress state (loading flags, edits, network tasks) resets unexpectedly and work restarts. Mutating a class through @State won't trigger the updates you expect.
terms.fix: Own the model with @StateObject (created once, lifetime tied to the view) and pass it down as @ObservedObject; use @State only for value types. Prefer the @Observable macro (iOS 17+) to narrow invalidation.

example.before: struct CartView: View { @ObservedObject var model = CartModel() } // recreated each render
example.after: struct CartView: View { @StateObject private var model = CartModel() }

terms.title: Crash reporting is wired and symbolicated (dSYM / ProGuard mapping / source maps)

signals: crash reporter added but dSYM / mapping.txt / source maps not uploaded
Hermes/minified release with no symbolication artifacts

terms.smell: Crash reporting isn't wired, or is wired but the symbolication artifacts (dSYM, ProGuard/R8 mapping.txt, RN source maps) aren't uploaded.
terms.why: Without symbolication every production crash is an unreadable wall of a.b.c / stripped addresses — you can't tell what broke, so you can't fix the crash users are hitting.
terms.fix: Wire a crash reporter and upload the matching dSYM / mapping.txt / source maps per release so stack traces deobfuscate.

terms.title: Touch targets meet platform minimums (~44pt iOS / ~48dp Android)

signals: a tappable control smaller than 44pt/48dp
hit area smaller than the visual element

terms.smell: A tappable control is smaller than ~44pt (iOS) / ~48dp (Android), or its hit area is smaller than the visual element.
terms.why: Small targets are hard to hit — mis-taps and frustration, worse for users with motor impairments or large fingers on a small phone.
terms.fix: Size interactive controls to the platform minimum and expand the hit area (hitSlop / padding / minimumTouchTargetSize) to at least match the visual.

terms.title: Every referenced NS*UsageDescription string is provided with a clear reason

signals: requesting camera/location/photos/mic/contacts with no matching NS*UsageDescription
a placeholder or empty usage string

terms.smell: A protected resource (camera, location, photos, mic, contacts) is accessed with no matching NS*UsageDescription string, or the string is empty/placeholder.
terms.why: iOS crashes the app the instant it requests the permission with no usage string, and App Review rejects missing or meaningless purpose strings.
terms.fix: Provide every referenced NS*UsageDescription with a clear, user-facing reason for the access.

terms.title: Version and build numbers are bumped

signals: a release-affecting change with no versionCode/versionName or CFBundleVersion bump

terms.smell: A release-affecting change ships without bumping the version/build number (versionCode/versionName, CFBundleShortVersionString/CFBundleVersion).
terms.why: The store rejects a duplicate build number, and unversioned builds make crash reports and rollbacks impossible to attribute to a release.
terms.fix: Bump the version and build numbers for any change going to the store.

terms.title: Autoscaling keys on the real bottleneck signal and accounts for cold-start time

signals: CPU-based autoscaling on an IO-bound workload
a slow-warming instance (JIT, cache fill, pool) behind spiky traffic

terms.smell: Autoscaling keys on the wrong signal (CPU for an IO-bound service that saturates on connection waits) or ignores a slow cold start on spiky traffic.
terms.why: Scaling on the wrong signal means it does not kick in when needed (overload persists) or thrashes; a 60s cold start means new capacity arrives after the spike already caused errors.
terms.fix: Scale on the metric that actually saturates — queue depth / concurrency / latency for IO-bound, CPU for compute-bound — and mind warm-up: pre-warm, keep a buffer, or use a faster-starting runtime for spiky traffic.

terms.title: Backpressure and rate limiting bound intake; overload sheds instead of accepting unbounded work

signals: queue.push(job) with no bound; an expensive endpoint with no rate limit
fan-out spawning unbounded concurrent downstream calls

terms.smell: Work is accepted without bound — an unbounded queue push, an expensive endpoint with no rate limit, or fan-out with no concurrency cap.
terms.why: Without backpressure, a traffic spike or slow downstream grows in-flight/queued work without limit -> memory climbs -> OOM -> the instance dies -> its load shifts to the others -> they OOM too. An unbounded queue converts a brief overload into a cascading outage.
terms.fix: Bound intake and shed when full: rate-limit per client (shared store), bounded queues/channels with 503 + Retry-After when full, and bounded concurrency (semaphore/worker pool) on fan-out.

example.before: queue.push(job); // no bound
example.after: if (queue.length >= MAX_QUEUE) return res.status(503).set('Retry-After', '5').end();

terms.title: Initial bundle has a budget and stays within it; routes are code-split

signals: every route's component eagerly imported into the entry bundle
no size budget on the initial JS chunk

terms.smell: Every route's code is eagerly imported into one bundle, and there is no budget on the initial JS, so it grows one dependency at a time.
terms.why: A 1.2MB initial bundle is ~1.2MB to download then parse + compile + execute on the main thread — 2-4s of CPU on a mid-range Android before the page is interactive, hurting LCP/INP.
terms.fix: Route-level code splitting (React.lazy/dynamic import, Angular loadChildren, Vue defineAsyncComponent) so the first route ships only its code; set and enforce a budget (e.g. initial JS <= 170KB gzipped).

example.before:
import Dashboard from './Dashboard';
import Editor from './Editor';   // shipped even when unused
example.after:
const Dashboard = lazy(() => import('./Dashboard'));
const Editor = lazy(() => import('./Editor'));

terms.title: Hit ratio justifies the cache; a low-hit cache is fixed or removed

signals: a cache added with no hit-ratio measurement
a cache on low-reuse / high-cardinality access, or on already-fast data

terms.smell: A cache is added on the hot read path without measuring its hit ratio, or on data that is cheap to compute, already fast, or low-reuse/high-cardinality.
terms.why: A cache below ~80-90% hit ratio on a hot read often is not paying for itself — it adds a round trip plus the real query, net slower, and carries an invalidation bug surface for no win.
terms.fix: Measure the hit ratio; if low, fix the key (§2) or drop the cache. Do not cache data that is cheap to compute or has a near-zero reuse pattern; do not cache to mask an unindexed query (fix the index instead).

terms.title: Writes invalidate or update the cache; the write pattern is deliberate

signals: a DB update with no corresponding cache.del / cache update
cached prices, permissions, or feature flags with no invalidation on write

terms.smell: Code writes the DB but does not invalidate or update the cached copy, and the cache-aside vs write-through choice is not deliberate.
terms.why: A price, permission, or feature flag changes in the DB but the cache serves the old value until the TTL lapses — customers check out at the old price, or a revoked permission still works. Intermittent (only on a hit) and high-stakes.
terms.fix: Pair every write with the invalidation it implies (write-then-del, or write-through update in the same step). For derived/multi-key caches, use tags/surrogate keys or a version prefix so one write clears all dependent entries.

example.before: await db.products.update(id, { price }); // cache still serves old price
example.after:
await db.products.update(id, { price });
await cache.del(`product:${id}`);

terms.title: Cache key carries nothing high-cardinality that kills the hit ratio

signals: a cache key includes a timestamp, request id, or JSON.stringify of all params
a key per request that almost never collides

terms.smell: A cache key embeds a high-cardinality dimension — a timestamp, a request id, the full param blob — so almost every key is unique and rarely collides.
terms.why: A 0.5% hit ratio pays all the cache machinery and complexity for almost no benefit, and can be slower than no cache (a round trip to a cold cache, then the real query anyway). Unbounded key cardinality also bloats cache memory and evicts useful entries.
terms.fix: Normalize the key and drop irrelevant dimensions so keys actually collide (search:normalize(query):page), keeping only what the output depends on.

example.before: cache.get(`search:${JSON.stringify(allParams)}:${timestamp}`) // unique every time
example.after: cache.get(`search:${normalize(query)}:${page}`)

terms.title: Cache key includes every input the output depends on

signals: a cache key omits a dimension the response varies by (currency, locale, user tier, auth)
the same key serves different users or different variants

terms.smell: A cache key omits a dimension the response depends on — currency, locale, user tier, auth scope — so one variant is served for another.
terms.why: A EUR user gets a USD-cached price; worse, a key missing the user dimension serves one user's data to another. A data-correctness/privacy bug that is intermittent (only on a hit) and brutal to debug.
terms.fix: Include every input the output varies by in the key (product:id:currency:locale:tier) — and only those. If the response is user-specific, the user must be in the key or it must not be shared-cached at all.

example.before: cache.get(`product:${id}`)  // varies by currency + locale + tier
example.after: cache.get(`product:${id}:${currency}:${locale}:${tier}`)

trips: cache.get(`price:${productId}`) // response also depends on currency

terms.title: Cache at the right layer for the data's scope (private vs public) and freshness

signals: Cache-Control: public on a user-specific response
volatile data cached at a CDN/shared layer with a long TTL

terms.smell: Mutable, user-specific data is cached at a shared/public layer (Cache-Control: public on /account/balance), or volatile data is cached far from the user with a long TTL.
terms.why: Caching per-user data at a public/CDN layer leaks one user's data to another — a correctness and security bug served at the edge. Caching volatile data with a long TTL serves stale data widely.
terms.fix: Pick the layer by who can share the data and how fresh it must be: private/no-store for user data, public+immutable only for hashed static assets, short TTL for volatile public data.

example.before: Cache-Control: public, max-age=3600    on GET /account/balance
example.after: Cache-Control: private, no-store        on GET /account/balance

terms.title: Every cache entry has a TTL (jittered) as a staleness and growth backstop

signals: cache.set(key, value) with no ttl argument
a cohort of keys set together with identical expiry (no jitter)

terms.smell: A cache entry is set with no TTL, or a cohort of keys is set with identical expiry so they all lapse in the same instant.
terms.why: No TTL means one missed invalidation leaves data stale forever, plus a cache that only grows until it OOMs or evicts hot keys. Un-jittered expiries make a cohort expire together and stampede the backend.
terms.fix: Always set a TTL as a backstop even under explicit invalidation, chosen by tolerance for staleness x recompute cost; add jitter so keys set together do not expire simultaneously.

example.before: cache.set(key, value); // no expiry
example.after: cache.set(key, value, { ttl: 300 + Math.random() * 30 }); // bounded + jittered

terms.title: Capacity is estimated (Little's Law), the first ceiling identified, and "holds 10x?" answered

signals: a change on a scaling-sensitive path with no capacity estimate
no answer to which resource (connections, memory, a hot key) is the first ceiling at 10x

terms.smell: A change on a scaling-sensitive path ships with no capacity estimate — no Little's Law arithmetic, no identified first ceiling, no answer to "does this hold 10x?"
terms.why: A feature that is fine at launch traffic silently becomes the bottleneck at 3x — discovered during the traffic spike you scaled up for, when it is hardest to fix. The estimate is cheap; the outage is not.
terms.fix: Estimate the resource at 10x with Little's Law (concurrency = arrival_rate x avg_latency -> connections, memory, workers), identify the first ceiling you will hit (DB connections, a hot key, memory, a downstream rate limit) — that ceiling is the real capacity — and load-test to the target.

terms.title: Global middleware is cheap — no per-request DB round trip or in-band blocking IO

signals: a DB lookup or synchronous audit/log write inside a global app.use middleware
expensive work in middleware that runs on every route including health checks

terms.smell: Global middleware does a DB round trip or synchronous IO (audit write, disk log) on every request — including routes and health checks that do not need it.
terms.why: A 20ms DB lookup in global auth middleware adds 20ms to every endpoint and DB load proportional to total traffic — a flat latency tax that is easy to miss because it is not in the handler.
terms.fix: Keep per-request middleware cheap (verify the JWT signature locally, no DB); load the user lazily only on routes that need it; push audit/logging to an async sink/queue; order cheap rejects (rate limit, auth) before expensive work.

example.before: app.use(async (req, res, next) => { req.user = await db.users.find(decode(req.token)); next(); });
example.after: app.use(verifyJwtSignatureOnly); // local crypto; load user lazily per route

terms.title: A CI perf gate and recorded baseline protect the paths that matter

signals: a perf-sensitive path has no bundlesize/Lighthouse/k6/benchstat gate
no baseline p50/p95/p99 or bundle size recorded to compare against

terms.smell: A hot path or shipped bundle has no automated gate (bundlesize/size-limit, Lighthouse CI, k6 threshold, benchstat) and no recorded baseline, so regressions land unnoticed.
terms.why: Without a gate, a year of "tiny" 5ms regressions compounds into a 200ms-slower endpoint (or a +250KB bundle) that nobody can bisect, because no single PR looked guilty.
terms.fix: Add a CI gate that fails the build when a budget/threshold is breached, and record a baseline first so future changes have something to compare against.

terms.title: No client-side request waterfalls — independent fetches parallelized, origins preconnected

signals: sequential awaits of independent client fetches before first paint
no preconnect/preload for critical late-discovered resources

terms.smell: Independent client fetches are awaited serially before first paint, and critical origins/ resources are not hinted early.
terms.why: Three serial 100ms fetches = 300ms of blank screen before content; the browser also cannot preconnect to an API/font origin until it parses the script that uses it.
terms.fix: Parallelize independent fetches (Promise.all), fetch on the server / in the route loader for above-the-fold data, preconnect/dns-prefetch required origins, and preload the critical late- discovered resource (LCP image, key font).

example.before:
const cfg = await fetch('/config').then(r => r.json());
const me  = await fetch('/me').then(r => r.json()); // independent, serial

terms.title: Heavy/optional features are dynamic-imported on demand, not in the initial chunk

signals: a heavy charting/editor/PDF/map library statically imported into the entry bundle
a 200KB library shipped for a feature a small fraction of users open

terms.smell: A heavy library for a rarely-used feature (chart, rich-text editor, PDF, map) is statically imported into the initial bundle.
terms.why: Shipping a 200KB library to everyone for a feature 5% reach taxes every visitor's download, parse, and execute time for code most never run.
terms.fix: Dynamic-import the library when the feature is actually used (await import(...) on click), so the other 95% never pay for it.

example.before: import { Chart } from 'heavy-charting-lib'; // 200KB for everyone
example.after: async function openChart() { const { Chart } = await import('heavy-charting-lib'); renderChart(Chart); }

terms.title: Fan-out is bounded against tail amplification — per-call timeouts, hedging, or partial results

signals: a request fans out to many backends and waits for all of them
a dashboard aggregates 20-30 downstream calls with no per-child timeout

terms.smell: A request fans out to many backends and waits for the slowest of all N, with no per-call timeout, no hedging, and no partial-result path.
terms.why: A dashboard that fans out to 30 services feels fine in testing (you rarely hit a tail) but is routinely slow in production because someone's child is always on its p99 — the parent's latency is the max of its children, not the average.
terms.fix: Add short per-child timeouts with a budget; hedge read-only idempotent calls (fire a second copy after a delay, take the first); reduce fan-out width; or return partial results when a non-critical child times out.

terms.title: File-descriptor and connection limits respected — resources closed on every path, outbound concurrency bounded

signals: open(path) / a socket / a connection not closed on the error path
unbounded outbound connections that can exhaust max_connections or ulimit

terms.smell: A file/socket/connection is opened without a guaranteed close on every path (leaked on the error branch), or outbound connections are unbounded.
terms.why: Leaked FDs hit the process ulimit -> EMFILE: too many open files and the service cannot accept connections while CPU/memory look fine; unpooled DB connections hit max_connections and reject requests. Both are limit exhaustion, not slowness — they cliff with no warning on a latency graph.
terms.fix: Use context managers / try-finally / defer close so resources close on every exit path; pool DB connections and bound outbound concurrency.

example.before: f = open(path)   # never closed on the error path
example.after: with open(path) as f: ...   # closed on every exit

terms.title: Responses project to the fields the client actually uses

signals: SELECT * or findMany() with no select, returning every column including big blobs
the response includes fields the client never reads

terms.smell: A query returns every column (SELECT * / no select clause), including large blobs the client never reads, then serializes all of it.
terms.why: Over-fetching columns inflates serialization CPU, network bytes, and memory on every request — and can pull big JSON/text blobs the caller discards.
terms.fix: Project to only the fields the client uses (select { ... } / a column list / GraphQL field selection).

terms.title: Fonts are subsetted, woff2, and limited to the weights used

signals: a webfont link requesting many weights (100,300,400,500,700,900)
unsubsetted, render-blocking fonts, or display:block

terms.smell: A font family is loaded with many weights, unsubsetted, and render-blocking (6 weights, display:block).
terms.why: A 6-weight unsubsetted font family is hundreds of KB of render-blocking download — pure budget cost that delays first paint.
terms.fix: Ship only the weights/styles used, woff2, subset to the glyphs needed, self-host or preconnect; each extra weight is another file. (font-display/swap mechanics -> frontend-review.)

terms.title: Non-critical dependencies are optional; the service degrades and sheds load instead of falling over

signals: Promise.all where a non-critical dependency (ads, recommendations) can fail the whole request
no fallback when an optional downstream times out

terms.smell: A request treats a non-critical dependency as all-or-nothing — one Promise.all where a slow/ failed ads or recommendations call fails the whole page — with no fallback and no load shedding.
terms.why: A single slow non-critical dependency takes down the whole page; without load shedding an overloaded service tries to serve everything slowly and fails all of it, instead of serving most requests well and rejecting the excess.
terms.fix: Make non-essential features optional (a timeout/failure returns a fallback, not an error); shed the cheapest-to-reject work early under overload (503 + Retry-After), serve stale cache, or disable expensive features to keep the core path alive.

example.before: const [feed, recs, ads] = await Promise.all([...]); // ads slow -> page hangs
example.after:
const feed = await getFeed();
const recs = await getRecs().catch(() => []); // optional, empty on failure

terms.title: No needless allocations, copies, or app-side work the DB should do in one query

signals: string concatenation or array building in a hot loop that is immediately discarded
filtering/joining/aggregating in app code that one SQL query could do

terms.smell: A hot loop allocates and discards (string concat, temp arrays, repeated copies), or app code filters/joins/aggregates data that a single DB query could return already shaped.
terms.why: Needless allocations show up as GC time in the flame graph — CPU and memory churn that caps throughput under load. Doing the DB's join in app code pulls far more rows over the wire and burns CPU the database would do faster.
terms.fix: Eliminate the per-iteration allocation (reuse buffers, build once); push filtering/joining/ aggregation into the query so the DB returns only the shaped result.

terms.title: HTTP caching headers are correct — immutable for hashed assets, ETag/304 for dynamic, private/no-store for user data

signals: a hashed static asset served with no Cache-Control (re-downloaded every visit)
a user-specific API response sent with public, max-age
no-cache used where no-store was meant, or vice versa

terms.smell: HTTP caching headers are wrong: a hashed asset with no Cache-Control, a user-specific response with public max-age, or no-cache confused with no-store.
terms.why: Re-downloading hashed assets that never change wastes bandwidth and slows loads; caching a user-specific API response publicly means one user sees another's data — a correctness/ security bug at the CDN.
terms.fix: immutable + content hash for static assets; ETag/If-None-Match -> 304 for dynamic-but- cacheable; private, no-store for user data. Remember no-cache means "revalidate," no-store means "do not store."

example.before: GET /api/me  ->  Cache-Control: public, max-age=600
example.after: GET /api/me  ->  Cache-Control: private, no-store

terms.title: Hydration/framework runtime cost is minimized for SSR apps

signals: an SSR page that hydrates its entire JS up front
large client bundle attached to server-rendered HTML

terms.smell: An SSR/isomorphic page ships and hydrates its entire JS up front, so the page looks ready but is not interactive until hydration finishes.
terms.why: A heavily-hydrated page is visually complete but unresponsive to clicks for seconds (the "looks loaded, feels broken" gap) — high TBT/INP.
terms.fix: Reduce what hydrates: islands / partial hydration (Astro, Qwik resumability), next/dynamic with ssr:false for client-only widgets, React Server Components to keep non-interactive parts off the client bundle. (Render internals -> frontend-review.)

terms.title: Images are modern-format, responsive, and compressed within a byte budget

signals: a full-resolution image shipped into a small slot with no srcset
JPEG/PNG originals with no AVIF/WebP, no responsive sizes

terms.smell: A large original image is shipped into a small display slot with no modern format and no responsive srcset (hero-4000px.jpg into a 400px box).
terms.why: Oversized/uncompressed images are usually the #1 LCP and bandwidth cost — megabytes of download for pixels that are thrown away.
terms.fix: AVIF/WebP with fallback, responsive srcset/sizes, right-sized and compressed; set an image- weight budget per page.

terms.title: Load/soak-test evidence is attached for scaling-sensitive changes, not just "works in dev"

signals: a scaling-sensitive change merged with only 'works in dev' as evidence
a long-running/stateful change with no soak test for memory/FD growth

terms.smell: A change on a hot or scaling-sensitive path is merged with "it works in dev" and no load/soak test at expected (or 10x) traffic.
terms.why: Merging a "should scale fine" change with no load evidence is how the capacity bug ships — and the first real load test becomes the production traffic spike.
terms.fix: Attach a load test at expected and ideally 10x traffic (open-model, reporting p95/p99 and error rate), a soak test for anything long-running (rule out memory/FD growth), and the identified first ceiling with headroom to it.

terms.title: Load tests use an open model / constant arrival rate (no coordinated omission)

signals: endpoint capacity claimed from a closed-loop load tool that waits for each response
a load test reports a suspiciously good p99

terms.smell: Capacity is validated with a closed-loop tool that waits for each response before sending the next, so a stall during the test records fewer samples instead of more.
terms.why: Coordinated omission makes the load test report p99=120ms when the real p99 is 4s, because the tool stopped sending traffic exactly when the server got slow. You ship "validated" capacity that does not exist.
terms.fix: Use an open-model / constant-arrival-rate load (k6 constant-arrival-rate, wrk2, vegeta) with pass/fail thresholds on p95/p99 so a stall still counts the requests that would have arrived.

terms.title: Every optimization or regression claim is backed by a number

signals: PR claims a change makes something faster with no benchmark or before/after
a perf finding asserts a regression without a profile or p95 number

terms.smell: An optimization is merged, or a regression is flagged, with no profile, benchmark, or before/after p95 — just an assertion that it is faster or slower.
terms.why: An optimization with no number is a guess that adds complexity for an unproven gain — a defect in itself. A blocker with no number blocks on a hunch. Both waste effort.
terms.fix: Attach the evidence: a profile, a micro/macro benchmark, or the before/after p95. If the cost is a guess, label the finding pending-a-number rather than blocking.

terms.title: In-process memoization is bounded (LRU/size/TTL) and correctly scoped

signals: a module-level Map memo keyed by user id / request body with no eviction
per-request data stored on a long-lived singleton

terms.smell: An in-process memo is unbounded (a module-level Map keyed by user input with no eviction) or wrongly scoped (per-request data stored on a long-lived singleton).
terms.why: An unbounded memo keyed by something high-cardinality grows until the process OOMs after hours/days. A memo on a singleton holding per-request data leaks request state across users — a correctness and memory bug at once.
terms.fix: Bound it (LRU with max size and/or TTL) and scope it correctly — never store per-request data on a singleton.

example.before:
const memo = new Map();
if (!memo.has(userId)) memo.set(userId, expensive(data)); // grows forever
example.after: const memo = new LRUCache({ max: 10_000, ttl: 60_000 });

terms.title: Memory is stable under sustained load — listeners/timers/connections cleaned up, soak-tested

signals: event listeners/timers added per request and never removed
growing closures or never-closed connections/streams; no soak test

terms.smell: Something accumulates with request volume and is never cleaned up — listeners/timers added per request, growing closures, never-closed connections/streams — and no soak test covers it.
terms.why: A leak that adds 2MB per 1,000 requests looks fine in a 5-minute test and OOMs the pod every 6 hours in production — a restart loop that pages someone, with no single request to blame.
terms.fix: Remove listeners, clear timers, and close resources on request end (req.on('close', ...)); bound caches/memos; confirm stability with a soak/endurance test (steady traffic for hours, watch RSS) — a short test will not catch it.

example.before: emitter.on('data', h); listeners.push(h); // added per request, never removed
example.after: emitter.on('data', h); req.on('close', () => emitter.off('data', h));

terms.title: Micro-benchmarks handle warmup, dead-code elimination, and realistic n

signals: a hand-rolled timer loop times a function to prove it is faster
a benchmark uses tiny n or discards its result

terms.smell: A hand-rolled micro-benchmark times a function with no warmup, an unused result, or n far smaller than production — so the number is measuring cold JIT, eliminated dead code, or a size that hides the O(n^2).
terms.why: A micro-benchmark that "proves" 5x faster but measured cold-vs-warm JIT or deleted work ships an optimization that adds complexity and helps nothing — and can be slower in the real macro context (cache effects, real data sizes).
terms.fix: Use a real harness that warms up, defeats DCE (blackhole/KeepAlive), and reports variance (tinybench/vitest bench, testing.B, pytest-benchmark, JMH, criterion). Prefer macro (load-test) evidence for endpoint claims.

example.after:
import { bench } from 'vitest';
bench('dedupe with Set', () => { blackhole(dedupe(input)); });

terms.title: Negative results are cached with a short TTL where misses are common or abusable

signals: cache only stores hits; a lookup for a missing id always hits the DB
a lookup for non-existent keys re-queries the backend every time

terms.smell: The cache stores only found results, so every lookup for a missing/invalid key re-hits the backend every time.
terms.why: A flood of requests for non-existent keys (a buggy client, or a deliberate cache-penetration attack) bypasses the cache entirely and hammers the DB — every one is a guaranteed miss.
terms.fix: Cache the "not found" too, with a short TTL (a null sentinel) so a later-created record still appears soon while absorbing the miss flood.

example.before: const u = await cache.get(`user:${id}`) ?? await db.users.find(id); // miss re-queries every time
example.after:
if (u === undefined) { u = await db.users.find(id);
  await cache.set(`user:${id}`, u ?? NULL_SENTINEL, { ttl: u ? 300 : 30 }); }

terms.title: Nothing strongly-consistent (balances, inventory, auth) is cached

signals: an account balance, sellable inventory count, or auth decision read from a cache
a value that must be exact served from a TTL cache

terms.smell: A value that must be strongly consistent — an account balance, inventory you sell against, an authorization decision — is served from a TTL cache.
terms.why: A stale read here is a correctness bug: overselling inventory, showing a wrong balance, or honoring a revoked permission. Cache invalidation cannot guarantee freshness the way these values require.
terms.fix: Read strongly-consistent values from the source of truth, not a cache. Cache only what tolerates bounded staleness.

terms.title: Outbound HTTP clients reuse connections with keep-alive

signals: a new HTTP client created per call, or requests.get() without a Session
no connection reuse on an outbound client on the hot path

terms.smell: An outbound HTTP client opens a fresh TCP+TLS connection per call — a new client per request, or requests.get() instead of a reused Session — with no keep-alive.
terms.why: A handshake (often 1-3 RTTs, tens of ms with TLS) is paid on every downstream call instead of reused, adding fixed latency to each request.
terms.fix: Share a persistent client/agent/session with keep-alive across calls (requests.Session, httpx client, Go's default http.Client with a reused transport).

terms.title: No over-fetching — request only the fields and rows the client shows

signals: fetch('/api/items') pulling 10k items / every field when 50 are shown
no pagination or field selection on a client data fetch

terms.smell: The client fetches far more than it renders — all rows, every field — when only a page is shown.
terms.why: Fetching 10k rows and every field when 50 are visible is megabytes of transfer plus serialization and client memory for data the user never sees.
terms.fix: Request only the fields and rows shown (GraphQL field selection, ?fields=, ?limit=&cursor=), paginate, and load more on demand.

example.before: const all = await fetch('/api/items').then(r => r.json()); // 10k items, every field
example.after: const page = await fetch('/api/items?limit=50&cursor=' + cursor).then(r => r.json());

terms.title: No over-rendering — long lists are virtualized, not mounted whole

signals: mapping 10k items to DOM nodes with no windowing
a long list rendered in full

terms.smell: A long list is mounted in full — thousands of DOM nodes rendered when only a screenful is visible.
terms.why: Ten thousand mounted nodes make a huge DOM that is slow to render, scroll, and update — memory and interaction latency both suffer.
terms.fix: Virtualize (windowing) so only the visible rows are in the DOM. (The INP/render mechanics are frontend-review's; the budget rule — do not ship 10k rows — is here.)

terms.title: Invariant or per-user-stable work is precomputed, memoized, or cached — not redone per request

signals: a config/template/rate-table is loaded, parsed, or compiled inside the request handler
the same value is recomputed on every request though it changes hourly at most

terms.smell: Work whose result changes at most hourly — loading and parsing a rate table, compiling a template, aggregating stable data — runs on every request inside the handler.
terms.why: Every request pays for parsing/compiling/aggregating that only changes hourly — pure waste that scales linearly with traffic.
terms.fix: Hoist invariant work to module load or startup (refresh on TTL/signal), memoize it, or cache it with a TTL. For per-user-but-stable derived data, cache keyed by user. Cache serialized bytes if the input is stable.

example.after:
const rates = loadAndParseRateTableFromDisk();   // once at startup
app.get('/pricing', (req, res) => res.json(price(req.query, rates)));

terms.title: No cold path optimized at the cost of complexity; optimize where the time is

signals: a once-a-week admin job or cold path is hand-optimized with added complexity
clever micro-optimization on code that is not on the hot path

terms.smell: A cold path (a once-a-day job, an admin route, code that runs rarely) is hand-optimized with added complexity and no measurement showing it is hot.
terms.why: A 10x speedup of code that runs once a week is noise; the added complexity is real cost with no user benefit. Premature optimization of a cold path is itself a defect.
terms.fix: Spend the complexity budget where the time is — the per-request hot path — and leave cold code simple. Measure before optimizing anything.

terms.title: Hot path identified from a flame graph, not from reading the code

signals: the PR optimizes a function it assumes is slow, with no profile
a perf fix targets the code that looks expensive rather than the measured widest frame

terms.smell: An optimization targets the function that looks expensive by eye, with no flame graph showing it is the widest frame on the hot path.
terms.why: Teams routinely spend days optimizing the function they suspect, ship it, and see no movement — because the real cost was a different frame (often JSON serialization, a lock, or GC). The flame graph would have pointed straight at it.
terms.fix: Profile first (clinic/py-spy/pprof/async-profiler/DevTools), find the widest frame on the hot path, optimize that, and re-profile to confirm the time actually moved. Use a heap profiler for leaks — a CPU flame graph will not show one.

terms.title: No accidental O(n^2) on the hot path

signals: .includes / .find / `in` / indexOf called inside a loop over the same collection
nested loops over the same collection, or a re-sort inside a loop

terms.smell: A linear scan (.includes/.find/indexOf/`in`) runs inside a loop, or two nested loops range over the same collection, or a collection is re-sorted every iteration — O(n^2) hiding on a hot path.
terms.why: Fine at 100 items in dev, 8s and climbing at 10k in prod. The cost scales with data, so it passes review on fixtures and degrades silently as the table grows.
terms.fix: Hoist the lookup structure out of the loop: build a Set/Map once and look up in O(1); sort once; or restructure to a single pass.

example.before: const out = results.filter(r => excludedIds.includes(r.id)); // O(n*m)
example.after:
const excluded = new Set(excludedIds);
const out = results.filter(r => !excluded.has(r.id));         // O(n)

trips: for (const r of rows) { if (seen.includes(r.id)) continue; ... }
clean: const seen = new Set(ids); for (const r of rows) { if (seen.has(r.id)) continue; }

terms.title: Queue depth is bounded and monitored, consumers scale to peak, and a DLQ exists

signals: a fixed worker count that cannot keep up with peak arrival rate
no queue-depth monitoring or dead-letter queue for poison messages

terms.smell: Producers can outpace consumers indefinitely — a fixed worker count below peak arrival rate, no depth/oldest-message monitoring, and no dead-letter queue.
terms.why: A queue whose depth grows unbounded means every job waits longer, the backlog never drains, and eventually the broker hits its limit or memory fills. By the time users notice "my job never ran," the backlog is hours deep. A poison message with no DLQ wedges the pipeline.
terms.fix: Size/autoscale consumers to the peak arrival rate (not the average), monitor queue depth and age of the oldest message and alert when they trend up, and add a DLQ for poison messages.

terms.title: Text responses are compressed (gzip/brotli)

signals: a large JSON/text response sent with no gzip/br compression
no compression middleware at the app or edge

terms.smell: A text/JSON response over ~1KB is sent uncompressed — no gzip/brotli at the app or edge.
terms.why: JSON compresses 5-10x; an uncompressed 2MB list vs a 60KB compressed one is seconds of transfer on a 3G/mobile connection for every request.
terms.fix: Enable gzip or Brotli (better ratio for text) at the app or edge for responses over ~1KB. Do not compress already-compressed bytes like images.

terms.title: Confirm a suspected regression against field/RUM, not just one lab run

signals: a regression is asserted or dismissed from a single Lighthouse/laptop run
optimizing for a lab score while field metrics have not moved

terms.smell: A regression is confirmed or dismissed from a single synthetic lab run (Lighthouse, a k6 pass, a laptop) that cannot reproduce real traffic mix, devices, networks, or data sizes.
terms.why: A change can look neutral in the lab and regress badly in the field (or vice versa). Chasing a lab score while field p75 does not move — or dismissing a real field regression because "Lighthouse looks fine on my machine" — optimizes the proxy, not the truth.
terms.fix: Confirm against field/RUM where available: web-vitals / CrUX field p75 for frontend, APM histograms (Datadog/Prometheus/OTel) production p95/p99 by endpoint for backend.

terms.title: Independent awaits run in parallel, not sequentially

signals: consecutive awaits where a later one does not reference an earlier one's result
several independent IO calls awaited one at a time on a page-load endpoint

terms.smell: Consecutive awaits where a later call does not depend on an earlier one's result, so independent IO is paid one-at-a-time — the sum of the latencies instead of the max.
terms.why: A page-load endpoint takes 255ms instead of 90ms — 165ms of pure latency reclaimed by deleting two await keywords. Multiplied across every such endpoint it is a sitewide slowdown.
terms.fix: Fire independent calls together with Promise.all (JS), errgroup (Go), asyncio.gather (Py), or CompletableFuture.allOf (Java). Keep sequential only for a true data dependency; to limit concurrency, cap it rather than serialize.

terms.title: No needless deep-clone or re-serialize of large objects on the hot path; stream large outputs

signals: JSON.parse(JSON.stringify(x)) used to clone a large object per request
a large response built as one giant in-memory string instead of streamed

terms.smell: A large object is deep-cloned or re-serialized per request (JSON.parse(JSON.stringify(x)) as a clone, re-marshaling a stable protobuf), or a big response is buffered into one string.
terms.why: Serialization and cloning frequently turn out to be the widest frame in a backend flame graph — CPU and allocation that nobody suspected, capping throughput; buffering a big payload holds it all in memory and delays TTFB.
terms.fix: Do not clone read-only data; if you must, use structured cloning. Cache serialized bytes when the input is stable. Stream large outputs (NDJSON/chunked) so bytes flow sooner.

example.before:
const safe = JSON.parse(JSON.stringify(bigConfig)); // full serialize+parse to clone
return template.render(safe);
example.after: return template.render(bigConfig); // read-only, no clone

terms.title: No single point of contention (global lock, hot row/key) serializing the whole service

signals: a global mutex taken on every request
every request incrementing the same DB row / counter / sequence

terms.smell: Every request serializes through one shared thing — a global mutex, a single hot row/key, a "current id" sequence — on the request path.
terms.why: A global lock or single hot key serializes the whole service through one point: you add instances and throughput does not improve because every request still queues on that lock/row.
terms.fix: Eliminate the shared point — shard the hot key into N and sum, buffer+batch updates, use atomic ops (atomic.AddInt64) or lock-free structures, or partition the key.

example.before: globalMu.Lock(); counter++; globalMu.Unlock() // every request serializes here
example.after: atomic.AddInt64(&counter, 1) // lock-free

terms.title: Stateless — no in-memory session/counter/rate-limit/local-disk state that breaks behind a load balancer

signals: an in-memory session store, counter, or rate limiter on a scalable service
uploads written to local disk / an in-process cron scheduler

terms.smell: Per-process state assumes a single instance — an in-memory session store, a local counter or rate limiter, local-disk uploads, or an in-process job scheduler.
terms.why: It works on one box, then you scale to three behind a load balancer and users randomly lose their session, counters are wrong, uploads vanish (saved to another pod's disk), and the cron runs twice. The bug only appears after you scale — the worst time to discover it.
terms.fix: Externalize shared state: sessions/rate-limits/counters -> Redis; uploads -> object storage; cache -> shared; use a distributed scheduler/leader election for cron.

example.before:
const sessions = new Map();      // in-memory session store
let requestCounter = 0;          // per-instance counter
example.after: const session = await redis.get(`sess:${id}`);

terms.title: Judge latency on p95/p99, not the average, and account for fan-out amplification

signals: a perf claim reports only the mean/average response time
a request fans out to many backends and is sized by average child latency

terms.smell: Performance is reported or gated on the average, hiding a right-skewed tail; or a fan-out is sized by the average child latency instead of the max.
terms.why: A change that improves the mean 45ms->40ms while pushing p99 300ms->850ms is a regression for the users on the tail — and the mean called it a win. Under fan-out to N children the parent waits for the slowest, so p99 amplifies: 20 calls each 1%-slow => ~18% of pages slow.
terms.fix: Report the distribution (p50/p95/p99/p99.9/max) and gate on the tail. For fan-out, size by the slowest child and add per-call timeouts, hedged reads, or partial-result degradation.

terms.title: Third-party scripts are deferred/lazy-loaded and budgeted

signals: a render-blocking analytics/chat/tag-manager script tag with no defer
a heavy third-party embed loaded eagerly on every page

terms.smell: A third-party script (analytics, chat, ads, tag manager) is loaded render-blocking or eagerly, with no defer/lazy and no budget.
terms.why: A single chat or tag-manager script can add hundreds of KB and a long main-thread task -> worse INP and a delayed interactive page — easy to miss because it is not in your bundle.
terms.fix: Defer scripts, lazy-load on interaction/idle (requestIdleCallback), audit their real cost (WebPageTest "block 3rd party"), and set a budget for them.

example.before: <script src="https://tag-manager.example/loader.js"></script>
example.after: <script src="https://analytics.example/a.js" defer></script>

terms.title: No whole-library or barrel-file imports defeating tree-shaking

signals: import _ from 'lodash' (whole library for one function)
importing from a barrel index that re-exports a whole directory

terms.smell: A whole library is imported for one function (import _ from 'lodash'), or a barrel index that re-exports a directory is imported, pulling the whole module graph.
terms.why: import _ from 'lodash' adds ~70KB for a one-line helper; a barrel import can drag dozens of unused components into the chunk — bytes the user downloads and parses for nothing.
terms.fix: Import the exact leaf (import debounce from 'lodash/debounce'; import { Button } from '@/components/Button'); ensure deps are ESM and side-effect-free ("sideEffects": false) so the bundler drops unused exports.

example.before:
import _ from 'lodash';
import { Button } from '@/components'; // barrel
example.after:
import debounce from 'lodash/debounce';
import { Button } from '@/components/Button';

terms.title: No unbounded in-memory growth — lists/maps/caches are bounded or evicted

signals: a module-level Map/array that only ever grows, keyed by user id or request data
a cache/memo with no max size and no eviction

terms.smell: A list, map, or cache accumulates entries and never evicts — an ever-growing structure keyed by something high-cardinality (user id, request body).
terms.why: It looks fine in a 5-minute test and OOMs the pod after hours/days of uptime — a slow leak that reads like a cache, with no single request to blame and a restart loop that pages someone.
